title: BatchNorm Breaks in Distributed Training
scenario: Your team is scaling a ResNet-50 training job from 1 GPU to 64 GPUs using data parallelism. After scaling, validation accuracy drops by 2.3% despite identical hyperparameters. The only difference: the effective local batch size per GPU dropped from 256 to 4 (global batch 256 preserved). A senior engineer suspects BatchNorm. Explain the root cause and two solutions.
common_mistake: Blaming the optimizer or learning rate schedule for the accuracy drop. The root cause is that BatchNorm statistics computed over a local batch of 4 samples are extremely noisy, degrading normalization quality.
realistic_solution: Root cause: BatchNorm computes mean and variance over the local batch dimension. With batch size 4 per GPU, these statistics have high variance (standard error of mean ~ 1/sqrt(4) = 0.5), causing noisy normalization during training and a mismatch with the running statistics accumulated for inference. Solutions: (1) SyncBatchNorm: compute mean/variance across all 64 GPUs before normalizing. This restores the effective batch size to 256 for statistics, but adds an AllReduce per BatchNorm layer per forward pass. Cost: 2 AllReduces (mean and variance) per BN layer. For ResNet-50 with 53 BN layers:
napkin_math: BN noise: std_error = sigma / sqrt(B_local). B_local=256: error ~ sigma/16. B_local=4: error ~ sigma/2 (8x noisier). SyncBN cost: 53 BN layers * 2 AllReduces * 2 (fwd+bwd) = 212 AllReduces per step. At 10us per AllReduce on NVLink: ~2ms overhead per step.

title: Designing an Autograd Engine From Scratch
scenario: You are designing a minimal autograd engine for a new ML framework. The engine must support: (a) arbitrary DAG-structured computational graphs, (b) gradient accumulation when a tensor is used multiple times, and (c) memory-efficient backward pass. Describe the data structures and algorithm, and explain why topological sort is essential for correctness.
common_mistake: Implementing backward as a naive recursive DFS from the loss node, which can compute gradients for shared nodes multiple times (exponential blowup) or miss accumulated gradients from multiple consumers.
realistic_solution: Data structures: Each tensor node stores: (1) its value, (2) a pointer to its creator operation (grad_fn), (3) an accumulated gradient buffer (initially None). Each operation node stores: (1) the backward function, (2) pointers to input tensor nodes, (3) saved tensors for backward. Algorithm: (1) Topological sort of the graph from loss to inputs (reverse BFS/DFS with visit counting). (2) Process nodes in reverse topological order. For each operation, call its backward function with the output gradient, then ADD (not replace) the resulting input gradients to each input tensor's gradient buffer.
napkin_math: Naive recursive: O(2^depth) for diamond-shaped graphs. Topological sort: O(nodes + edges), processes each node exactly once. For a 100-layer transformer with residual connections: ~400 nodes, ~600 edges. Topo sort: 1000 operations. Naive recursion: potentially 2^100 visits (intractable).

title: Designing Around Non-Differentiable Operations
scenario: You are building an ML system that must include discrete decision steps (e.g., hard token selection in a mixture-of-experts router, or discrete action selection in a reinforcement learning policy). These operations have zero gradients almost everywhere, breaking standard backpropagation. Design an approach that enables end-to-end training, and analyze the systems cost of your solution.
common_mistake: Suggesting to simply 'make everything continuous' without understanding the fundamental tension: discrete decisions are needed for routing efficiency (MoE) or combinatorial optimization, but break the differentiable computational graph.
realistic_solution: Three approaches with increasing systems complexity: (1) Straight-Through Estimator (STE): use discrete forward, continuous backward (pass gradients through argmax as if it were identity). Cost: zero additional compute. Limitation: biased gradient estimates, can cause training instability. (2) Gumbel-Softmax relaxation: replace discrete sampling with a continuous approximation using Gumbel noise + temperature-controlled softmax. Forward: sample from Gumbel-Softmax (differentiable). Cost: one extra softmax per discrete decision + Gumbel noise generation. Memory: stores the full probability dist
napkin_math: STE: 0 extra FLOPs, biased gradients. Gumbel-Softmax: +E multiplies per discrete step, E=64 experts: +131K FLOPs per token (negligible vs attention). REINFORCE: 2-5x forward passes for variance reduction. Auxiliary loss approach: 1 extra softmax + cross-entropy per layer, <0.01% overhead.

title: Optimal Recompute-vs-Save Scheduling
scenario: You are building a memory optimizer for a training framework. Given a computational graph with N operations, each with known compute cost c_i and activation memory size m_i, you must decide which activations to save and which to recompute during the backward pass, minimizing peak memory while staying within a compute budget of at most 33% overhead. Describe the problem formulation and a practical algorithm.
common_mistake: Treating this as a simple 'checkpoint every sqrt(N) layers' heuristic. While sqrt(N) is a good approximation for uniform chains, real computational graphs are DAGs with heterogeneous operation costs and branching.
realistic_solution: Problem formulation: This is an instance of the time-memory tradeoff on DAGs, which is NP-hard in general but admits good heuristics. For a chain of N operations: the optimal solution (Griewank & Walther, 2000) uses binomial checkpointing with O(N/log(N)) memory and O(N*log(N)) compute. For DAGs (real transformers): (1) Model: binary decision variable s_i in {0,1} for each operation (save=1, recompute=0). Objective: minimize max_{t} sum of alive activations at each backward step t. Constraint: total recompute FLOPs <= 0.33 * forward FLOPs. (2) Practical algorithm (used by PyTorch's activation
napkin_math: Uniform chain sqrt(N) heuristic: memory O(sqrt(N)), compute overhead 1/3. 96-layer transformer: sqrt(96) ~ 10 checkpoints, memory drops from 96 units to ~20 units (10 checkpoints + up to 10 recomputed). Optimal binomial: memory O(N/log(N)) = 96/6.5 ~ 15 units, but requires recursive recomputation scheduling.

title: The Feature Store Latency Budget
scenario: A recommendation model requires 200 user features and 50 item features at serving time. Each feature lookup from the online feature store takes ~0.5ms. The end-to-end SLA is 50ms and the model inference itself takes 15ms. Can you serve this model within SLA using sequential feature lookups?
common_mistake: Assuming feature lookups are instantaneous. With 250 sequential lookups at 0.5ms each, feature retrieval alone consumes 125ms — 2.5× the entire SLA budget.
realistic_solution: No. Sequential lookups take $250 \times 0.5\text{ms} = 125\text{ms}$, which exceeds the 50ms SLA before inference even starts. You must batch feature lookups into a single multi-get request or use parallel key lookups. A single batched Redis MGET for 250 keys takes ~2-5ms, leaving ~30ms headroom for inference and network overhead.
napkin_math: Sequential: $250 \times 0.5\text{ms} = 125\text{ms}$ (exceeds 50ms SLA). Batched MGET: ~3ms for 250 keys. Total with inference: $3 + 15 = 18\text{ms}$ — well within SLA.

title: The Embedding Table Memory Wall
scenario: A DLRM-style recommendation model has 100 embedding tables, each with 10 million rows and 128-dimensional FP32 embeddings. How much memory do the embedding tables require, and can they fit on a single A100 (80GB HBM)?
common_mistake: Forgetting to multiply by all three dimensions (tables × rows × dimensions × bytes). A common error is computing only one table's size.
realistic_solution: Each table: $10\text{M} \times 128 \times 4\text{B} = 5.12\text{ GB}$. Total: $100 \times 5.12\text{ GB} = 512\text{ GB}$. This is 6.4× the capacity of a single 80GB A100. The embeddings must be sharded across at least 7 GPUs, or use mixed-dimensional embeddings to reduce less important tables.
napkin_math: $100 \times 10^7 \times 128 \times 4\text{B} = 512\text{ GB}$. Single A100 = 80GB. Need $\lceil 512/80 \rceil = 7$ GPUs minimum.

title: The Training-Serving Skew Trap
scenario: During training, your feature pipeline computes 'user_avg_session_duration' using a Spark batch job that runs nightly. At serving time, the same feature is computed in real time from the last 24 hours of clickstream data. After deployment, the model's conversion rate drops 8%. What is the most likely cause?
common_mistake: Blaming the model architecture or hyperparameters. The model hasn't changed — the features it sees at serving time are subtly different from what it saw during training.
realistic_solution: Training-serving skew. The batch Spark job and the real-time computation use different code paths, time windows, aggregation logic, or null-handling. For example, the batch job might compute the average over all sessions in a calendar day (midnight to midnight), while the real-time path computes a rolling 24-hour window. Edge cases — timezone handling, session boundary definitions, null imputation — create a systematic bias between training and serving feature values. The model learned patterns on batch-computed features and cannot transfer to the different distribution at serving time.
napkin_math: If the average feature value shifts by even 10-15% between training and serving distributions, a model relying heavily on that feature can see 5-10% metric degradation. With 200+ features, even small per-feature skews compound.

title: The Batch vs Real-Time Feature Tradeoff
scenario: You are building a fraud detection system. The feature 'transaction_count_last_1_hour' is critical for catching velocity attacks. Your current architecture computes all features via a nightly batch pipeline that writes to a feature store. Why is this architecture fundamentally inadequate for this feature, and what is the minimum viable real-time path?
common_mistake: Thinking you can just run the batch pipeline more frequently (e.g., every 5 minutes). Even 5-minute staleness means a fraudster can execute dozens of transactions before the feature updates.
realistic_solution: A nightly batch pipeline produces features that are 0-24 hours stale. For a 1-hour window feature, the batch value is always wrong — it reflects yesterday's transactions, not the last hour's. The minimum viable real-time path: (1) Publish transaction events to a stream (Kafka/Kinesis), (2) A stream processor (Flink/Spark Streaming) maintains a sliding 1-hour window counter per user, (3) On each transaction, the processor updates the count and writes to a low-latency online store (Redis/DynamoDB), (4) At inference time, the model reads the up-to-date count. This changes feature freshness from h
napkin_math: Batch staleness: 0-24h average = 12h stale. Stream processing latency: event → Kafka → Flink → Redis ≈ 200-500ms. For a fraudster doing 50 transactions in 10 minutes, the batch pipeline detects 0 of 50 (stale features); the streaming pipeline detects by transaction ~3 (sub-second updates).

title: The Feature Backfill Cost
scenario: You add a new feature 'user_lifetime_spend_percentile' to your ranking model. Training needs 6 months of historical feature values, but this feature didn't exist 6 months ago. The raw transaction logs exist in your data lake. Estimate the compute cost of backfilling this feature for 100 million users over 180 days of logs, assuming 1 billion transactions per day stored in Parquet on S3.
common_mistake: Assuming backfill is a simple SQL query. At 180B rows across 180 days, the I/O and compute costs are substantial, and the aggregation must be computed as a point-in-time snapshot for each training example to avoid label leakage.
realistic_solution: Each day requires scanning that day's transactions (1B rows × ~200 bytes/row = 200 GB/day). Over 180 days: $180 \times 200\text{ GB} = 36\text{ TB}$ total scan. But it's worse: for point-in-time correctness, each training example needs the feature computed as-of its timestamp, requiring a cumulative running aggregation. Using Spark on a 100-node cluster: ~36TB scan at ~200 MB/s/node effective throughput = 30 minutes per day's partition. With 180 partitions and smart parallelism: ~3-4 hours. S3 scan cost: $36\text{ TB} \times \$5/\text{TB scanned} = \$180$. Compute cost: 100 nodes × 4 hours × ~
napkin_math: Data volume: $180 \times 10^9 \times 200\text{B} = 36\text{ TB}$. Scan cost: ~$180. Compute: ~$1,200. Engineering time: 2-4 weeks at $100/hr = $8K-16K. Total cost is dominated by engineering, not compute.

title: The Embedding Lookup Bottleneck
scenario: Your DLRM recommendation model spends 80% of inference time on embedding table lookups but only 5% of total FLOPs. The GPU compute utilization is 12%. A junior engineer suggests upgrading to a GPU with 2× more TFLOPS. Will this help?
common_mistake: Throwing more compute at a memory-bandwidth-bound problem. If 80% of time is embedding lookups (random memory access), 2× compute helps only the 20% that is compute.
realistic_solution: No. Embedding lookups are memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound. Each lookup is a sparse gather: read a 128-dim × 4-byte row = 512 bytes from a random location in HBM. With millions of lookups per batch, the access pattern is essentially random, defeating HBM's sequential bandwidth advantage. A GPU with 2× TFLOPS but the same HBM bandwidth will see at most a 1.25× speedup (Amdahl's Law: $1/(0.8 + 0.2/2) = 1.11\times$). The real fixes: (1) reduce embedding dimension, (2) use mixed-dimension embeddings (important features get 128-dim, unimportant get 16-dim), (3) use quantized INT8 embedding
napkin_math: Amdahl's Law: speedup = $1/(0.8 + 0.2/2) = 1/(0.9) = 1.11\times$. Upgrading 2× compute yields only 11% improvement. Switching from FP32 to INT8 embeddings: 4× less memory per lookup, effective 4× bandwidth for the 80% bottleneck: $1/(0.8/4 + 0.2) = 1/(0.4) = 2.5\times$ speedup.

title: The Feature Interaction Explosion
scenario: Your ads ranking model uses 500 sparse categorical features (user demographics, ad categories, device types, etc.). A data scientist proposes adding all pairwise feature crosses to capture interactions. How many new features does this create, and what is the systems impact?
common_mistake: Thinking 'pairwise crosses of 500 features is just 250,000 new features — manageable.' The real explosion is in the vocabulary size of each crossed feature, not just the count of crosses.
realistic_solution: Number of pairwise crosses: $\binom{500}{2} = 124,750$ new crossed features. But the real problem is vocabulary explosion: if feature A has 1,000 unique values and feature B has 5,000 unique values, their cross has up to 5,000,000 unique values — each needing an embedding row. With 500 features averaging 10K vocabulary each, the total cross vocabulary can reach billions. This creates: (1) Memory: billions of embedding rows × 128 dims × 4 bytes = terabytes of parameters, (2) Sparsity: most crosses are seen < 5 times in training data — insufficient to learn meaningful embeddings, (3) Serving lat
napkin_math: $\binom{500}{2} = 124,750$ crosses. Average cross vocabulary: $10\text{K} \times 10\text{K} = 100\text{M}$ (assuming independence). Even at 10% fill rate: 10M rows × 128 dims × 4B = 5 GB per cross. Total: impractical. DCN-v2 models all interactions with a single $d \times d$ weight matrix: $1024^2 \times 4\text{B} = 4\text{ MB}$.

title: The Point-in-Time Join Correctness
scenario: Your model training pipeline joins the label table (user clicked ad at time T) with the feature table (user features computed daily at midnight). A newly trained model shows 2% higher offline AUC than the production model, but after deployment, online metrics show no improvement. The data scientist is confused: 'The offline eval used a held-out test set — it should be unbiased.' What went wrong?
common_mistake: Assuming that a held-out test set prevents all forms of data leakage. Temporal leakage through feature joins is invisible to random train/test splits.
realistic_solution: The feature join uses 'as-of-now' features, not 'as-of-event-time' features. When you join user features at time $T_{now}$ with a label event from time $T_{event}$ (where $T_{event} < T_{now}$), the features contain information from after the event — future leakage. For example, if a user's 'total_purchases' was 5 at event time but is 12 now, the model trains on leaked future information. This inflates offline metrics because both train and test suffer the same leakage. Online, the model sees only current features with no future information, so the inflated performance vanishes. The fix: point
napkin_math: If 30% of features change meaningfully between event time and join time (avg 2 months later), and each leaks ~0.5% signal, the cumulative leakage inflates AUC by $0.3 \times 0.005 \approx 1.5\%$ — matching the observed offline-online gap.

title: The Feature Freshness vs Cost Tradeoff
scenario: Your e-commerce recommendation system uses 300 features. The ML team wants all features computed in real time for maximum freshness. The infra team pushes back: the streaming infrastructure to compute 300 features in real time for 50M daily active users would cost $2M/year. Profile the features and propose a tiered architecture that captures 90% of the freshness value at 20% of the cost.
common_mistake: Treating all features as equally time-sensitive. In practice, user demographics change monthly, behavioral features change hourly, and contextual features change per-request. Computing 'user_age_bucket' in real time is pure waste.
realistic_solution: Tier features by staleness tolerance: **Tier 1 — Static (daily batch, ~$50K/yr):** User demographics, account age, device type. ~100 features. Updated nightly. **Tier 2 — Near-real-time (hourly micro-batch, ~$150K/yr):** Session-level aggregates, daily spend, category affinities. ~150 features. Updated every 1-4 hours via Spark micro-batches. **Tier 3 — Real-time (streaming, ~$200K/yr):** Cart contents, last-click item, session duration, fraud velocity signals. ~50 features. Updated sub-second via Flink. Total: $400K/yr vs $2M/yr (80% savings). The 50 real-time features capture the behavioral
napkin_math: Full real-time: 300 features × stream processing = $2M/yr. Tiered: Tier 1 (100 features, batch) = $50K. Tier 2 (150 features, micro-batch) = $150K. Tier 3 (50 features, streaming) = $200K. Total = $400K = 20% of full-streaming cost. Feature importance analysis shows top-50 time-sensitive features contribute 85-90% of model quality improvement from freshness.

title: The Embedding Sharding Strategy
scenario: Your production DLRM has 50 embedding tables totaling 400 GB. Table sizes range from 200 MB to 80 GB. You have 8 GPUs with 80 GB HBM each. A naive row-parallel sharding splits every table equally across all 8 GPUs. The system runs, but GPU utilization is only 25% and All-to-All communication dominates the profile. What went wrong, and how do you fix it?
common_mistake: Assuming that splitting every table across all GPUs is always optimal. Small tables sharded across 8 GPUs generate 8× the communication overhead for negligible memory benefit.
realistic_solution: Row-parallel sharding of all 50 tables across 8 GPUs means every forward pass requires an All-to-All collective to redistribute embeddings — regardless of table size. For a 200 MB table, each GPU holds only 25 MB, but the All-to-All overhead of exchanging lookups across 8 GPUs far exceeds the compute. The fix is a **mixed sharding strategy**: (1) **Table-wise placement** for small tables (< 10 GB): place entire table on a single GPU. With 35 small tables averaging 2 GB each = 70 GB, distribute across GPUs as whole tables. (2) **Column-wise sharding** for medium tables (10-40 GB): split embeddi
napkin_math: Naive: 50 tables × All-to-All per forward = massive comm overhead. All-to-All for 8 GPUs: $O(N \times \text{message size})$. Mixed: 35 small tables placed locally (zero comm), 10 medium tables with AllReduce (partial comm), 5 large tables with All-to-All. Communication volume drops ~75%. GPU utilization: 25% → 60-70%.

title: The Streaming Feature Consistency Trap
scenario: Your fraud detection model uses a streaming feature 'rolling_30min_transaction_count'. During a Kafka consumer rebalance, the Flink job restarts from a checkpoint. After recovery, you notice the feature value for some users is temporarily double-counted: transactions processed before the checkpoint are replayed and counted again. The model starts flagging legitimate users as fraudsters. How do you ensure exactly-once semantics for streaming features?
common_mistake: Assuming Kafka's exactly-once guarantee extends end-to-end. Kafka's EOS covers produce→consume, but the feature store write is a separate system — without idempotent writes, replayed events create duplicates.
realistic_solution: The double-counting happens because the Flink checkpoint captures Kafka offsets but the feature store write is not atomically coupled to the checkpoint. On recovery, events between the last checkpoint and the failure are replayed. If the feature store already committed those increments before the crash, they are applied twice. Three approaches: (1) **Idempotent writes**: include a sequence number or event ID with each feature update; the feature store deduplicates on write. (2) **Two-phase commit**: Flink's built-in exactly-once sink connector for supported stores (e.g., Kafka transactions + c
napkin_math: Rebalance frequency: ~1-2 per day in a healthy cluster. Checkpoint interval: 1 minute. Events to replay per rebalance: 1 minute × 10K events/sec = 600K events. If 1% of users are affected, that's ~6K users with inflated counts. At a 0.1% fraud threshold, double-counted users may see 2× normal velocity, flagging hundreds of false positives per rebalance event.

title: The Feature Store Hot Key Problem
scenario: Your feature store is backed by Redis Cluster with 16 shards. Feature keys are sharded by user_id. During a viral marketing campaign, a single celebrity influencer drives 500K simultaneous users to view their profile page. The recommendation model needs to look up the influencer's item features for ranking. Redis P99 latency spikes from 2ms to 800ms, and the single shard holding the influencer's user_id becomes CPU-saturated. How do you fix this without re-architecting the entire feature store?
common_mistake: Adding more Redis shards. The hot key lives on one shard — adding shards doesn't split a single key's traffic.
realistic_solution: This is the hot-key (celebrity) problem: a single key receives disproportionate read traffic that saturates one shard. Solutions (in order of implementation effort): (1) **Client-side caching with TTL**: Cache the influencer's features locally in the serving process for 5-10 seconds. 500K requests hit local memory instead of Redis. Cost: ~10 lines of code. (2) **Read replicas for hot keys**: Replicate the hot shard to 4-8 read replicas. Spread reads across replicas. Reduces per-node load by 4-8×. (3) **Key replication with jitter**: Store the same feature under keys `user:123:replica:0` throug
napkin_math: Single Redis shard: ~100K ops/sec max. Hot key traffic: 500K QPS × 3 feature lookups = 1.5M ops/sec to one shard. 15× overload. Client-side cache with 5s TTL: first request hits Redis, next 5s × 500K = 2.5M requests served from local cache. Redis load: ~200 ops/sec (one cache miss per 5s per server). 7,500× reduction.

title: The Feature Version Mismatch
scenario: Your feature engineering team deploys a new version of the 'user_engagement_score' feature that changes the normalization from min-max to z-score. The model serving system immediately starts using the new feature values, but the model was trained on min-max normalized values. The ranking model's click-through rate drops 15% before anyone notices. Design a feature versioning system that prevents this class of failures.
common_mistake: Relying on manual coordination ('just tell the model team when features change'). At scale with hundreds of features and dozens of models, manual coordination always fails.
realistic_solution: Implement a **feature contract system**: (1) **Versioned feature schemas**: every feature has a version tag (e.g., `user_engagement_score_v2`). The feature store serves both v1 and v2 simultaneously. (2) **Model-feature binding**: each model artifact declares the exact feature versions it was trained on (e.g., `requires: user_engagement_score_v1`). The serving system resolves feature lookups based on the model's declared dependencies. (3) **Compatibility checks**: before a new feature version is promoted to default, a CI job validates that no active model depends on the old version without a r
napkin_math: With 300 features and 20 models, a single uncoordinated feature change can break multiple models simultaneously. If feature changes happen 5×/week and each has a 10% chance of causing a model-feature mismatch, the expected time to incident is 2 weeks. With versioned contracts, mismatches are caught at deploy time (MTTD = 0).

title: The GPU Preprocessing Offload
scenario: Your training pipeline for a large recommendation model spends 60% of step time on CPU-based feature preprocessing (hashing, bucketing, normalization of 500 features) and only 40% on GPU forward/backward passes. The GPUs are idle 60% of the time. Adding more CPU workers doesn't help because the preprocessing is already distributed across 32 CPU cores. Design a system that moves feature preprocessing to the GPU and estimate the speedup.
common_mistake: Assuming all CPU preprocessing can trivially move to GPU. Operations like string hashing and dictionary lookups are not naturally parallel and may not benefit from GPU execution.
realistic_solution: Classify preprocessing operations by GPU-friendliness: **GPU-native (80% of ops):** Numeric normalization (z-score, min-max), bucketing (searchsorted), embedding lookups (already on GPU), log transforms, polynomial features. These are embarrassingly parallel. **CPU-better (20% of ops):** String hashing (MurmurHash — can be GPU-ported but complex), regex feature extraction, JSON parsing, variable-length list features. **Architecture:** Use NVIDIA DALI or NVTabular to build a GPU-native preprocessing pipeline. Preprocessing and model forward pass share the same GPU memory, eliminating CPU→GPU da
napkin_math: Current: CPU preprocessing = 60ms, GPU compute = 40ms, step = 100ms. GPU preprocessing: 80% of ops at 50× speedup = $0.6 \times 0.8 / 50 = 0.96\text{ms}$. Remaining CPU ops: $0.6 \times 0.2 = 12\text{ms}$ (overlapped with GPU). New step time: ~$\max(12, 40 + 1) = 41\text{ms}$. Speedup: $100/41 = 2.4\times$. GPU utilization: $40/41 = 97\%$.

title: The Unified Feature Store Architecture
scenario: Your company has 30 ML models across 5 teams, each with its own feature computation code. Feature definitions are duplicated in training (Python/Spark), serving (Java microservice), and analytics (SQL dashboards). A new compliance regulation requires you to prove that training and serving features are identical. You find 47 discrepancies across 300 features. Design a feature store architecture that guarantees training-serving consistency by construction, not by testing.
common_mistake: Building a feature store that is just a database — storing precomputed features without addressing the dual-compute-path problem. If training and serving still compute features via different code paths, the store only moves where the skew happens.
realistic_solution: Design principle: **single computation, dual materialization**. (1) **Single feature definition language**: Each feature is defined once in a declarative DSL (e.g., Feast feature definitions, Tecton transformations). The definition specifies the computation, not the implementation. (2) **Dual materialization engine**: The same definition is compiled to both a batch engine (Spark/BigQuery for training) and a streaming engine (Flink/Spark Streaming for real-time). Feature store SDKs ensure both paths produce identical outputs via automated consistency tests on overlapping time windows. (3) **Off
napkin_math: 47 discrepancies across 300 features = 15.7% inconsistency rate. If each discrepancy degrades model quality by 0.1-0.5%, cumulative impact is 5-24% quality loss. With a unified feature store: inconsistency rate drops to <0.1% (only from floating-point non-determinism). Migration cost: ~6 engineer-months. Break-even: 2-3 months of eliminated debugging time ($50K/month across 5 teams).

title: The Silent Feature Pipeline Failure
scenario: Your production recommendation model's conversion rate has been slowly declining over 3 weeks — from 4.2% to 3.1%. No model retraining has occurred. No code changes were deployed. All monitoring dashboards (GPU utilization, serving latency, error rate) show green. Walk through a systematic debugging process to identify the root cause, given that you suspect a feature pipeline issue.
common_mistake: Retraining the model immediately. If the root cause is a broken feature pipeline, retraining on corrupted features will just learn the corrupted distribution — the decline continues.
realistic_solution: Systematic feature pipeline diagnosis: **Step 1 — Feature distribution drift detection**: For each of the model's 300 features, compute the KL divergence between the current serving distribution and the training distribution. Flag features with KL > 0.1. **Step 2 — Identify the broken feature**: You find that 'user_category_affinity' has shifted dramatically: it was a float in [0, 1] during training but is now showing values clustered at exactly 0.0 for 40% of users. **Step 3 — Trace the pipeline**: The feature is computed by a Spark job that joins user clickstream data with a category taxonom
napkin_math: Feature importance of 'user_category_affinity': top-5 feature contributing ~8% of model's predictive power. With 40% of users seeing a zeroed feature, effective model quality loss ≈ $0.08 \times 0.40 = 3.2\%$ — matching the observed conversion decline from 4.2% to ~3.1%. Revenue impact at $10M monthly revenue: $3.2\% \times \$10\text{M} = \$320\text{K/month}$ lost for 3 weeks = ~$240K total.

title: The Feature Serving Latency Decomposition
scenario: Your search ranking model uses 150 batch features from a feature store and 20 real-time features computed on the fly. End-to-end serving P99 is 120ms against a 100ms SLA. The model inference takes 25ms. Decompose the remaining 95ms into feature retrieval components and propose optimizations to bring P99 under 100ms.
common_mistake: Assuming all 95ms is 'network latency' and trying to optimize the network. Feature serving latency has multiple distinct components that require different optimizations.
realistic_solution: **Latency decomposition**: (1) Feature store lookup (150 batch features via Redis MGET): ~8ms P50, ~25ms P99 (tail from Redis GC pauses and hot shards). (2) Real-time feature computation (20 features from raw signals): ~15ms P50, ~40ms P99 (Flink window lookups + aggregation). (3) Feature assembly and serialization: ~5ms (protobuf serialization of 170 features). (4) Network RTT (client → feature service → model service): ~10ms × 2 hops = 20ms. Total: $25 + 40 + 5 + 20 = 90\text{ms P50}$, $25 + 25 + 40 + 5 + 20 = 115\text{ms P99 (dominated by tail)}$. **Optimizations**: (a) **Parallelize**: Fet
napkin_math: Current P99: 120ms. Bottleneck: real-time feature computation (40ms P99) + sequential feature fetch. Parallelizing batch + real-time: P99 drops by $\min(25, 40) = 25\text{ms}$. Pre-computing real-time features: P99 drops by additional 35ms. New P99: $120 - 25 - 35 = 60\text{ms}$ — well under 100ms SLA.

title: The Multi-Model Feature Platform
scenario: You are the tech lead for a platform team at a company with 50 ML models across search, recommendations, ads, and trust & safety. Each team has built its own feature computation and storage. Total infrastructure cost is $5M/year across duplicated pipelines. Design a centralized feature platform that serves all 50 models with shared computation, while allowing individual teams to iterate independently on their own features. Address the organizational and technical tradeoffs.
common_mistake: Designing a monolithic feature platform that requires all teams to adopt the same tools, timelines, and release cadences. This creates a coordination bottleneck that slows everyone down — the cure is worse than the disease.
realistic_solution: **Architecture: Federated feature platform with shared infrastructure, decentralized ownership.** **(1) Shared layer (platform team owns):** Feature store infrastructure (Redis cluster for online, BigQuery/Hive for offline), stream processing framework (managed Flink), feature registry (metadata, lineage, schema validation), and monitoring/alerting. **(2) Team-owned layer:** Feature definitions (written in a shared DSL), feature-specific SLOs, A/B testing of new features, and per-model feature selection. **(3) Computation sharing:** The registry identifies when multiple teams compute semantica
napkin_math: Current: $5M/yr across 50 models. Duplicate features: ~35% of 300 total unique features are computed 2-4 times. Deduplication saves: $5M × 0.35 = $1.75M/yr. Platform operational efficiency (shared infra, auto-scaling): additional 15% savings = $750K. Total savings: ~$2.5M/yr (50% reduction). Platform team cost: 6 engineers × $300K fully loaded = $1.8M/yr. Net savings: $700K/yr + massive velocity improvement (teams ship features in hours, not weeks).

title: The Embedding Serving at 10M QPS
scenario: You need to serve embedding lookups for a search system at 10 million queries per second. Each query requires lookups from 5 embedding tables, each with 1 billion rows and 256-dimensional FP16 embeddings. Total embedding size is 2.56 TB. The P99 latency SLA for the embedding lookup service is 5ms. Design the serving architecture, including storage, caching, and networking.
common_mistake: Trying to serve 2.56 TB of embeddings from GPU HBM for latency. At 10M QPS × 5 tables × 512 bytes/embedding = 25.6 TB/s read bandwidth — far beyond any single-node memory system. This is a distributed systems problem, not a single-machine GPU problem.
realistic_solution: **Architecture: Distributed embedding serving with hot-tier caching.** **(1) Storage tier analysis:** 2.56 TB total. Power-law access: top 0.1% of rows (1M per table = 5M total) serve 80% of queries = ~2.56 GB hot tier. Top 5% (50M per table = 250M total) serve 99% of queries = ~128 GB warm tier. **(2) Hot tier (L1 — in-process memory):** Top 5M embeddings (2.56 GB) cached in each serving node's RAM. Latency: ~0.1ms. Handles 80% of lookups. **(3) Warm tier (L2 — distributed cache):** Top 250M embeddings (128 GB) distributed across a memcached/Redis cluster. Latency: ~1ms. Handles 19% of lookup
napkin_math: Total read bandwidth: $10\text{M QPS} \times 5 \times 512\text{B} = 25.6\text{ TB/s}$. L1 hit rate 80%: L1 serves $20.5\text{ TB/s}$ from local RAM. L2 hit rate 19%: L2 serves $4.9\text{ TB/s}$ across 16 Redis nodes = 306 GB/s per node (within 10 Gbps NIC capacity with batching). L3 hit rate 1%: L3 serves $256\text{ GB/s}$ across 32 SSD nodes = 8 GB/s per node (within NVMe capacity). Serving fleet: 200 nodes × $0.50/hr = $100/hr = $876K/yr.

title: The End-to-End Feature Pipeline Redesign
scenario: After a series of production incidents caused by training-serving skew, your VP of Engineering mandates: 'I want zero training-serving skew, guaranteed by architecture.' You currently have separate Python training pipelines and Java serving pipelines, 500 features, 30 models, and a team of 15 ML engineers. The VP gives you 6 months and 4 engineers. Design the architecture, migration plan, and validation framework.
common_mistake: Promising zero skew by rewriting all 500 features in a new system in 6 months. This is a 12-18 month project at minimum. The real skill is scoping a phased approach that delivers value incrementally.
realistic_solution: **Phase 1 (Month 1-2): Detection and triage.** Deploy a skew detection framework that runs parallel feature computation (training path vs serving path) for all 500 features on a 1% sample of production traffic. Quantify skew per feature (Jensen-Shannon divergence). Prioritize: ~50 features will show significant skew (JSD > 0.05). The remaining 450 are already consistent enough. **Phase 2 (Month 2-4): Unified computation for top-50 skewed features.** Write feature definitions in a single language (Python using Feast/Tecton SDK). The SDK generates both the batch (training) and online (serving) c
napkin_math: 50 skewed features × 2 engineer-weeks each = 100 engineer-weeks = 25 months of work for 1 engineer = 6.25 months for 4 engineers. Barely fits in 6 months for just the top-50. Full 500-feature migration: 500 × 0.5 engineer-weeks (simpler features) + 50 × 2 weeks (complex) = 350 engineer-weeks = 22 months for 4 engineers. Revenue recovered by fixing top-50 skewed features: if each costs ~0.1% conversion, $50 \times 0.001 \times \$100\text{M revenue} = \$5\text{M/yr}$.

title: The Adam Optimizer Memory Multiplier
scenario: You want to fine-tune a 7B parameter model in BF16 on a single A100-80GB GPU. The weights alone occupy 14 GB. You launch training with the Adam optimizer, and PyTorch immediately throws an OOM error. The model weights fit easily — what is consuming the remaining 66 GB?
common_mistake: Assuming the OOM is caused by batch size or activation memory. In reality, Adam optimizer states alone require far more memory than the model weights.
realistic_solution: Adam stores two additional states per parameter: the first moment (mean) and second moment (variance), each in FP32 (4 bytes). Combined with the FP32 master weights and FP32 gradients, training requires roughly 4x the weight memory. 7B params: 14 GB weights (BF16) + 28 GB optimizer states (2 × FP32) + 14 GB gradients (BF16) + 28 GB master weights (FP32) = ~84 GB — exceeds 80 GB before a single activation is stored.
napkin_math: Weights: 7B × 2 bytes = 14 GB | Master weights (FP32): 7B × 4 bytes = 28 GB | Gradients: 7B × 2 bytes = 14 GB | Adam m + v: 7B × 4 bytes × 2 = 56 GB | Total: ~112 GB (without activations) — way over 80 GB

title: The Gradient Accumulation Trick
scenario: Your GPU can only fit a micro-batch of 4 samples due to memory constraints, but your training recipe calls for an effective batch size of 256. Without adding more GPUs, how do you achieve this effective batch size, and what is the computational cost?
common_mistake: Assuming you need 64 GPUs to train with batch size 256, or that reducing batch size to 4 is acceptable despite known convergence issues at that scale.
realistic_solution: Use gradient accumulation: run 64 forward-backward passes with micro-batch 4, accumulating gradients in-place, then perform one optimizer step. The effective batch size is 64 × 4 = 256. The cost: training time scales linearly — 64 micro-steps per optimizer step, meaning each weight update takes 64× longer than a single micro-batch iteration. The memory cost stays constant because you never store more than 4 samples of activations.
napkin_math: Micro-batch: 4 | Accumulation steps: 256/4 = 64 | Time per weight update: 64 × (forward + backward for 4 samples) | Memory: same as batch_size=4 | Throughput: 256 samples per update, but wall-clock time = 64× a single micro-step

title: The Critical Batch Size
scenario: Your colleague proposes doubling the batch size to cut training time in half. Under what condition does this linear speedup hold, and when does it break down?
common_mistake: Believing that doubling batch size always halves training time. This holds only in the data-parallel regime below the critical batch size.
realistic_solution: There is a critical batch size B_crit for every model and dataset. Below B_crit, doubling batch size roughly halves the number of steps needed (data-parallel scaling holds). Above B_crit, doubling batch size yields diminishing returns — you process more samples per step but need nearly the same total number of gradient updates to converge. Beyond ~2× B_crit, you are wasting compute. For GPT-3-scale models, B_crit is typically 1-10M tokens, depending on the training phase.
napkin_math: Below B_crit: 2× batch → ~0.5× steps → ~1× total compute (perfect scaling) | Above B_crit: 2× batch → ~0.9× steps → ~1.8× total compute (diminishing returns) | B_crit for large LLMs: ~1-10M tokens

title: The Training Time Estimation
scenario: You need to estimate how long it will take to pre-train a 70B parameter model on 2 trillion tokens using a cluster of 512 H100 GPUs. Assume each H100 achieves 40% MFU (Model FLOPS Utilization) of its 989 TFLOPS BF16 peak. How many days will training take?
common_mistake: Forgetting the 6× FLOPS multiplier for training (2× forward pass FLOPS for the backward pass, with the forward being 2 × params × tokens), or using peak FLOPS instead of effective FLOPS.
realistic_solution: Total training FLOPS ≈ 6 × 70B × 2T = 8.4 × 10^23 FLOPS. Effective throughput per GPU: 989 TFLOPS × 0.4 = 395.6 TFLOPS. Cluster throughput: 512 × 395.6 = 202,547 TFLOPS = 2.025 × 10^17 FLOPS/s. Time = 8.4 × 10^23 / 2.025 × 10^17 = 4.15 × 10^6 seconds ≈ 48 days.
napkin_math: Training FLOPS: 6 × 70e9 × 2e12 = 8.4e23 | Per-GPU effective: 989 × 0.4 = ~396 TFLOPS | Cluster: 512 × 396 = ~203,000 TFLOPS | Time: 8.4e23 / 2.03e17 ≈ 4.1e6 s ≈ 48 days

title: The Loss Spike at Step 50K
scenario: Your 13B LLM pre-training run has been progressing smoothly for 50,000 steps. Suddenly, the training loss spikes from 2.8 to 15.0 and does not recover after 500 steps. The learning rate schedule shows you are in the cosine decay phase. What are the three most likely root causes, ordered from most to least common?
common_mistake: Immediately blaming hardware failure or data corruption. While possible, gradient-related instabilities are far more common at this scale.
realistic_solution: 1. **Gradient explosion from a bad data batch**: A batch with anomalously long sequences or corrupted data can produce extreme gradients. Check: inspect the batch at step 50K, look for NaN/Inf in gradients. 2. **Learning rate too high for this loss landscape region**: As the model traverses the loss landscape, it may enter a sharper region where the current LR causes overshooting. Check: was there a warmup restart or schedule bug? 3. **Numerical instability in mixed precision**: BF16 underflow in small gradient values that accumulate into FP32, or loss scaling failures in FP16 training. Check:
napkin_math: Gradient norm monitoring: healthy range ~0.1-10.0, spike to >100 signals explosion | Loss scaler skip rate: healthy <1%, spike to >50% signals precision instability | Batch inspection: check max sequence length and token distribution at the offending step

title: The Warmup Necessity
scenario: A junior engineer removes the learning rate warmup from your LLM pre-training recipe, arguing that 2,000 warmup steps waste compute on a 300,000-step run. Training diverges within the first 100 steps. Why is warmup critical for large models with Adam, and how do you calculate a reasonable warmup duration?
common_mistake: Thinking warmup is just a hyperparameter tuning hack. It addresses a fundamental numerical issue with adaptive optimizers at initialization.
realistic_solution: At step 0, Adam's second moment (v_t) estimates are initialized to zero and biased low. This causes the effective learning rate (LR / sqrt(v_t + eps)) to be astronomically large in early steps — potentially 100-1000× the intended LR. Warmup gradually increases the nominal LR, giving the second moment estimates time to accumulate meaningful statistics. Rule of thumb for LLM pre-training: warmup steps = 0.5-1% of total steps, or until the gradient statistics stabilize (monitor gradient norm variance). For a 300K step run, 2,000 steps (0.67%) is reasonable.
napkin_math: Step 1 without warmup: v_1 ≈ (1-β₂) × g₁² ≈ 0.001 × g₁² (very small) | Effective LR ≈ LR / sqrt(0.001 × g₁²) = LR / (0.032 × |g₁|) — can be 30× higher than intended | With warmup: nominal LR at step 1 is LR × (1/2000) = 0.0005× LR — safely small

title: The FP16 Loss Scaling Dance
scenario: You switch a training run from BF16 to FP16 to use Tensor Core optimizations on older V100 GPUs. Training appears to work for 1,000 steps, then the loss flatlines and stops decreasing. Gradient norms show zero. What happened, and what mechanism should have prevented this?
common_mistake: Assuming gradients are actually zero because the model converged. In reality, small gradients underflow to zero in FP16's limited dynamic range.
realistic_solution: FP16 has a much smaller dynamic range than BF16 (max ~65K, min ~6×10⁻⁸). Small gradient values — common in later training stages — underflow to zero in FP16. Dynamic loss scaling should prevent this: it multiplies the loss by a large factor (e.g., 2¹⁶ = 65536) before the backward pass, keeping gradients in FP16's representable range. After backward, gradients are unscaled before the optimizer step. If loss scaling fails (e.g., the scale becomes too small after repeated overflows), gradients silently underflow. Check: is the loss scaler's current scale very small? Has it been reducing after rep
napkin_math: FP16 smallest normal: ~6e-8 | Typical late-training gradient: ~1e-7 | Without scaling: gradients at 1e-7 barely survive in FP16 | With loss scale 65536: gradient becomes 1e-7 × 65536 ≈ 6.5e-3 — safely representable | BF16 range: ~1e-38 to ~3e38 — underflow is essentially never a problem

title: The Activation Checkpointing Tradeoff
scenario: You are training a 30B model and cannot fit a single sample through the forward+backward pass due to activation memory. Your colleague suggests activation checkpointing (gradient checkpointing). What is the memory-compute tradeoff, and how do you decide which layers to checkpoint?
common_mistake: Thinking activation checkpointing is free, or that it always saves exactly sqrt(N) memory. The actual tradeoff depends on the checkpointing strategy.
realistic_solution: Activation checkpointing discards intermediate activations during forward pass and recomputes them during backward pass. Full checkpointing (every layer boundary) reduces activation memory from O(L) to O(sqrt(L)) with ~33% more compute (one extra forward pass). Selective checkpointing is more efficient: checkpoint only the memory-expensive layers (attention and MLP) while keeping cheap layers (LayerNorm, residual adds). For a 30B transformer with 48 layers: without checkpointing ~60 GB activations (batch=1, seq=2048); with full checkpointing ~8-10 GB activations but ~33% slower training.
napkin_math: Without checkpointing: 48 layers × ~1.25 GB/layer = ~60 GB activations | Full checkpointing: sqrt(48) ≈ 7 segments → ~7 × 1.25 GB = ~9 GB | Compute cost: 1 extra forward pass ≈ 33% overhead (backward ≈ 2× forward) | Selective (attention only): ~30 GB saved, ~20% overhead

title: The Batch Size Scaling Wall
scenario: Your team scales a GPT training run from 64 to 512 GPUs using data parallelism, increasing the global batch size from 1M to 8M tokens. Per-step training time drops 6.5× (not 8× due to AllReduce overhead), but final model quality at 300B tokens is significantly worse than the 64-GPU baseline. The learning rate was scaled linearly with batch size per standard practice. Diagnose the quality regression.
common_mistake: Blaming AllReduce overhead or assuming the LR scaling rule is wrong. The real issue is that 8M tokens exceeds the critical batch size for this model at this stage of training.
realistic_solution: The global batch size of 8M tokens has exceeded the critical batch size B_crit. Beyond B_crit, each additional sample per batch provides diminishing gradient signal — the gradient estimate is already accurate enough, and more samples per step just waste compute without improving convergence per update. The linear LR scaling rule (Goyal et al.) only works in the data-parallel regime (B < B_crit). Solutions: (1) Use gradient accumulation to keep effective batch size at 1-2M while still utilizing all 512 GPUs with smaller micro-batches. (2) Use SQRT scaling for the learning rate instead of linear
napkin_math: B_crit estimation via gradient noise scale: B_noise = tr(H) / |G|² | If B_noise ≈ 2M tokens, then at 8M you are 4× over-provisioned | Effective compute efficiency: at 4× B_crit, roughly 60% of compute is wasted | Fix: micro-batch=2K tokens/GPU × 512 GPUs = 1M tokens, accumulate if needed

title: The Training Memory Budget Decomposition
scenario: You have a single A100-80GB and want to train the largest model possible from scratch. Walk through the complete memory budget decomposition and determine the maximum model size (in parameters) you can train with Adam in mixed precision (BF16 compute, FP32 optimizer), batch size 1, sequence length 2048.
common_mistake: Only accounting for weights and optimizer states, forgetting that activation memory grows with sequence length and model width, and that CUDA context and memory fragmentation consume 2-5 GB.
realistic_solution: Memory budget: 80 GB total, ~2 GB for CUDA context = 78 GB usable. Per-parameter costs: BF16 weights (2B) + FP32 master weights (4B) + FP32 Adam m (4B) + FP32 Adam v (4B) + BF16 gradients (2B) = 16 bytes/param. At 78 GB for parameters: 78 GB / 16 B = ~4.9B params maximum IF activations were zero. But activations for a 4.9B model at seq_len=2048, batch=1: roughly 10-15 GB (depends on architecture). With activation checkpointing: ~3-5 GB. So realistic maximum: ~4B params with checkpointing, ~3B without.
napkin_math: Per-param training cost: 2 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 2 = 16 bytes | 80 GB - 2 GB CUDA - 5 GB activations (w/ checkpointing) = 73 GB | Max params: 73 GB / 16 B ≈ 4.6B | Without checkpointing (15 GB activations): 63 GB / 16 B ≈ 3.9B | Practical answer: ~3-4B params on one A100-80GB

title: The Gradient Norm Anomaly
scenario: Your monitoring dashboard shows that gradient clipping (max_norm=1.0) is activating on 85% of training steps for your 70B model. A colleague says this is fine because clipping prevents divergence. Is this a healthy training run?
common_mistake: Accepting 85% clip rate as normal. If clipping is firing on most steps, it is not a safety net — it is actively modifying the optimizer's intended update direction on nearly every step.
realistic_solution: An 85% clip rate is a major red flag. It means the optimizer is almost never taking the step it intended — the effective learning rate is being reduced on most steps because clipping scales the entire gradient vector down. This typically indicates: (1) the learning rate is too high for this model/data combination, (2) the batch size is too small causing high gradient variance, or (3) there is a data quality issue causing high-norm gradient outliers. Healthy training: clip rate should be 1-5% (rare emergency brake). Diagnosis: plot gradient norm distribution. If it is bimodal (a cluster at norm
napkin_math: With max_norm=1.0 and actual grad norm=5.0: effective step is 1/5 of intended | 85% clipping → 85% of steps take a smaller step than Adam computed | Effective LR ≈ scheduled_LR × (1.0 / median_grad_norm) | If median norm is 3.0: effective LR is ~1/3 of configured LR

title: The Learning Rate Schedule Selection
scenario: You are designing the training recipe for a new 20B LLM. Compare cosine annealing vs. constant-then-linear-decay (Chinchilla-style) vs. warmup-stable-decay (WSD) learning rate schedules. Your training run is 500B tokens and you may need to continue pre-training on additional data later. Which schedule do you choose and why?
common_mistake: Choosing cosine annealing because it is the most common in papers, without considering the implications for continued pre-training.
realistic_solution: Cosine annealing decays the LR to near-zero by the end of training, making continued pre-training difficult — you must restart with a warmup that destabilizes the model. Chinchilla-style (constant for 80%, linear decay for 20%) is better for fixed-budget runs but still commits to a decay endpoint. WSD (warmup-stable-decay) is optimal for your use case: the model trains at a constant LR for most of the run (after warmup), and you only begin decay when you are certain about the total budget. If new data arrives, you can extend the stable phase indefinitely. The tradeoff: WSD may converge slightl
napkin_math: Cosine: LR at step 500B ≈ 0 → continued training requires LR restart (often 5-10% quality loss) | WSD: LR at step 500B = peak LR (if decay not triggered) → seamless continuation | Empirical: WSD matches cosine within ~1% perplexity for equal compute, but allows arbitrary extension

title: The Pre-Training Cost Estimate
scenario: Your VP asks for a cost estimate to pre-train a 70B model on 2T tokens. You have access to H100 instances at $3/GPU-hour on a cloud provider. Estimate the total training cost, including a realistic buffer for failures and restarts.
common_mistake: Computing raw GPU-hours from FLOPS alone and presenting it as the final number. Production training runs are never 100% efficient — failures, restarts, evaluation, hyperparameter tuning, and checkpoint I/O all add overhead.
realistic_solution: From the training time estimation: ~48 days on 512 H100s at 40% MFU. GPU-hours = 512 GPUs × 48 days × 24 hours = 589,824 GPU-hours. Raw cost: 589,824 × $3 = $1.77M. But real training overhead: (1) Hardware failures require rollback to checkpoint (~5-10% wasted compute), (2) Evaluation runs consume ~5% of GPU-hours, (3) Hyperparameter tuning at smaller scale: ~10% of main run budget, (4) Initial failed runs that diverge: ~5-10%. Total multiplier: 1.25-1.35×. Realistic budget: $1.77M × 1.3 = $2.3M. Plus storage, networking, engineering time: total project cost ~$3-4M.
napkin_math: GPU-hours: 512 × 48 × 24 = 589,824 | Raw compute cost: 590K × $3 = $1.77M | Failure overhead (1.3×): $2.3M | Storage (checkpoints, data): ~$100K | Engineering (6 months, 3 engineers): ~$600K | Total project: ~$3M

title: The Optimizer Memory Tradeoff at Scale
scenario: A team training a 175B LLM proposes switching from Adam to SGD with momentum to save memory — SGD stores only 1 state tensor per parameter instead of Adam's 2, potentially saving hundreds of GB across the cluster. Evaluate this proposal. When would you consider it, and when is Adam's memory overhead justified?
common_mistake: Dismissing SGD entirely for large-scale LLM training, or accepting it uncritically because it saves memory. The answer depends on the loss landscape and the scale.
realistic_solution: For a 175B model: Adam stores m + v = 2 × 175B × 4 bytes = 1.4 TB of optimizer state across the cluster. SGD with momentum stores only momentum = 175B × 4 bytes = 700 GB — saving 700 GB total. However, Adam's per-parameter adaptive learning rates are critical for LLMs because: (1) different parameter groups (embeddings, attention, FFN) have gradient distributions that vary by orders of magnitude, (2) Adam adapts to the local curvature, which matters enormously in the flat-but-sharp landscape of large transformers, (3) empirically, SGD requires 3-5× more tokens to reach the same loss as Adam fo
napkin_math: Adam: 175B × 4B × 2 states = 1.4 TB optimizer state | SGD+momentum: 175B × 4B × 1 state = 700 GB | 8-bit Adam: 175B × 1B × 2 states = 350 GB (saves more than SGD!) | ZeRO Stage 1: 1.4 TB / N_GPUs — at 512 GPUs = 2.7 GB/GPU | SGD convergence tax: 3-5× more tokens ≈ $5-10M more compute cost

title: The Slow Training Step Diagnosis
scenario: Your 70B model training on 256 H100 GPUs shows each training step takes 12 seconds, but your napkin math predicts it should take ~4 seconds. GPU utilization averages 35%. Walk through a systematic profiling methodology to identify where the 8 seconds are being lost.
common_mistake: Immediately blaming the network or AllReduce. A 3× gap requires decomposing the step into all components.
realistic_solution: Systematic decomposition of one training step: (1) **Forward pass**: profile with nsys — check kernel occupancy and whether matmuls are hitting Tensor Cores (look for SM efficiency >80%). (2) **Backward pass**: should be ~2× forward. If >2.5×, check for unintended gradient computation on frozen layers or recomputation without checkpointing benefits. (3) **AllReduce**: time the gradient sync. For 70B params in BF16 = 140 GB. Ring AllReduce with 256 GPUs over NVLink+IB: theoretical ~1.5s. If actual >3s, check for stragglers or network congestion. (4) **Optimizer step**: Adam update on 70B params
napkin_math: Expected: forward ~1s + backward ~2s + AllReduce ~1s + optimizer ~0.2s = ~4.2s | If data loading adds 3s between steps → 7.2s | If AllReduce has straggler adding 2s → 9.2s | If both: 12.2s (matches observation) | Diagnosis: nsys trace reveals gaps between backward end and AllReduce start → data pipeline stall

title: The Chinchilla Scaling Decision
scenario: You have a fixed GPU budget of 1M H100-hours. The Chinchilla scaling law says the compute-optimal 70B model should be trained on ~1.4T tokens. But your team has only 500B tokens of high-quality data. Do you: (A) train a 70B model on 500B tokens (undertrained by Chinchilla), (B) train a smaller ~25B model on 500B tokens (Chinchilla-optimal for your data), or (C) train a 70B model and repeat the 500B tokens ~3 times? Justify quantitatively.
common_mistake: Blindly following Chinchilla to train a smaller model. Recent evidence (Llama, Mistral) shows that inference-optimal scaling prefers larger models trained beyond Chinchilla-optimal because inference cost dominates deployment cost.
realistic_solution: The answer depends on whether you are optimizing for **training cost** or **total cost of ownership (training + inference)**. Chinchilla-optimal (B) minimizes training loss per FLOP, but a 70B model trained on even 500B tokens will likely outperform a 25B model trained on the same data for downstream tasks. Option C (repeating data) shows diminishing returns after ~4 epochs on the same data — empirically, quality plateaus after 2-3 repeats. Best strategy: Option A with a twist — train the 70B model on 500B tokens (Chinchilla-undertrained), accept the training inefficiency, but win on inference
napkin_math: Chinchilla: N_opt ∝ C^0.5, D_opt ∝ C^0.5 | 1M H100-hours ≈ 3.5e23 FLOPS | Chinchilla-optimal: ~70B params, ~1.4T tokens | Your data constraint: 500B tokens | Option A (70B, 500B): uses 35% of budget, strong model, suboptimal training efficiency | Option B (25B, 500B): uses 100% of budget, weaker model, Chinchilla-optimal | Inference cost: 70B-INT4 ≈ 35 GB, 25B-INT4 ≈ 12.5 GB — but 70B quality >> 25B quality | Choose A if inference-heavy, B if training-cost-constrained

title: The Gradient Accumulation vs. Data Parallelism Choice
scenario: You need an effective batch size of 4M tokens for your 13B model. You have 128 GPUs available. Compare two approaches: (A) micro-batch 32K tokens/GPU with no gradient accumulation (128 × 32K = 4M), vs. (B) micro-batch 8K tokens/GPU with 4 accumulation steps (128 × 8K × 4 = 4M). Both achieve the same effective batch size. What are the systems-level tradeoffs?
common_mistake: Assuming both approaches are identical because they achieve the same effective batch size. The systems implications are significantly different.
realistic_solution: Approach A (large micro-batch, no accumulation): Higher activation memory per GPU (32K tokens worth of activations), but only 1 AllReduce per optimizer step. The larger matmuls have higher arithmetic intensity → better GPU utilization. Risk: may OOM at 32K token micro-batch for a 13B model. Approach B (small micro-batch, 4× accumulation): Lower activation memory per GPU (only 8K tokens stored), but still only 1 AllReduce per optimizer step (gradients accumulate locally). The smaller matmuls have lower arithmetic intensity → worse GPU utilization per micro-step. However, you can overlap forward
napkin_math: Approach A: 1 forward(32K) + 1 backward(32K) + 1 AllReduce = 1 step | Matmul size: 32K × hidden → high arithmetic intensity, ~45% MFU | Memory: ~40 GB activations (may OOM with optimizer states) | Approach B: 4 × [forward(8K) + backward(8K)] + 1 AllReduce = 1 step | Matmul size: 8K × hidden → lower arithmetic intensity, ~35% MFU | Memory: ~10 GB activations (safe) | B is ~20% slower but fits in memory

title: The 10K-GPU Training Instability
scenario: You are leading pre-training of a 405B model on 16,384 H100 GPUs. At step 80K (out of 300K total), the training loss suddenly diverges. You roll back to the step-79K checkpoint and resume, but the divergence happens again at approximately the same step. This is not a hardware failure (the cluster is healthy). Design a systematic approach to diagnose and recover from this reproducible training instability.
common_mistake: Simply reducing the learning rate globally or skipping the offending data batch. Both are band-aids that may mask deeper issues and cost significant compute.
realistic_solution: This is a loss landscape instability at a specific training phase — the model has entered a sharp region of the loss landscape. Systematic diagnosis: (1) **Characterize the instability**: log per-layer gradient norms, activation norms, and attention entropy around step 78K-82K. Look for which layers spike first — typically attention logits or embedding norms. (2) **Data investigation**: extract the exact data batches at steps 79K-81K. Check for distribution shifts in the data curriculum that coincide with the spike. (3) **Targeted interventions** (in order of disruption): (a) Reduce LR by 10-3
napkin_math: 16,384 GPUs × $3/hr = $49K/hour | Rollback from step 80K to 79K = 1,000 steps lost | At ~12s/step = 12,000s = 3.3 hours = $164K wasted per rollback | If it takes 3 rollbacks to diagnose: ~$500K wasted | z-loss overhead: <1% compute cost, prevents most instabilities | ROI of loss spike detection: saves $500K+ per avoided incident

title: The Training Recipe Design
scenario: You are architecting the full training recipe for a new 40B model. Your cluster has 1,024 H100-80GB GPUs, 400 Gbps InfiniBand, and 3PB of training data (5T tokens). Design the complete training configuration: precision format, optimizer, learning rate schedule, batch size ramp, parallelism strategy, and checkpoint policy. Justify each choice with quantitative reasoning.
common_mistake: Copying a recipe from a published paper without adapting to your specific hardware, data budget, and model size. Each dimension interacts with the others.
realistic_solution: **Precision**: BF16 mixed precision with FP32 master weights. BF16 over FP16 because the 8-bit exponent prevents underflow without loss scaling complexity. FP8 (E4M3 forward, E5M2 backward) if H100 Transformer Engine is stable for your architecture — saves 40% memory bandwidth. **Optimizer**: AdamW with β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight_decay=0.1. Per-param memory: 16 bytes (BF16 weights + FP32 master + FP32 m + FP32 v + BF16 grads). Total optimizer state: 40B × 16 = 640 GB. **Parallelism**: 40B model fits on 1 GPU for inference (80 GB > 40B × 2 = 80 GB), but not for training (640 GB optimizer). Use FSD
napkin_math: FLOPS: 6 × 40e9 × 5e12 = 1.2e24 | Cluster TFLOPS: 1024 × 989 × 0.4 = 405K TFLOPS | Time: 1.2e24 / 4.05e17 = 2.96e6 s ≈ 34 days | Cost: 1024 × 34 × 24 × $3 = $2.5M compute | Memory/GPU: 0.625 GB optimizer + ~10 GB activations (w/ checkpointing, TP=4) + ~80 MB weights (sharded) ≈ 11 GB — plenty of headroom

title: The Continual Pre-Training Dilemma
scenario: Your company has a general-purpose 70B LLM that cost $4M to pre-train. You need a domain-specific version for medical text. You have 100B tokens of medical data. Design the continual pre-training strategy, addressing: how to prevent catastrophic forgetting of general capabilities, what learning rate to use relative to the original pre-training LR, and how to validate that the model improved on medical tasks without regressing on general benchmarks.
common_mistake: Simply continuing training on medical data at the original learning rate. This causes catastrophic forgetting — the model rapidly overwrites general knowledge with domain-specific patterns.
realistic_solution: **Learning rate**: Use 10-20% of the original peak LR (if original was 3×10⁻⁴, use 3-6×10⁻⁵). Higher LR destroys general knowledge; lower LR learns too slowly. Use WSD schedule: brief warmup (200 steps), stable phase for 90% of tokens, decay for final 10%. **Data mixing**: Mix medical data with a replay buffer of general data. Ratio: 70-80% medical, 20-30% general (sampled from original pre-training distribution). This provides an implicit regularization against forgetting. **Validation**: Create a dual evaluation suite: (a) Medical benchmarks (MedQA, PubMedQA, clinical NER), (b) General bench
napkin_math: Full continual PT: 6 × 70e9 × 100e9 = 4.2e22 FLOPS | 64 H100s × 989T × 0.4 = 25.3K TFLOPS | Time: 4.2e22 / 2.53e16 ≈ 1.66e6 s ≈ 19 days | Wait — that is expensive. With only 64 GPUs: reduce to 100B tokens subset priority → ~19 days, ~$110K | With 256 GPUs: ~5 days, ~$92K | LoRA alternative: only update ~500M params, 10× faster convergence → 1-2 days on 8 GPUs ≈ $600

title: The Warp Size Thread Count
scenario: A junior engineer writes a CUDA kernel launching 100 threads per block to process a 100-element vector. They notice that GPU utilization is low even though every thread has work. How many threads actually execute per scheduling unit on an NVIDIA GPU, and why does launching 100 threads per block waste resources?
common_mistake: Thinking that every thread maps to an independent execution unit, like CPU cores. Engineers unfamiliar with SIMT assume all 100 threads run independently and efficiently.
realistic_solution: NVIDIA GPUs execute threads in groups of 32 called warps. A block of 100 threads creates 4 warps: three full warps (32 threads each = 96 threads) and one partial warp with only 4 active threads. That last warp wastes 28/32 = 87.5% of its execution slots. The SM must still schedule and execute the full warp, but 28 lanes sit idle. Rounding block size up to 128 (4 full warps) eliminates the partial warp entirely.
napkin_math: 100 threads / 32 threads per warp = 3 full warps + 1 partial warp (4 threads). Utilization of partial warp: 4/32 = 12.5%. Overall thread utilization: 100/128 = 78.1%. With 128 threads: 128/128 = 100% warp utilization.

title: The Memory Coalescing Penalty
scenario: Two CUDA kernels process the same 2D matrix stored in row-major order. Kernel A has each thread in a warp access consecutive elements in the same row (thread $i$ accesses `M[row][i]`). Kernel B has each thread access elements in the same column (thread $i$ accesses `M[i][col]`). Both kernels perform the same arithmetic. On an H100 with 3.35 TB/s HBM bandwidth, Kernel A runs 8x faster than Kernel B. Why?
common_mistake: Assuming both access patterns are equivalent because they access the same total number of elements. Engineers from CPU backgrounds may not realize that GPU memory transactions are coalesced at the warp level.
realistic_solution: GPU memory controllers coalesce memory requests from threads within the same warp into a minimum number of cache-line-sized transactions (128 bytes on modern GPUs). In Kernel A, 32 consecutive threads access 32 consecutive 4-byte floats = 128 bytes, which is exactly one cache line transaction. In Kernel B, 32 threads access elements separated by a full row stride, causing up to 32 separate cache line fetches for the same warp. Each fetch loads 128 bytes but only uses 4 bytes, wasting 124/128 = 97% of bandwidth. The effective bandwidth drops from 3.35 TB/s to roughly 3.35 TB/s / 32 = ~105 GB/s.
napkin_math: Coalesced (Kernel A): 32 threads x 4 bytes = 128 bytes per transaction. 1 transaction per warp. Strided (Kernel B): 32 threads x 1 cache line (128 bytes) = 4,096 bytes fetched but only 128 bytes used. Bandwidth waste ratio: 4096/128 = 32x. Effective bandwidth: 3.35 TB/s / 32 = ~105 GB/s. Speedup of A over B: ~32x theoretical, ~8x in practice due to L2 cache partially mitigating strided accesses.

title: The Occupancy-Bound Kernel
scenario: You write a CUDA kernel that uses 64 registers per thread and 48 KB of shared memory per block, with 256 threads per block. The target H100 SM has 65,536 registers, 228 KB of shared memory, and supports a maximum of 2,048 resident threads. The profiler reports only 12.5% occupancy. Diagnose which resource is the binding constraint limiting occupancy.
common_mistake: Blaming shared memory without doing the math. Engineers often guess the largest resource consumer rather than computing the bottleneck for each resource independently.
realistic_solution: You must check all three resource limits independently: (1) Registers: 256 threads x 64 registers = 16,384 registers per block. The SM has 65,536 registers, so it can host 65,536 / 16,384 = 4 blocks. (2) Shared memory: 48 KB per block. The SM has 228 KB, so it can host 228 / 48 = 4 blocks (rounding down). (3) Threads: 256 threads per block. Max 2,048 threads per SM, so 2,048 / 256 = 8 blocks. The binding constraint is the tie between registers and shared memory, both limiting the SM to 4 blocks. At 4 blocks x 256 threads = 1,024 resident threads out of 2,048 maximum, occupancy = 1,024 / 2,048
napkin_math: Register limit: 65,536 / (256 x 64) = 4 blocks. Shared mem limit: 228 KB / 48 KB = 4 blocks. Thread limit: 2,048 / 256 = 8 blocks. Min(4, 4, 8) = 4 blocks. Occupancy: (4 x 256) / 2,048 = 50%. If observed is 12.5% (1 block), check for dynamic shared memory allocation or L1/shared memory carve-out configuration.

title: The Warp Divergence Tax
scenario: You profile a sparse attention kernel that uses an `if-else` branch to skip zero-valued elements. The kernel's arithmetic intensity should place it in the compute-bound region of the roofline, yet the profiler shows it achieves only 15% of peak FLOPS. The profiler also reports 'branch divergence' at 65%. Explain the performance gap and estimate the throughput loss from this divergence pattern.
common_mistake: Thinking branch divergence causes random thread stalls like on a CPU. On a GPU, the penalty is not random delays but serialized execution of both paths within the same warp.
realistic_solution: In SIMT execution, all 32 threads in a warp must execute the same instruction at the same time. When threads within a warp take different branches (diverge), the GPU serializes execution: first all threads taking the `if` path execute while the others are masked (idle), then all threads taking the `else` path execute while the first group is masked. With 65% divergence, most warps execute both paths, effectively doubling the instruction count. For a 50/50 branch split, peak throughput halves. But with sparse attention, the zero-skipping branch is ~65% of threads while the compute branch is ~35
napkin_math: Without divergence: 32 threads execute useful FLOPs every cycle. With 65% divergence (65% of warps diverge): divergent warps execute both paths serially. If 35% of threads do compute and 65% skip: useful throughput per divergent warp = 35% during compute pass + 0% during skip pass. Fraction of cycles doing useful work: 35% / (35% + 65%) = 35% of instruction slots. Across all warps: 0.35 x 0.65 (divergent) + 1.0 x 0.35 (non-divergent) = 0.228 + 0.35 = ~0.58 instruction efficiency. Combined with other overhead: ~15% of peak FLOPS observed.

title: The Shared Memory Tiling Matmul
scenario: A naive CUDA matrix multiplication kernel for C = A x B (all 4096 x 4096 FP32) loads each element of A and B directly from HBM for every multiply-accumulate. You are asked to estimate the HBM traffic for this naive approach versus a tiled approach using 32x32 shared memory tiles. By what factor does tiling reduce HBM traffic?
common_mistake: Thinking tiling only helps with latency, not bandwidth. Engineers may also forget that in the naive kernel, each element is re-read once per output element in its row/column, leading to massive redundant traffic.
realistic_solution: Naive kernel: To compute one element of C, you load one full row of A (4096 elements) and one full column of B (4096 elements). For all 4096x4096 = 16.7M output elements, total loads = 16.7M x (4096 + 4096) = 137 billion element loads = 137B x 4 bytes = 549 GB from HBM. Tiled kernel with 32x32 tiles: The 4096-length inner dimension is split into 4096/32 = 128 tiles. Each tile of A (32x32) is loaded once per 32-column block of C, meaning each A tile is loaded 4096/32 = 128 times total. Total A loads: 128 x 128 x 32 x 32 = 16.7M elements. Same for B. Total: 33.6M x 4 bytes = 134 MB. Alternativel
napkin_math: Naive HBM reads: 2 x N^3 = 2 x 4096^3 = 137B elements = 549 GB. Tiled (tile=32): 2 x N^3 / T = 2 x 4096^3 / 32 = 4.29B elements = 17.2 GB. Reduction: 549 / 17.2 = 32x. More precisely, the reduction factor equals the tile dimension T = 32. At 3.35 TB/s HBM bandwidth: naive takes 164ms for loads alone; tiled takes 5.1ms.

title: The Register Pressure Spill
scenario: After aggressively loop-unrolling a custom Triton kernel for a fused attention operation, you observe that the kernel's execution time actually increased by 40% despite reducing instruction count. The profiler shows a dramatic increase in 'local memory' traffic. What happened, and why did an optimization that reduces instructions make the kernel slower?
common_mistake: Assuming fewer instructions always means faster execution. On CPUs this is usually true because register files are small but caches are large and fast. On GPUs, the register file is the fast memory, and 'local memory' (the spill destination) is actually in slow HBM.
realistic_solution: Loop unrolling increased the number of live variables, pushing register usage beyond the SM's per-thread register budget. When a kernel exceeds the available registers, the compiler 'spills' excess variables to 'local memory' — which despite its name lives in HBM (the slowest level of the GPU memory hierarchy). Each spilled register access now costs ~300ns instead of ~1ns (a 300x penalty per access). The 40% slowdown occurs because the bandwidth savings from fewer instructions is overwhelmed by the latency and bandwidth cost of spilling to HBM. The fix: reduce unroll factor, use fewer accumula
napkin_math: Before unrolling: 48 registers per thread, zero spills. After unrolling: 96 registers requested, SM has 65,536 registers. At 256 threads per block, the block needs 96 x 256 = 24,576 registers — within budget. But occupancy drops from 4 blocks to 2 blocks (24,576 x 2 = 49,152 < 65,536). If actually spilling: each spill/fill is 4 bytes to HBM. At 1,000 spills per thread per kernel invocation: 1,000 x 4 bytes x 2 (read+write) = 8 KB per thread of HBM traffic. For 256 threads: 2 MB of additional HBM traffic per block, at 3.35 TB/s = 0.6 microseconds extra — but the latency (300ns per access, seria

title: The Flash Attention Tiling Insight
scenario: Standard attention computes $\text{Softmax}(QK^T)V$ by first materializing the full $N \times N$ attention matrix in HBM (where $N$ is sequence length), then applying softmax, then multiplying by $V$. Flash Attention achieves 2-4x speedup without approximation. It never materializes the full attention matrix. Explain the tiling strategy that makes this possible — specifically, how does Flash Attention compute an exact softmax without having the full row of scores available at once? What memory hierarchy level does it exploit?
common_mistake: Thinking Flash Attention is an approximation (like sparse attention or linear attention). It computes the exact same result. Another common mistake is saying it just 'uses shared memory' without explaining the online softmax trick that makes tiling possible.
realistic_solution: Flash Attention tiles the attention computation along the key/value sequence dimension. For each tile of K and V loaded into SRAM (shared memory), it computes partial QK^T scores and partial softmax statistics (running max and running sum of exponentials). The key algorithmic insight is the 'online softmax' (Milakov & Gimelshein, 2018): you can compute softmax incrementally by maintaining a running maximum $m$ and a running normalizer $\ell$. When processing a new tile, you rescale previous partial results by $e^{m_{old} - m_{new}}$ and accumulate. This produces the mathematically exact softma
napkin_math: For sequence length N=8192, head dim d=128, FP16: Standard attention HBM traffic: write $N^2$ = 67M entries x 2 bytes = 128 MB for the attention matrix alone, plus reads for softmax and V multiply. Total: ~384 MB per head. Flash Attention HBM traffic: read Q, K, V once each = 3 x 8192 x 128 x 2 bytes = 6 MB, write O once = 2 MB. Total: ~8 MB per head. Reduction: 384/8 = 48x less HBM traffic. At 3.35 TB/s: standard = 115 microseconds (memory alone); Flash = 2.4 microseconds. The kernel is now compute-bound rather than memory-bound.

title: The Kernel Launch Overhead Wall
scenario: You are profiling a transformer inference pipeline and notice that the GPU is idle 60% of the time despite running a compute-heavy model. The Nsight Systems trace shows hundreds of tiny kernels (LayerNorm, GELU, bias-add, residual connections) each taking 5-20 microseconds of GPU time, but separated by 10-15 microsecond gaps. The total kernel launch overhead exceeds the total kernel compute time. How do you eliminate this overhead, and what are the tradeoffs of each approach?
common_mistake: Suggesting 'use a bigger GPU' or 'increase batch size.' A bigger GPU makes each kernel even shorter, worsening the launch overhead ratio. Increasing batch size helps but doesn't address the fundamental architectural issue of hundreds of tiny dispatches.
realistic_solution: There are three complementary approaches, each with different tradeoffs: (1) Kernel fusion: Merge sequential element-wise operations (LayerNorm + GELU + bias-add) into a single kernel. This eliminates intermediate HBM writes and launch gaps. Tradeoff: custom kernel development cost, harder to debug, less modular code. (2) CUDA Graphs: Capture the entire sequence of kernel launches once, then replay the graph with a single CPU dispatch. This reduces per-kernel CPU-side overhead from ~10 microseconds to ~0.5 microseconds. Tradeoff: the graph is static — you cannot change tensor shapes, control f
napkin_math: 200 kernels per forward pass x 12 microsecond average launch gap = 2.4 ms of overhead. 200 kernels x 10 microsecond average compute = 2.0 ms of useful work. GPU utilization: 2.0 / (2.0 + 2.4) = 45%. With CUDA Graphs (0.5 microsecond launch): overhead = 0.1 ms. Utilization: 2.0 / 2.1 = 95%. With fusion (50 kernels instead of 200, 40 microsecond average): 50 x 12 = 0.6 ms overhead, 50 x 40 = 2.0 ms compute. Utilization: 2.0 / 2.6 = 77%. Combined fusion + CUDA Graphs: 50 x 0.5 = 0.025 ms overhead. Utilization: 2.0 / 2.025 = 99%.

title: The Occupancy vs IPC Tradeoff
scenario: Two versions of a matrix multiply kernel target an H100 SM. Version A achieves 100% occupancy (2,048 threads resident) using 32 registers per thread and small 16x16 tiles. Version B achieves only 25% occupancy (512 threads) using 128 registers per thread and large 64x64 tiles. Counterintuitively, Version B runs 2x faster despite having 4x fewer resident threads. Explain why higher occupancy did not translate to higher performance, and when you should intentionally sacrifice occupancy.
