Total demand: 40 GB/s — within budget. BUT: training also saturates host DRAM bandwidth (DDR5: ~200 GB/s per socket). Training preprocessing: ~120 GB/s. Inference preprocessing: ~30 GB/s. Total: 150 GB/s — within 200 GB/s budget but at 75% utilization. Queueing at DRAM controllers: 60% latency increase is consistent with memory controller congestion at high utilization.

title: The Opportunistic Training Checkpoint Race
scenario: Your cluster runs high-priority inference serving alongside opportunistic (preemptible) training jobs that backfill idle capacity. When a traffic spike triggers autoscaling, the scheduler must preempt training jobs to free GPUs for inference. But training jobs take 15 minutes to checkpoint a 70B model, and inference needs GPUs within 60 seconds to meet SLA. Three times this week, training jobs were killed mid-checkpoint, losing 4 hours of work each time. How do you redesign the preemption protocol?
common_mistake: Setting a long preemption grace period (e.g., 20 minutes) to allow checkpointing. This violates the inference SLA — you cannot wait 20 minutes for GPUs during a traffic spike.
realistic_solution: The solution requires decoupling checkpoint frequency from preemption response time. Three-pronged approach: (1) **Proactive checkpointing**: opportunistic jobs must checkpoint frequently enough that maximum lost work is bounded (e.g., every 30 minutes, limiting loss to 30 min). (2) **Tiered preemption signal**: send a 'warning' signal 5-10 minutes before hard preemption, allowing the job to initiate an emergency checkpoint. Use inference traffic prediction (time-of-day patterns) to send warnings proactively. (3) **Fast checkpointing**: use async checkpointing to NVMe (4 GB/s per drive) instea
napkin_math: 70B model checkpoint at FP16: 140 GB. Network storage (1 Gbps = 125 MB/s): 140 GB / 125 MB/s = 1,120 seconds ≈ 19 minutes. Local NVMe (4 GB/s): 140 GB / 4 GB/s = 35 seconds. Async checkpoint to NVMe + background upload: preemption in 35 seconds + 0 lost GPU time for inference. Lost work per kill without checkpointing: 4 hours × 64 GPUs × $2/hr = $512. With 30-min checkpoint interval: max loss = 0.5hr × 64 × $2 = $64 — 8× reduction.

title: The Power Density Ceiling
scenario: Your datacenter has 10 MW total power capacity. You have 2,000 H100 GPUs (700W TDP each) plus associated CPUs, networking, and cooling. During a large training run, the GPUs hit sustained peak power. The datacenter trips a breaker, shutting down an entire power distribution unit (PDU) and killing 25% of your cluster. The facilities team says you can never run all 2,000 GPUs at max TDP simultaneously. How does the scheduler need to account for power as a schedulable resource, and what is the maximum number of GPUs you can realistically schedule at peak?
common_mistake: Treating power as infinite or assuming GPUs always draw TDP. In reality, power is a hard physical constraint that the scheduler must manage alongside GPU count, memory, and network.
realistic_solution: Power must become a first-class schedulable resource, like GPUs and memory. The scheduler needs a power budget per rack, per PDU, and per datacenter. Step 1: Calculate true power budget. 10 MW total, with PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) of 1.3 = 10/1.3 = 7.7 MW for IT equipment. Networking, storage, CPUs consume ~1.5 MW. Remaining for GPUs: ~6.2 MW. Step 2: Power-aware scheduling. Each job declares or the scheduler estimates its GPU power draw profile. Training at peak: ~700W/GPU. Inference at average: ~300W/GPU. Mixed workloads can pack more GPUs under the same power envelope. Step 3: Impleme
napkin_math: Available GPU power: 6.2 MW. At 700W TDP per H100: 6.2 MW / 700W = 8,857 — but you only have 2,000 GPUs. Headroom: 6.2MW / 2000 = 3.1 kW/GPU? No — the 10 MW is total with PUE. Real GPU power budget: 6.2 MW. 2000 × 700W = 1.4 MW for GPUs at peak. That fits! But the PDU trip suggests the power is distributed: each PDU might serve 500 GPUs = 350 kW, while the PDU breaker is rated at 300 kW. 500 × 700W = 350 kW > 300 kW breaker. Solution: cap at 428 GPUs per PDU at peak (300kW / 700W), or power-cap to 600W (500 × 600W = 300 kW). Total schedulable at peak without capping: 4 PDUs × 428 = 1,712 GPUs

title: The Heterogeneous Workload Placement Matrix
scenario: You manage a fleet with 3 GPU types: 500 H100s (80GB HBM3, 3.35 TB/s), 300 A100s (80GB HBM2e, 2 TB/s), and 200 L4s (24GB GDDR6, 300 GB/s). Three workload classes compete for resources: (1) LLM pre-training (massive compute, needs NVLink), (2) 7B model inference serving (memory-bandwidth-bound, latency-sensitive), (3) batch embedding generation (throughput-oriented, not latency-sensitive). Design the placement policy that maximizes fleet-wide cost-efficiency. Which workload goes on which GPU type, and why?
common_mistake: Putting everything on H100s because they're the fastest. This maximizes per-job speed but destroys cost-efficiency — H100s cost 3-4× more than L4s. Using H100s for embedding generation is like using a Ferrari for grocery delivery.
realistic_solution: Match workload characteristics to GPU capabilities based on the binding resource: (1) **LLM pre-training → H100s**: Pre-training is compute-bound with massive matmuls that exploit Tensor Cores and needs multi-GPU NVLink for AllReduce. Only H100s have the FLOPS and NVLink connectivity. (2) **7B inference serving → A100s**: 7B model decode is memory-bandwidth-bound (low arithmetic intensity). A100 provides 2 TB/s at lower cost-per-bandwidth than H100 (H100's extra FLOPS go unused). The 80GB VRAM fits the model with room for large KV caches. (3) **Batch embedding generation → L4s**: Embedding gen
napkin_math: Cost per inference token: H100 at $3/hr, 200 tok/s for 7B = $0.0042/1K tok. A100 at $1.50/hr, 150 tok/s = $0.0028/1K tok. L4 can't serve 7B (24GB < 14GB weights + KV cache for reasonable batch). A100 is 33% cheaper per token. Cost per embedding: H100 at $3/hr, 5000 emb/s = $0.00017/emb. L4 at $0.50/hr, 1500 emb/s = $0.000093/emb. L4 is 45% cheaper per embedding. Annual savings from proper placement vs all-H100: assume 500 A100-equivalent inference hours + 200 L4-equivalent embedding hours daily. Savings: (500 × $1.50 + 200 × $2.50) × 365 = ~$456K/year.

title: The GPU Fragmentation Death Spiral
scenario: Your 1,000-GPU cluster shows 85% allocation but only 55% actual compute utilization. Investigation reveals that many jobs requested 8 GPUs but only use 4 at any given time (the other 4 idle during data loading or preprocessing). Meanwhile, a queue of 4-GPU jobs is growing because the scheduler cannot find 4 contiguous free GPUs — they are scattered as single idle GPUs across half-allocated nodes. Utilization is low, allocation is high, and everyone is waiting. Diagnose this 'fragmentation death spiral' and propose a multi-level fix.
common_mistake: Blaming users for over-requesting resources. While this is partially true, the real problem is systemic — the scheduler has no mechanism to detect, defragment, or prevent this pattern.
realistic_solution: This is resource fragmentation — the GPU equivalent of memory fragmentation. Three-level fix: (1) **Admission control with right-sizing**: monitor actual GPU utilization per job and provide feedback ('Your job used 4.2 of 8 GPUs on average — consider requesting 5'). Reject grossly over-provisioned requests. (2) **Compaction/defragmentation**: periodically migrate (checkpoint → reschedule) jobs to consolidate free GPUs onto whole nodes. This is expensive (~5-10 min per migration) but creates large contiguous blocks. (3) **Topology-aware packing**: modify the scheduler to prefer filling nodes co
napkin_math: 1,000 GPUs at 85% allocation = 850 allocated, 150 free. But 150 free GPUs are spread as 1-2 per node across 100 nodes. A 4-GPU job needs 4 contiguous GPUs on one node — there are 0 such blocks available despite 150 free GPUs. After defragmentation: consolidate to 37 fully-free 4-GPU blocks (150/4 = 37). Queue clears. Fragmentation cost: 150 idle GPUs × $2/hr = $300/hr = $7,200/day = $2.6M/year wasted.

title: The Multi-Tenant Starvation Cascade
scenario: You are the Staff engineer responsible for the GPU scheduling platform serving 20 ML teams at your company. The current system uses fair-share scheduling with per-team quotas. Three problems have converged into a crisis: (1) Team A (search ranking) consistently uses 120% of their quota by borrowing idle capacity, and now claims they cannot give it back without missing launch deadlines. (2) Team B (NLP research) has a 256-GPU training job that has been queued for 3 days because their fair share is only 50 GPUs and no preemption mechanism exists for borrowed capacity. (3) Team C (ads inference)
common_mistake: Proposing simple priority queues or hard quotas. Hard quotas waste resources (teams cannot borrow idle capacity), and simple priorities just shift the starvation to a different team. Buying more hardware is not a scheduling solution — fragmentation and policy failures persist at any scale.
realistic_solution: Implement a three-tier scheduling architecture: **Tier 1 — Guaranteed quota with lending**: Each team gets a hard minimum (guaranteed capacity they can always reclaim). Team A: 100 GPUs guaranteed. Team B: 50 GPUs guaranteed. Team C: 80 GPUs guaranteed. Total guaranteed < total cluster (e.g., 800 of 1000) — the gap is the lending pool. **Tier 2 — Opportunistic borrowing with preemption contracts**: Teams can borrow from other teams' idle quota and from the lending pool. Borrowed capacity is preemptible with a 30-minute warning. Team A's 20 extra GPUs above quota are preemptible — Team B's 256-
napkin_math: Cluster: 1,000 GPUs. Guaranteed: A=100, B=50, C=80, others=470. Total guaranteed=700. Lending pool=300. Current state: A uses 120 (100 guaranteed + 20 borrowed). B needs 256 but has 50. Resolution: preempt A's 20 borrowed → A has 100. Team B gets 50 guaranteed + 206 from lending pool (300 available - others). Total for B: 256. Time to preempt: 30-min checkpoint warning. Time for B to start: ~35 minutes (vs 3 days). Team C Black Friday: reserve 200 GPUs for 48 hours. Cost of reservation: 200 × 48 × $2 = $19,200. Revenue protected: $50M+ (Black Friday ad revenue).

title: The Liquid-Cooled Scheduling Frontier
scenario: Your company is deploying next-generation B200 GPUs (1,000W TDP each) alongside the existing H100 fleet (700W TDP). The new B200 racks require direct-to-chip liquid cooling, but only 30% of your datacenter floor has liquid cooling infrastructure (the rest is legacy air-cooled). You have 500 B200s (liquid-cooled zone) and 2,000 H100s (air-cooled zone). A critical 1,000-GPU LLM pre-training job needs the fastest possible time-to-completion. The B200s are 2x faster per GPU for training. Design the scheduling and placement strategy. Can you use both GPU types in a single training job? What are the
common_mistake: Mixing B200 and H100 GPUs in a single data-parallel training job. Heterogeneous GPU types in synchronous data parallelism create a straggler problem — every iteration runs at the speed of the slowest GPU. The B200s would idle 50% of the time waiting for H100s.
realistic_solution: You cannot naively mix GPU types in synchronous training. Three strategies, evaluated by time-to-completion: **Option 1 — B200-only (500 GPUs)**: Use all 500 B200s. Each B200 is 2x faster than H100, so effective throughput = 500 × 2 = 1,000 H100-equivalent. Time = T_base. **Option 2 — H100-only (1,000 GPUs)**: Use 1,000 H100s. More GPUs but slower each. Throughput = 1,000 H100-equivalent, but communication overhead increases with more nodes. Time ≈ T_base × 1.15 (15% communication overhead from 2x nodes). **Option 3 — Heterogeneous with async**: Use B200s for the primary training job (fast ite
napkin_math: Training throughput: B200 = 2x H100 per GPU. 500 B200s: 500 × 2 = 1,000 H100-eq FLOPS. Communication: 500 GPUs / 8 per node = 63 nodes. Ring AllReduce for 70B model (140 GB gradients): 140 GB / 400 Gbps IB × 2 × 62/63 ≈ 5.5 seconds. 1,000 H100s: 1,000 / 8 = 125 nodes. AllReduce: 140 GB / 400 Gbps × 2 × 124/125 ≈ 5.5 seconds (same bandwidth, but more nodes = more synchronization points). Compute per step: B200 at 500 GPUs ≈ 3.5 seconds. H100 at 1000 GPUs ≈ 3.5 seconds. Total per step: B200 fleet = 3.5 + 5.5 = 9.0 sec. H100 fleet = 3.5 + 5.5 + ~1.5 sec sync overhead = 10.5 sec. B200 fleet is ~15

title: The Storage Tier Latency Gap
scenario: A training cluster has four storage tiers: HBM on the GPU, host DRAM, local NVMe SSDs, and a parallel filesystem (Lustre) over the network. Roughly how many orders of magnitude separate the access latency of the fastest tier (HBM) from the slowest (Lustre)? Why does this gap matter for ML training?
common_mistake: Assuming the gap is small, like 10-100x. Engineers who have only worked with single-machine setups often underestimate how catastrophic a remote filesystem read is compared to on-chip memory access.
realistic_solution: The gap spans roughly 5-6 orders of magnitude. HBM access latency is ~300ns, host DRAM is ~100ns but accessed via PCIe at ~1-2us effective, local NVMe is ~10-100us, and a Lustre read over the network is ~1-10ms. From HBM (~300ns) to Lustre (~3ms), that is approximately 10,000x or ~4 orders of magnitude. If we include L1/L2 cache at ~1ns, the full hierarchy spans ~6 orders of magnitude. This matters because any training step that stalls on a remote filesystem read pays a latency penalty thousands of times larger than a local memory access, making storage tiering essential for keeping accelerato
napkin_math: L1 cache: ~1ns. HBM: ~300ns. DRAM (via PCIe): ~2us. NVMe SSD: ~50us. Lustre over network: ~3ms. Ratio of slowest to fastest (Lustre vs L1): $3 \times 10^6$ — six orders of magnitude. Even HBM to Lustre: $3 \times 10^6 / 300 = 10{,}000\times$.

title: The Lustre Stripe Throughput
scenario: A Lustre parallel filesystem has 100 Object Storage Targets (OSTs), each capable of 1 GB/s sequential read throughput. A training job reads a single large file that has been striped across 50 of these OSTs. What is the maximum aggregate read throughput the job can achieve from this file? What happens if the file is stored on only 1 OST?
common_mistake: Assuming you get the full 100 GB/s aggregate bandwidth regardless of how the file is striped. A file can only read in parallel from the OSTs it actually spans.
realistic_solution: Maximum throughput is 50 GB/s, because the file is striped across 50 OSTs, each contributing up to 1 GB/s. If the file were stored on a single OST, throughput would be limited to 1 GB/s — a 50x reduction. This is why parallel filesystems stripe large files across many targets: it converts a sequential I/O bottleneck into a parallel one. Conversely, millions of small files that each land on a single OST create a metadata and throughput bottleneck, which is the 'small file problem' in ML data pipelines.
napkin_math: 50 OSTs $\times$ 1 GB/s per OST = 50 GB/s aggregate. Single OST: 1 GB/s. To feed 1,000 GPUs each needing 50 MB/s of training data: $1{,}000 \times 50\text{ MB/s} = 50\text{ GB/s}$ — exactly the capacity of the 50-OST stripe. Under-striping would starve the cluster.

title: The Checkpoint Bandwidth Bottleneck
scenario: You are training a 175B parameter model across 4,096 GPUs using FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel). Each GPU holds a shard of approximately 43M parameters. A full checkpoint must save all optimizer states (2x the model size for Adam) plus the model weights, totaling roughly 1 TB. The parallel filesystem provides 200 GB/s aggregate write bandwidth. How long does a synchronous checkpoint take, and what fraction of training time is lost if you checkpoint every 30 minutes?
common_mistake: Only counting model weight size (350 GB for FP16) and forgetting optimizer states. Adam stores first and second moment estimates, tripling the checkpoint size. Another mistake is assuming each GPU writes independently without considering aggregate filesystem bandwidth as the binding constraint.
realistic_solution: Total checkpoint size: 175B params × 2 bytes (FP16 weights) + 175B × 4 bytes (FP32 master weights) + 175B × 4 bytes (first moment) + 175B × 4 bytes (second moment) = 350 GB + 700 GB + 700 GB = ~1.75 TB. At 200 GB/s aggregate write bandwidth: $1{,}750 \text{ GB} / 200 \text{ GB/s} = 8.75$ seconds — but only if all 4,096 GPUs can write concurrently without contention. In practice, metadata overhead and lock contention inflate this to 15-30 seconds. At 30-minute intervals, a 30-second checkpoint costs $30/1800 = 1.7\%$ of training time. This seems small, but at $2/GPU-hour across 4,096 GPUs, 1.7%
napkin_math: Checkpoint size $\approx 1.75$ TB. PFS write BW = 200 GB/s. Time = $1{,}750/200 \approx 8.75$s (ideal). Real-world with contention: ~20s. Fraction lost = $20/1800 \approx 1.1\%$. Cost at \$2/GPU-hr, 4096 GPUs: \$2 $\times$ 4096 $\times$ 24 $\times$ 0.011 = \$2,163/day wasted on checkpoint stalls.

title: The NVMe Warm Cache Miss
scenario: Your training cluster uses a two-tier storage architecture: a local NVMe SSD cache (3.84 TB per node, 6 GB/s read) and a shared Lustre parallel filesystem (500 GB/s aggregate). The training dataset is 50 TB. Each node processes a random shard of the data each epoch. During the first epoch, training throughput drops by 40% compared to subsequent epochs. Why?
common_mistake: Blaming the GPU or the data loading library. The throughput drop is not a compute problem — it is a cold cache problem in the storage hierarchy.
realistic_solution: During the first epoch, none of the training data is cached on local NVMe. Every data access must be served from the remote Lustre filesystem at network speed. Even at 500 GB/s aggregate, each node's share of that bandwidth is limited by its network link (typically 100-200 Gbps = 12.5-25 GB/s). After the first epoch, the most recently accessed shards are cached on local NVMe, and subsequent reads hit the 6 GB/s local SSD instead of the remote filesystem. The 40% throughput drop in epoch 1 reflects the latency difference between NVMe (~50us) and Lustre over network (~3ms), which stalls the data
napkin_math: Per node: local NVMe read = 6 GB/s at ~50us latency. Remote Lustre via 200 Gbps link = 25 GB/s peak, but ~3ms latency per access. If the data loader issues 1,000 random reads/sec, NVMe: $1{,}000 \times 50\mu s = 50$ms of total I/O wait. Lustre: $1{,}000 \times 3$ms = 3s of I/O wait per second — the pipeline is I/O-bound 100% of the time. Effective GPU utilization drops from ~85% to ~50%, explaining the 40% throughput loss.

title: The Object Store Training Anti-Pattern
scenario: A team stores their 10 TB training dataset as 50 million individual JPEG files in an S3-compatible object store. During training, each GPU worker fetches individual files randomly. They observe that data loading saturates at 500 samples/sec per worker despite having 100 Gbps network links, and the S3 bill is astronomical. What is the root cause, and how should they restructure the data?
common_mistake: Blaming network bandwidth. 100 Gbps = 12.5 GB/s, and at 200 KB per JPEG, 500 samples/sec is only 100 MB/s — nowhere near saturating the link. The bottleneck is not bandwidth but request overhead.
realistic_solution: Object stores like S3 have high per-request latency (~20-50ms for a GET) and charge per API call. At 500 samples/sec, the worker is issuing 500 HTTP GET requests per second. Each request incurs DNS resolution, TLS handshake (amortized), HTTP overhead, and S3's internal metadata lookup. The 20ms per-request latency with 500 concurrent requests saturates the client's connection pool, not the bandwidth. The fix: pack training samples into large sequential files (TFRecord, WebDataset tar files, or Parquet). A single 1 GB tar file containing 5,000 samples requires 1 GET request instead of 5,000, re
napkin_math: 50M files $\times$ \$0.0004 per GET = \$20,000 per full epoch just in API costs. At 3 epochs: \$60,000 in S3 GET fees alone. After packing into 10,000 tar files: 10,000 $\times$ \$0.0004 = \$4 per epoch. Throughput with packed files: one 1 GB sequential read at 12.5 GB/s = 80ms for 5,000 samples = 62,500 samples/sec — a 125$\times$ improvement.

title: The Write Amplification Tax
scenario: Your training infrastructure uses local NVMe SSDs as a staging tier for checkpoints before asynchronously flushing to a parallel filesystem. Each checkpoint is 2 TB. After six months, the SSDs are failing at 3x the expected rate. The vendor says the drives have exceeded their rated write endurance. How is a 2 TB checkpoint causing more than 2 TB of physical writes to the SSD?
common_mistake: Assuming 2 TB of logical writes equals 2 TB of physical writes. Engineers who haven't worked with flash storage often ignore write amplification entirely.
realistic_solution: Flash SSDs cannot overwrite data in place. They must erase entire blocks (typically 256 KB - 4 MB) before writing new data. When a 2 TB checkpoint is written to an SSD that is already 80% full, the SSD's flash translation layer (FTL) must perform garbage collection: reading valid pages from partially-used blocks, erasing the block, and writing the valid pages back along with the new data. This 'write amplification factor' (WAF) means 1 logical write can cause 3-10x physical writes depending on drive fullness and write pattern. At WAF = 4x, each 2 TB checkpoint causes 8 TB of physical writes. C
napkin_math: Checkpoint = 2 TB logical. WAF at 80% capacity $\approx 4\times$. Physical writes per checkpoint = 8 TB. Checkpoints per day at 30-min interval = 48. Physical writes/day = $48 \times 8 = 384$ TB. Drive endurance: 3.84 TB drive $\times$ 1 DWPD = 3.84 TB/day rated. Actual load: $384 / 3.84 = 100\times$ over-provisioned. Fix: keep SSD at <50% capacity (reduces WAF to ~2x), use overprovisioned drives (3+ DWPD), or checkpoint to DRAM-backed tmpfs first.

title: The Metadata Storm
scenario: You manage a 10,000-GPU training cluster. At the start of each training run, all 10,000 workers simultaneously open and stat their data shards on a Lustre filesystem. The metadata server (MDS) becomes unresponsive for 2-3 minutes, delaying job startup for the entire cluster. Once training begins, data throughput is fine. The MDS has 256 GB of RAM and 24 CPU cores. Why does this 'metadata storm' happen, and how do you fix it without upgrading the MDS hardware?
common_mistake: Assuming the problem is data throughput. The MDS does not serve data — it serves metadata (file paths, permissions, stripe layout). The bottleneck is metadata operations per second (IOPS), not bandwidth.
realistic_solution: Lustre separates metadata (handled by MDS) from data (handled by OSTs). When 10,000 workers simultaneously issue open() + stat() + read-stripe-layout for their data files, the MDS must handle 10,000+ metadata operations in a burst. Each operation requires a lock on the directory entry, a lookup in the inode table, and a response. Lustre's MDS processes roughly 50,000-200,000 metadata ops/sec depending on configuration. A 10,000-node stampede issuing 3-5 ops each (open, stat, readdir, getattr) creates 30,000-50,000 concurrent metadata requests. If these all target the same directory, directory
napkin_math: 10,000 workers $\times$ 4 metadata ops each = 40,000 ops in a burst. MDS capacity: ~100,000 ops/sec (cached). But single-directory lock contention reduces effective throughput to ~5,000 ops/sec under contention. Time to drain: $40{,}000 / 5{,}000 = 8$s — plus TCP backoff and retries from clients that timed out, creating a feedback loop that extends the storm to 2-3 minutes. With jittered startup over 30s: peak rate = $40{,}000 / 30 \approx 1{,}333$ ops/sec — well within MDS capacity.

title: The Checkpoint Tiering Strategy
scenario: You are designing the checkpoint system for a 100K-GPU training run of a 1 trillion parameter model. A full checkpoint is ~18 TB. The cluster has three storage tiers: local NVMe (6 GB/s per node, 3.84 TB each), a Lustre parallel filesystem (2 TB/s aggregate), and S3-compatible object storage (effectively unlimited capacity, 100 GB/s aggregate). Using Young-Daly, your optimal checkpoint interval is 12 minutes. Design a tiered checkpoint strategy that keeps GPU idle time under 10 seconds per checkpoint.
common_mistake: Trying to write the full 18 TB synchronously to Lustre every 12 minutes. At 2 TB/s, that is 9 seconds of synchronous I/O — barely under budget, with no margin for jitter. And it saturates the filesystem for the entire cluster, blocking data loading for other jobs.
realistic_solution: Use a three-phase tiered checkpoint pipeline: Phase 1 (synchronous, <2 seconds): Each GPU writes its local shard to host DRAM (memcpy). With FSDP, each GPU's shard is ~180 MB (18 TB / 100K GPUs). A GPU-to-host memcpy at 32 GB/s takes ~6ms. This is the only phase that blocks training. Phase 2 (asynchronous, ~30 seconds): A background thread on each node flushes the DRAM buffer to local NVMe. 180 MB at 6 GB/s = 30ms per GPU, but multiple GPUs per node share the NVMe, so ~240ms per 8-GPU node. Phase 3 (asynchronous, ~3 minutes): A background daemon aggregates per-node NVMe checkpoints and streams
napkin_math: Per-GPU shard: $18\text{ TB} / 100{,}000 = 180\text{ MB}$. Phase 1 (GPU→DRAM): $180\text{ MB} / 32\text{ GB/s} = 5.6\text{ms}$ + barrier sync $\approx 2\text{s}$. Phase 2 (DRAM→NVMe, async): $180\text{ MB} \times 8\text{ GPUs/node} / 6\text{ GB/s} = 240\text{ms}$. Phase 3 (NVMe→Lustre, async): $18\text{ TB} / 2\text{ TB/s} = 9\text{s}$ — fits within the 12-min interval. Training blocked: $\approx 2$s. Overhead: $2/720 = 0.28\%$.

title: The I/O Wall at Scale
scenario: A team scales their training job from 256 GPUs to 4,096 GPUs. Per-GPU throughput (samples/sec/GPU) drops by 35%. Profiling shows GPU compute utilization has dropped from 82% to 53%, with large gaps between training steps. The network interconnect shows only 40% utilization. The data pipeline uses a shared Lustre filesystem with 500 GB/s aggregate bandwidth. What is happening?
common_mistake: Blaming the network interconnect because 'scaling problems must be network problems.' The interconnect is underutilized — the bottleneck is upstream of the network, in the storage layer.
realistic_solution: This is the 'I/O wall' — storage bandwidth becomes the binding constraint before network or compute. At 256 GPUs, each GPU's data demand might be 100 MB/s (for a vision model with large images). Total demand: $256 \times 100 = 25.6$ GB/s — well within Lustre's 500 GB/s. At 4,096 GPUs: $4{,}096 \times 100 = 409.6$ GB/s — approaching 82% of Lustre's capacity. But Lustre's effective bandwidth degrades non-linearly under heavy load due to lock contention, metadata overhead, and OST hotspots. At 80%+ utilization, read latency spikes 3-5x, creating data pipeline stalls. The GPUs finish their compute
napkin_math: Per-GPU data rate: 100 MB/s. At 256 GPUs: 25.6 GB/s demand / 500 GB/s capacity = 5% utilization — no contention. At 4,096 GPUs: 409.6 GB/s / 500 GB/s = 82% utilization. Lustre at 80%+ load: effective latency increases ~3x (from 3ms to ~10ms per read). Data loader stall per step: $\Delta t_{\text{stall}} \approx 7\text{ms} \times 100\text{ reads/step} = 700\text{ms}$. Training step compute time: ~1.5s. With stalls: $1.5 + 0.7 = 2.2$s. Throughput drop: $(2.2 - 1.5) / 2.2 \approx 32\%$ — matches the observed 35%.

title: The Erasure Coding Overhead
scenario: Your storage team proposes switching checkpoint storage from 3-way replication (3x storage overhead) to Reed-Solomon erasure coding (8+3, meaning 8 data chunks + 3 parity chunks). This would reduce storage overhead from 200% to 37.5%. However, the training team reports that after the switch, checkpoint write latency increased by 60% and they are missing their checkpoint time budget. Why does erasure coding hurt write performance, and when is the tradeoff worth it?
common_mistake: Thinking erasure coding is 'strictly better' than replication because it uses less storage. Engineers often forget that storage efficiency trades off directly against write performance and CPU overhead.
realistic_solution: With 3-way replication, a write is simply: send the same data to 3 storage targets in parallel. The write latency is determined by the slowest of the 3 targets — no computation required. With 8+3 erasure coding, the client must: (1) accumulate 8 chunks of data, (2) compute 3 parity chunks using Galois field arithmetic (CPU-intensive), (3) write all 11 chunks to 11 different storage targets, (4) wait for acknowledgment from all 11. The 60% latency increase comes from three sources: the encoding CPU cost (~10-15% of total), the need to coordinate writes to 11 targets instead of 3 (higher tail la
napkin_math: 3-way replication: write 2 TB to 3 targets in parallel. Latency $\approx \max(T_1, T_2, T_3) \approx$ 5s. Storage cost: $2 \times 3 = 6$ TB. 8+3 EC: compute parity (CPU: ~1s for 2 TB), write 2.75 TB across 11 targets. Latency $\approx$ encoding + $\max(T_1, \ldots, T_{11}) \approx$ 1 + 7 = 8s. Storage cost: $2 \times 1.375 = 2.75$ TB. Latency increase: $(8 - 5)/5 = 60\%$. Storage savings: $(6 - 2.75)/6 = 54\%$.

title: The Data Gravity Problem
scenario: Your organization trains models on an on-premise GPU cluster (2,048 H100s) but stores all training data in a cloud object store (S3) because it is cheaper and 'infinitely scalable.' The cluster connects to the cloud via a 400 Gbps dedicated link. A new multimodal training job requires 200 TB of image-text pairs. The team estimates 8 epochs over 2 weeks. After starting, they discover the job will take 6 weeks instead. Diagnose the bottleneck and propose a storage architecture that resolves the data gravity problem.
common_mistake: Trying to fix this by adding more network bandwidth. The fundamental issue is that 200 TB of data has 'gravitational pull' — it is cheaper to move compute to where the data lives than to continuously pipe data to where the compute lives.
realistic_solution: The 400 Gbps link provides 50 GB/s — respectable for one-time transfers, but catastrophic for iterative training. Each epoch reads the full 200 TB dataset. Over 8 epochs: $200 \times 8 = 1{,}600$ TB of data must traverse the link. At 50 GB/s: $1{,}600{,}000 \text{ GB} / 50 \text{ GB/s} = 32{,}000\text{s} = 8.9$ hours per epoch just for data transfer. But training compute per epoch might only be 4-5 hours — data transfer dominates. With contention, retries, and S3 GET latency, effective throughput drops to ~30 GB/s, pushing epoch data time to ~15 hours. The fix is a hierarchical caching archite
napkin_math: One-time S3→Lustre copy: $200\text{ TB} / 50\text{ GB/s} = 4{,}000\text{s} \approx 67\text{ min}$. Per-epoch from Lustre: $200\text{ TB} / 500\text{ GB/s} = 400\text{s} \approx 7\text{ min}$ aggregate (pipelined with compute, effectively hidden). Per-epoch from S3: $200\text{ TB} / 30\text{ GB/s} = 6{,}667\text{s} \approx 1.85\text{ hours}$ (not hideable — dominates). 8 epochs from S3: $8 \times 1.85 = 14.8$ hours of pure I/O wait. 8 epochs from Lustre: 0 hours (fully pipelined). The 67-minute one-time copy saves 14.8 hours of training time worth $\sim\$20{,}000$ in GPU-hours.

title: The Checkpoint Cascade Failure
scenario: A 50,000-GPU training run checkpoints every 15 minutes using asynchronous checkpoint-to-NVMe followed by background flush to Lustre. The Lustre filesystem has 1 TB/s aggregate write bandwidth. After 3 days of stable training, checkpoint writes suddenly start failing. NVMe drives on hundreds of nodes report 'no space left on device,' yet each drive is 3.84 TB and each checkpoint shard is only 200 MB. No other jobs are running on these nodes. What caused this cascade, and how do you prevent it?
common_mistake: Assuming a single checkpoint is the problem. At 200 MB per shard, even 20 checkpoints would only consume 4 GB. Engineers forget to account for the accumulation rate vs drain rate over time.
realistic_solution: This is a classic producer-consumer imbalance in the storage pipeline. Each checkpoint produces 200 MB per GPU on NVMe. With 8 GPUs per node, each checkpoint deposits 1.6 GB on the local NVMe. At 15-minute intervals, that is 6.4 GB/hour accumulating on NVMe. The background flush to Lustre must drain this at least as fast. The total cluster checkpoint size is $50{,}000 \times 200\text{ MB} = 10\text{ TB}$. At 1 TB/s Lustre write bandwidth, draining takes 10 seconds — easy. But the failure mode is subtle: Lustre bandwidth is shared. If any other I/O load (data loading, logging, other jobs' check
napkin_math: Per-node accumulation rate: $1.6\text{ GB} / 15\text{ min} = 6.4\text{ GB/hr}$. Per-node drain rate (ideal): same. If drain lags 5%: deficit = $0.08\text{ GB/checkpoint} \times 4\text{ checkpoints/hr} = 0.32\text{ GB/hr}$. After 72 hours: $72 \times 0.32 = 23\text{ GB}$ deficit. Meanwhile, if the system retains the last 3 checkpoints for safety: $3 \times 1.6 = 4.8\text{ GB}$ pinned. Total NVMe consumed: $23 + 4.8 = 27.8\text{ GB}$ — still fits. But if drain lag increases to 20% (Lustre under load): deficit = $0.32 \times 4 = 1.28\text{ GB/hr}$. After 72 hours: $72 \times 1.28 = 92\text{ GB}$

title: The I/O Jitter Amplification
scenario: A 16,000-GPU training job uses synchronous data parallelism. Each training step takes 2 seconds: 1.5s of compute and 0.5s of AllReduce. The data pipeline prefetches 3 batches ahead on each worker. Occasionally, one or two workers experience an I/O 'hiccup' where a Lustre read takes 500ms instead of the usual 50ms. When this happens, the entire training step — across all 16,000 GPUs — slows down by 450ms. Explain the amplification mechanism and propose a fix.
common_mistake: Thinking the I/O delay only affects the slow worker. 'It is just one worker out of 16,000 — the impact should be negligible.' This ignores the synchronization barrier inherent in data-parallel training.
realistic_solution: In synchronous data parallelism, every GPU must complete its forward + backward pass before the AllReduce can begin (or the next step can start, depending on the overlap strategy). When one worker's data pipeline stalls for 500ms, its compute start is delayed by 500ms - 50ms = 450ms (above normal). The prefetch buffer absorbs typical jitter — with 3 batches at 2s each, the buffer covers 6 seconds of I/O delay. But the buffer only helps if the stall is *isolated*. If the Lustre hiccup affects a busy OST that serves data to dozens of workers, multiple workers stall simultaneously, draining the p
napkin_math: Single I/O stall cost: 450ms $\times$ 16,000 GPUs = 7,200 GPU-seconds = 2 GPU-hours wasted per incident. At \$2/GPU-hour: \$4 per stall. If stalls occur 10 times per hour: \$40/hour = \$960/day = \$350K/year in wasted compute — from storage jitter alone. Increasing prefetch from 3→8 batches costs $5 \times 2\text{s} \times 100\text{ MB/s} = 1\text{ GB}$ additional DRAM per worker — trivial cost to eliminate \$350K/year waste.

title: The 100K-GPU Storage Architecture
scenario: You are the Principal Storage Architect for a new 100,000-GPU training cluster that will train frontier models (1T+ parameters). The cluster will run continuous pre-training jobs lasting 3-6 months. Your requirements: (1) feed 100K GPUs at 200 MB/s each = 20 TB/s aggregate read bandwidth for training data, (2) checkpoint 20 TB every 10 minutes with <5 seconds of GPU idle time, (3) survive up to 50 simultaneous node failures without losing more than 30 seconds of training progress, (4) total storage budget is $15M for 3 years. Design the complete storage hierarchy.
common_mistake: Designing a single monolithic storage tier (e.g., a massive Lustre filesystem). No single tier can simultaneously provide 20 TB/s read throughput, fast checkpoint writes, and cost-effective capacity. Another mistake is ignoring the failure recovery path — checkpoints are useless if you cannot read them back fast enough to resume training within the MTBF window.
realistic_solution: The architecture requires four tiers, each optimized for a different access pattern: Tier 0 — Host DRAM (synchronous checkpoint staging): Each of the 12,500 nodes has 1-2 TB of DRAM. Checkpoint path: GPU→DRAM memcpy takes <1s (200 MB/shard at 32 GB/s). This is the only synchronous operation. Total DRAM capacity: ~15 PB across the cluster. Tier 1 — Local NVMe (async checkpoint + data cache): 4× 3.84 TB NVMe per node = 15.36 TB/node, 192 PB aggregate. Serves two roles: async checkpoint staging (DRAM→NVMe flush in background) and warm cache for training data. NVMe read: 25 GB/s per node → 312 TB/
napkin_math: Data feed: 100K GPUs $\times$ 200 MB/s = 20 TB/s. NVMe aggregate: 12,500 nodes $\times$ 25 GB/s = 312 TB/s — 15× headroom. Checkpoint flow: Phase 1 (sync, GPU→DRAM): 200 MB at 32 GB/s = 6ms. Phase 2 (async, DRAM→NVMe): 200 MB at 6 GB/s = 33ms. Phase 3 (async, NVMe→Lustre): 20 TB at 4 TB/s = 5s (background). GPU idle: <2s (Phase 1 + barrier). Recovery from 50-node failure: redistribute shards from surviving DRAM copies (FSDP redundancy) or read from Lustre: 20 TB at 4 TB/s = 5s. Resume training: <30s total.

title: The Storage Disaggregation Dilemma
scenario: You are advising a hyperscaler on their next-generation AI training cluster architecture. The current design uses 'converged' nodes where each GPU server has local NVMe SSDs for data caching and checkpoint staging. The infrastructure team proposes 'disaggregated storage': removing local SSDs entirely and replacing them with a high-bandwidth network-attached NVMe-oF (NVMe over Fabric) storage pool. Their argument: better utilization (shared pool vs stranded per-node capacity), easier replacement of failed drives, and simpler node design. The training platform team pushes back, arguing this will
common_mistake: Taking an absolute position — either 'disaggregation is always better because it improves utilization' or 'local storage is always better because it is faster.' The right answer depends on the workload's sensitivity to I/O latency and the network fabric's bandwidth relative to local NVMe bandwidth.
realistic_solution: The tradeoff hinges on two ratios: (1) the network bandwidth per node relative to local NVMe bandwidth, and (2) the I/O latency sensitivity of the workload. Converged wins when: (a) Checkpoint writes are in the critical path. Local NVMe at 6-25 GB/s per node with ~50us latency is 10-60× faster than NVMe-oF at 3-6 GB/s with ~300-500us latency over a 200 Gbps fabric. (b) The training data pipeline requires low-jitter random reads. Local NVMe provides deterministic latency; network-attached storage introduces tail latency from fabric congestion, competing traffic, and switch hops. (c) The cluster
napkin_math: Converged: 4× 3.84 TB NVMe per node = 15.36 TB, 25 GB/s read, ~50us latency. Per-node checkpoint write: 1.6 GB at 6 GB/s = 267ms. Disaggregated (NVMe-oF over 200 Gbps RDMA): effective 20 GB/s shared across 8 GPUs → 2.5 GB/s per GPU equivalent, ~300us latency. Per-node checkpoint write: 1.6 GB at 2.5 GB/s = 640ms — 2.4× slower. At scale (12,500 nodes), converged NVMe total capacity: 192 PB. Average utilization for a single training job: ~15% (most capacity is for checkpoint rotation). Wasted capacity: 163 PB × $50/TB = $8.2M stranded. Disaggregated pool sized to actual demand (30 PB at $80/TB f

title: BSP vs Streaming Dataflow for Feature Computation
scenario: Your team needs to compute 500 features for a recommendation model. Some features (e.g., 'user purchase count last 30 days') are recomputed nightly in a Spark batch job. Others (e.g., 'items viewed in current session') must update within seconds. A junior engineer proposes running everything in a single Apache Flink streaming job to 'simplify the architecture.' Why is a pure streaming approach problematic for the batch features, and what execution model mismatch is at play?
common_mistake: "Streaming is strictly better because it's lower latency." This ignores the fundamental difference between Bulk Synchronous Parallel (BSP) and streaming execution models and their resource efficiency tradeoffs.
realistic_solution: BSP (batch) systems like Spark process bounded datasets in synchronized stages: shuffle all data, then aggregate, then output. This is efficient for full-dataset scans (e.g., computing a 30-day window over 10B events) because it can sort-merge join and use disk spill efficiently. Streaming systems process unbounded data record-by-record with local state. Maintaining a 30-day rolling window in a streaming system requires holding 30 days of state per user in RocksDB or similar — for 100M users, that's terabytes of managed state with compaction overhead, TTL management, and exactly-once checkpoin
napkin_math: 100M users × 30-day window × 50 events/user/day × 200 bytes/event = 30 TB of managed streaming state. Flink checkpoint interval at 1 min with 30 TB state: each checkpoint writes ~30 TB to S3, taking ~300 seconds at 100 GB/s — longer than the checkpoint interval itself. The batch Spark job reads the same 30 TB once, aggregates in 3 shuffle stages, completes in ~20 minutes on a 200-node cluster.

title: The Shuffle Data Volume Calculation
scenario: You are running a distributed Spark job to join a 10 TB user-activity table with a 500 GB user-profile table, both partitioned by user_id across 200 nodes. The join key is user_id. How much data moves across the network during the shuffle phase, and what determines whether this is a broadcast join or a shuffle join?
common_mistake: "Both tables are shuffled, so 10.5 TB moves across the network." This ignores the broadcast join optimization — when one table is small enough to fit in each executor's memory, it can be broadcast to avoid shuffling the large table entirely.
realistic_solution: If both tables are already partitioned by user_id with the same partition function and same number of partitions, zero shuffle is needed — this is a co-partitioned join. If partitioned differently, the optimizer must choose: (1) Shuffle both sides: ~10.5 TB network I/O, or (2) Broadcast the 500 GB table to all 200 nodes: 500 GB × 200 = 100 TB total network I/O — worse. (3) Re-partition only the 500 GB table to match the 10 TB table's partitioning: 500 GB network I/O. Option 3 is optimal. The rule: shuffle the smaller table to match the larger table's existing partitioning.
napkin_math: Option 1 (shuffle both): 10 TB + 500 GB = 10.5 TB over network. At 25 Gbps bisection bandwidth per node, 10.5 TB / (200 × 3.125 GB/s) = ~17 seconds shuffle time. Option 3 (shuffle small only): 500 GB / (200 × 3.125 GB/s) = 0.8 seconds. 21× faster.

title: The Skewed Join Straggler Problem
scenario: You are joining a 5 TB click-event table with a 200 GB merchant table on merchant_id. The job has 1,000 partitions and usually finishes in 8 minutes, but the last 3 partitions take 2 hours. Profiling shows these 3 partitions each contain 200 GB of data while the other 997 partitions average 4.5 GB. The 3 slow partitions correspond to Amazon, Walmart, and Target — merchants with billions of clicks. How do you fix this without changing the data model?
common_mistake: "Just increase the number of partitions to 10,000." This redistributes uniformly, but the skewed keys still land on the same partitions — the problem is key skew, not partition count.
realistic_solution: Use salted keys for the hot merchants. Append a random suffix (0-99) to the merchant_id for the 3 hot keys in the click table, creating 100 sub-partitions per hot merchant. Replicate the corresponding 3 rows in the merchant table 100 times with matching salted keys. Now the 200 GB per hot merchant distributes across 100 partitions (2 GB each), matching the other partitions. The merchant table grows from 200 GB to 200 GB + (3 rows × 100 copies) — negligible. Alternatively, use Spark's Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) which detects skew at runtime and automatically splits oversized partitions.
napkin_math: Before salting: 3 partitions × 200 GB = 600 GB on 3 tasks. At 50 MB/s disk merge speed per task, each takes 200 GB / 50 MB/s = 4,000s ≈ 67 minutes. After salting: 300 partitions × 2 GB = 600 GB spread across 300 tasks. Each takes 2 GB / 50 MB/s = 40 seconds. Job completion time drops from 67 minutes (straggler-bound) to ~8 minutes (matching the non-skewed partitions).

title: The Petabyte Deduplication Shuffle Wall
scenario: You need to deduplicate a 15 TB web crawl corpus (3 billion documents) for LLM pre-training. The naive approach computes a hash per document and groups by hash. But your team wants fuzzy deduplication — removing documents that are 80%+ similar, not just exact matches. A colleague proposes computing MinHash signatures (128 hash functions) for all 3B documents and then performing an all-pairs comparison. Why does this approach fail at petabyte scale, and what is the standard distributed deduplication pipeline?
common_mistake: "MinHash is efficient — it's O(n) to compute signatures." True for signature computation, but the all-pairs comparison is O(n²) and the shuffle to group similar documents creates a network I/O explosion.
realistic_solution: The standard approach is MinHash + Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) banding. Instead of comparing all pairs, divide each 128-hash signature into b bands of r rows. Two documents hash to the same LSH bucket only if they share an identical band. This converts the O(n²) comparison into O(n × b) hash lookups. The key insight: LSH is a form of approximate nearest-neighbor search that trades recall for efficiency. With b=20 bands of r=6 rows (for 80% similarity threshold), only ~5% of all pairs that are 80%+ similar are missed, while 99.99% of dissimilar pairs are never compared. The distributed pip
napkin_math: 3B documents all-pairs: 3B × 3B / 2 = 4.5 × 10¹⁸ comparisons — impossible. With LSH banding (b=20, r=6): each document emits 20 band hashes. Total records to shuffle: 3B × 20 = 60B (band_hash, doc_id) pairs × 20 bytes = 1.2 TB shuffle. Average bucket size: ~10 documents. Pairwise comparisons per bucket: 10×9/2 = 45. Total comparisons: 60B/10 × 45 = 270B — a 16-million-fold reduction from naive all-pairs.

title: Actor Model vs BSP for Data Processing Pipelines
scenario: Your team preprocesses training data using a Spark (BSP) pipeline: read raw text, tokenize, filter by quality score, write shards. The pipeline processes 2 TB/hour on a 100-node cluster. A colleague proposes rewriting it in Ray (actor model) because 'actors are more flexible.' Under what conditions would the actor-model approach outperform BSP for data processing, and when would it be strictly worse?
common_mistake: "Actors are always faster because they avoid the synchronization barriers of BSP." This ignores that BSP's synchronization barriers enable global optimizations (sort-merge joins, optimal shuffle planning) that actors cannot do without explicit coordination.
realistic_solution: BSP (Spark) excels at homogeneous, shuffle-heavy workloads: joins, aggregations, sort-based deduplication. The barrier synchronization enables the query optimizer to plan globally optimal data movement. The actor model (Ray) excels at heterogeneous, stateful, and irregular workloads: when different documents need different processing (e.g., some need OCR, some need language detection, some need decompression), when processing time varies 100× across documents, or when the pipeline has complex branching logic. BSP handles stragglers poorly — every stage waits for the slowest task. Actor model h
napkin_math: Uniform pipeline (tokenize + filter): BSP overhead per stage = ~2s barrier sync × 4 stages = 8s overhead per batch. Actor overhead per task = ~0.5ms scheduling × 2B tasks = ~280 hours cumulative — but amortized across 100 nodes = 2.8 hours wall time. BSP wins on uniform work. Heterogeneous pipeline (some docs need 10ms, others 10s): BSP straggler in a 1000-task stage = max(1000 draws from [10ms, 10s]) ≈ 10s. 997 nodes idle for 9.99s. Actor model: no idle time, each actor pulls next task immediately.

title: The Missing Combiner in Distributed Aggregation
scenario: You are computing per-domain token counts across a 20 TB web corpus distributed over 500 nodes. Each node holds a 40 GB shard. The naive MapReduce approach emits (domain, 1) for every token and shuffles all pairs to reducers. The shuffle phase transfers 18 TB across the network and takes 3 hours. Your colleague points out that the final output — domain counts — is only 2 GB. Why is 18 TB moving across the network to produce a 2 GB result, and how do you fix it?
common_mistake: "The shuffle is necessary because we need all tokens for the same domain on the same reducer." This is true for non-associative operations, but counting is associative and commutative — it can be partially aggregated before the shuffle.
realistic_solution: Add a combiner (local pre-aggregation) phase. Before shuffling, each of the 500 nodes locally aggregates its (domain, 1) pairs into (domain, local_count). If the corpus has ~10M unique domains, each node emits at most 10M records instead of billions. The shuffle volume drops from 18 TB (billions of (domain, 1) pairs) to 500 nodes × 10M domains × 20 bytes = 100 GB — a 180× reduction. The mathematical requirement: the aggregation function must be associative and commutative (counts, sums, min, max). Non-decomposable aggregations (median, percentiles, count-distinct) cannot use simple combiners a
napkin_math: Without combiner: 20 TB corpus × ~0.9 overhead ratio = 18 TB shuffle. At 25 Gbps bisection bandwidth per node: 18 TB / (500 × 3.125 GB/s) ≈ 12 seconds — but the shuffle is not perfectly parallel due to hotspots, so 3 hours is realistic. With combiner: 500 nodes × 10M domains × 20 bytes/record = 100 GB shuffle. Same bandwidth: 100 GB / (500 × 3.125 GB/s) = 0.064 seconds. Even with overhead: ~1 minute total.

title: Bootstrapping a Quality Classifier for Trillion-Token Corpora
scenario: You are building the data pipeline for a 3 trillion token LLM pre-training corpus. The raw crawl is 15 trillion tokens. You need to filter to high-quality text, but you have no labeled quality dataset. Your plan: train a fastText classifier on 'Wikipedia + curated books = positive, random web = negative' and score every document. A senior researcher warns this approach has a critical systematic bias. What is the bias, and how do you build a more robust quality scoring pipeline at this scale?
common_mistake: "Wikipedia is high quality, so using it as the positive class is correct." This confuses domain with quality. Wikipedia has a specific style (encyclopedic, formal, citation-heavy) that is not representative of all high-quality text. The classifier learns 'Wikipedia-like' rather than 'high-quality.'
realistic_solution: The bias is domain-quality conflation. The classifier will assign high scores to encyclopedic text and low scores to high-quality but non-encyclopedic text (e.g., technical forums, literary fiction, scientific papers, code documentation). This systematically removes diverse high-quality content, reducing the corpus's domain coverage. A robust pipeline: (1) Multi-signal quality scoring: combine a classifier score with heuristic signals — perplexity from a small language model, text statistics (sentence length, vocabulary richness, repetition ratio, symbol-to-word ratio), and URL reputation scor
napkin_math: 15T tokens at 4 bytes/token = 60 TB raw. FastText inference: ~1M documents/sec on a 32-core machine. At 500 tokens/document: 30B documents. Time: 30B / 1M = 30,000 seconds = 8.3 hours on 1 machine, or ~5 minutes on a 100-node cluster. The classifier itself is cheap — the bias it introduces costs months of wasted pre-training compute. A 3T token pre-training run on 2,000 H100s at $3/GPU-hour costs ~$2M. If biased filtering degrades the model by even 2% on downstream tasks, the data pipeline cost savings are irrelevant.

title: The Curriculum Data Mixing Problem
scenario: You are designing the data mixing strategy for a 1 trillion token LLM pre-training run. Your corpus consists of 5 domains: web text (8T tokens available), code (2T tokens), scientific papers (500B tokens), books (200B tokens), and math (50B tokens). You need exactly 1T tokens total. A naive approach samples proportionally to availability (73% web, 18% code, 5% science, 2% books, 0.5% math). However, Llama and Chinchilla papers show that proportional sampling produces weak math and reasoning. How do you determine the optimal mixture, and what systems constraint makes this problem harder than it
common_mistake: "Just upsample the rare domains to equal proportions." Equal mixing wastes capacity on domains with diminishing returns (web text has 8T tokens — you only need a fraction) and risks overfitting on small domains (math has only 50B tokens — sampling 200B means 4 epochs, introducing memorization).
realistic_solution: The optimal mixture requires empirical search because there is no closed-form solution for how domain proportions affect downstream performance. The standard approach: (1) Train small proxy models (1B params, 50B tokens) with different mixtures using a grid or Bayesian optimization over the mixing proportions. (2) Evaluate on a diverse benchmark suite (MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, etc.) to find Pareto-optimal mixtures. (3) Apply the winning mixture to the full-scale run. The systems constraint: each domain has a maximum epoch count before overfitting. Math at 50B tokens can be repeated ~4× (200B to
napkin_math: Proxy model search: 20 mixture configs × 1B params × 50B tokens × 6 FLOPs/token = 6 × 10¹⁸ FLOPs per config. On 64 H100s at 50% MFU: 6e18 / (64 × 989e12 × 0.5) = ~190 seconds per config. Total proxy search: 20 × 190s = ~1 hour. Compare to full-scale run: 1T tokens × 70B params × 6 FLOPs/token = 4.2 × 10²³ FLOPs on 2,000 H100s = ~4.7 days. The 1-hour proxy search saves potentially weeks of wasted full-scale training with a bad mixture.

title: The Streaming Deduplication State Explosion
scenario: Your data pipeline ingests a continuous web crawl at 500 GB/hour and must deduplicate documents against the existing 50 TB corpus in near-real-time (within 10 minutes of ingestion). A colleague proposes a Flink streaming job that maintains a Bloom filter of all document hashes in memory for exact dedup, plus a MinHash LSH index in RocksDB state for fuzzy dedup. After 2 weeks, the job's checkpoint time has grown from 30 seconds to 25 minutes, exceeding the 10-minute checkpoint interval, and the job starts failing. Diagnose the root cause and propose an architecture that handles unbounded state
common_mistake: "Increase the checkpoint interval to 30 minutes." This extends the window of data loss on failure and doesn't solve the fundamental problem of unbounded state growth.
realistic_solution: The root cause is unbounded state growth in both the Bloom filter and the RocksDB LSH index. The Bloom filter for 50 TB of documents (at ~500 bytes/doc average, that's ~100B documents) requires ~120 GB at 1% false positive rate (10 bits per element). After 2 weeks of ingesting 500 GB/hour = 168 TB new data, the filter has grown to hold ~400B+ entries (500+ GB), exceeding memory. The RocksDB state similarly grows linearly. The fix: externalize the dedup index. Use a tiered architecture: (1) Hot tier: in-memory Bloom filter covering only the last 7 days of ingested documents (~84 TB, ~170B entri
napkin_math: Bloom filter sizing: n=100B elements, p=0.01 FPR → m = -n × ln(p) / (ln 2)² ≈ 10 × 100B = 1 trillion bits = 125 GB. After 2 weeks at 500 GB/hr: ~400B additional docs → Bloom grows to 500 GB. Flink checkpoint of 500 GB state to S3 at 2 GB/s = 250 seconds = 4.2 minutes — but RocksDB compaction + serialization overhead 5× → 25 minutes. Hot-only Bloom (7 days, 170B entries): 200 GB checkpoint in ~100 seconds — feasible with 5-minute intervals.

title: The Distributed Join Strategy Selection
scenario: You need to enrich a 50 TB training dataset with metadata from three lookup tables: a 5 GB language-detection table, a 200 GB domain-quality table, and a 2 TB URL-to-category mapping. Each join is on a different key. You have a 500-node Spark cluster with 64 GB RAM per executor. For each of the three joins, determine the optimal join strategy (broadcast, shuffle hash, or sort-merge) and calculate the total network I/O across all three joins.
common_mistake: "Use sort-merge join for everything because the tables are large." This ignores that broadcast join is dramatically more efficient when one side fits in executor memory, and that join strategy choice depends on the size ratio between the two tables.
realistic_solution: Join 1 (50 TB × 5 GB on language key): Broadcast the 5 GB table. Each of the 500 executors receives a copy: 5 GB × 500 = 2.5 TB network I/O, but the 50 TB table stays put (zero shuffle). Total: 2.5 TB. Join 2 (50 TB × 200 GB on domain key): 200 GB fits in executor memory (64 GB with some overhead — marginal). If it fits, broadcast: 200 GB × 500 = 100 TB — this is MORE than shuffling both sides (50.2 TB). So use shuffle hash join: shuffle both sides by domain key. Network I/O: 50 TB + 200 GB ≈ 50.2 TB. Join 3 (50 TB × 2 TB on URL key): Both tables are large. Sort-merge join: shuffle both sides
napkin_math: Broadcast breakeven point: broadcast costs R × N (where R = small table size, N = number of nodes). Shuffle costs L + R (both tables shuffled). Broadcast wins when R × N < L + R, i.e., R < L / (N - 1). For N=500, L=50 TB: R < 50 TB / 499 ≈ 100 GB. So tables under 100 GB should be broadcast. 5 GB: broadcast. 200 GB: shuffle (200 > 100). 2 TB: shuffle.

title: Designing a Multi-Signal Data Quality Pipeline at Petabyte Scale
scenario: You are the tech lead for the data team at an LLM lab. Your task: build a reusable data quality scoring pipeline that processes 100 TB of raw web crawl per week and assigns a composite quality score to each document. The score must combine: (1) a learned quality classifier, (2) a perplexity score from a 1B parameter reference LM, (3) 15 heuristic signals (repetition ratio, boilerplate fraction, toxic content score, etc.), and (4) a deduplication flag. The pipeline must be deterministic (same input always produces same output) and fully reproducible. The research team wants to ablate each quali
common_mistake: "Compute everything in a single monolithic Spark job that reads the raw data, runs all scorers, and writes the output." This fails the ablation requirement — changing one weight forces re-running every signal computation, including the expensive LM perplexity inference.
realistic_solution: Use a columnar, append-only architecture with signal decomposition. Phase 1 — Signal Computation (run once per crawl): (a) Heuristic signals: embarrassingly parallel Spark job, ~100 TB/hour on 500 nodes. Write 15 score columns to a Parquet table keyed by doc_id. (b) Quality classifier: batch inference on CPU cluster, ~50 TB/hour. Write classifier_score column. (c) Perplexity scoring: batch inference on GPU cluster (1B model, ~10K tokens/sec per GPU). 100 TB ≈ 200B tokens. On 64 A100s: ~200B / (64 × 10K) = 312,500 seconds ≈ 3.6 days. Write perplexity column. (d) Deduplication: MinHash LSH pipel
napkin_math: Phase 1 total compute cost: Heuristics (500 CPU nodes × 2 hours × $0.10/hr) = $100. Classifier (500 CPU nodes × 2 hours × $0.10/hr) = $100. Perplexity (64 A100s × 86 hours × $3/hr) = $16,500. Dedup (500 nodes × 4 hours × $0.10/hr) = $200. Total: ~$17K per crawl. Phase 2 per ablation: read 20 GB score table, apply weights, write 5 GB output. Cost: $0.50 on a single node. 100 ablation experiments: $50 vs re-running Phase 1 each time: $1.7M. The decomposed architecture saves 34,000× in ablation cost.

title: The Curriculum Ordering vs Random Shuffling Tradeoff
scenario: Your research team proposes curriculum learning for a 70B LLM pre-training run: start with 'easy' documents (short, simple vocabulary, low perplexity) and gradually increase difficulty. The hypothesis is that this will improve convergence speed. However, your infrastructure team pushes back: the standard pipeline randomly shuffles shards to ensure each training batch is i.i.d., which is critical for SGD convergence guarantees. The curriculum approach requires reading shards in a specific order, which breaks i.i.d. sampling AND forces sequential reads from the storage system (eliminating the ab
common_mistake: "Curriculum learning is strictly better — it's been proven to help in supervised learning." The evidence for curriculum learning in large-scale LLM pre-training is mixed, and the systems costs are significant. Also, 'i.i.d. is required for SGD convergence' is an oversimplification — large enough batches approximate i.i.d. even with ordered data.
realistic_solution: The tension is between statistical efficiency (curriculum ordering may improve learning per token) and systems efficiency (random shuffling enables optimal data pipeline throughput). The key insight: at the scale of LLM pre-training (trillions of tokens, thousands of GPUs), the systems costs dominate unless the curriculum provides substantial per-token learning improvement. Systems costs of curriculum ordering: (1) Sequential shard access eliminates random prefetching — throughput drops because the data pipeline cannot read-ahead from arbitrary shards. (2) All GPUs in a data-parallel group mus
napkin_math: Random shuffling throughput: 500 nodes reading random 1 GB shards from a 10 PB corpus — each node issues 1 random read/second from any of 10M shards. Parallel filesystem handles this easily at 500 GB/s aggregate. Strict curriculum throughput: 500 nodes must read from the same 'easy' tier (1 PB, 1M shards) simultaneously. Storage hotspot: 500 GB/s concentrated on 10% of the filesystem. If the PFS has non-uniform shard placement, throughput drops to ~200 GB/s (60% degradation). Soft curriculum (tier-shuffled): 500 nodes read randomly within the current tier. As long as the tier has >5,000 shards

title: The Connected Components Problem in Fuzzy Deduplication
scenario: After running MinHash LSH deduplication on your 5 billion document corpus, you have 800 million candidate pairs of near-duplicate documents. Now you must decide which documents to keep and which to remove. A colleague proposes: for each pair, keep the longer document and remove the shorter one. After running this, you discover you only removed 200 million documents, but manual inspection shows there are clusters of 50-100 near-identical documents (e.g., the same news article syndicated across 80 sites). The pairwise approach only removed one document per pair, leaving 49 duplicates in each clu
common_mistake: "Just run the pairwise dedup iteratively until no more duplicates are found." This is O(k) iterations where k is the maximum cluster size, and each iteration requires re-running the full 800M-edge dedup — totaling 80 × 800M = 64B pair comparisons.
realistic_solution: This is a connected components problem on a graph with 5B nodes and 800M edges. Build the similarity graph (document = node, near-duplicate pair = edge), find connected components, and within each component keep only the highest-quality representative. Distributed connected components at this scale: use the Hash-to-Min algorithm (or the more efficient Alternating algorithm): (1) Initialize each node's component label as its own ID. (2) Each node sends its current minimum label to all neighbors. (3) Each node updates its label to the minimum received. (4) Repeat until convergence (~log(diameter
napkin_math: Graph: 5B nodes, 800M edges. Average component size after convergence: ~15 documents (yielding ~53M components with duplicates + 4.2B singleton components). 53M components × 15 docs/cluster × 14 removals/cluster = 742M documents removed (vs 200M with pairwise). Hash-to-Min iterations: ~8 (for max cluster diameter ~100). Per iteration: emit (neighbor_id, min_label) for each edge: 800M × 2 directions × 16 bytes = 25 GB/iteration. 8 iterations = 200 GB total shuffle. On 500 nodes at 3 GB/s each: 200 GB / 1,500 GB/s = 0.13 seconds per iteration. Total: ~1 second of shuffle time + overhead ≈ 10 min

title: Designing the End-to-End Pre-Training Data Pipeline
scenario: You are hired as the founding data infrastructure engineer at a new LLM lab. Your mandate: design the complete data pipeline from raw web crawl to training-ready token shards for a 2 trillion token pre-training corpus. The pipeline must handle: (1) ingestion of 500 TB/month of Common Crawl + proprietary data sources, (2) language detection and filtering, (3) PII removal, (4) exact and fuzzy deduplication against the full historical corpus, (5) quality scoring with multiple signals, (6) domain classification and mixing, (7) tokenization and shard packaging, and (8) curriculum-aware shard orderi
common_mistake: "Build a single Airflow DAG that chains Spark jobs." This fails on several fronts: monolithic stages cannot be independently re-run for ablation, the dedup step requires state across monthly crawls (not just within one), PII removal may need GPU inference (different compute shape than CPU-heavy heuristics), and reproducibility requires deterministic execution (random shuffles, hash seeds, floating
realistic_solution: Use a staged, columnar architecture with heterogeneous compute. Stage 1 — Ingestion & Extraction (CPU, 2 days): Download raw WARC files, extract text with trafilatura, detect language (fastText), write Parquet with columns [doc_id, url, text, language, source]. Technology: Spark on spot instances (cheap, embarrassingly parallel, tolerant of spot interruptions with lineage-based recovery). Stage 2 — Deduplication (CPU, 1 day): Exact dedup via SHA-256 hash (trivial). Fuzzy dedup via MinHash LSH against an external persistent index (not Spark state). The index is a sharded RocksDB cluster that pe
napkin_math: Stage 1: 500 TB input, spot instances at $0.03/vCPU-hr. At 100 MB/s/core throughput: 500 TB / (0.1 GB/s × 3600) = 1,389 core-hours. With overhead (HTML parsing, language detection): ~10,000 core-hours × $0.03 = $300. Stage 2: MinHash (128 hashes × 500B documents = 64 trillion hash computations). At 10M hashes/s/core: 6.4M core-seconds = 1,778 core-hours × $0.03 = $53. LSH index queries: 500B docs × 20 bands = 10T lookups against RocksDB. 100K lookups/s/core: 100M core-seconds on-demand (need durability) at $0.05/hr = $1,389. Stage 3: Perplexity scoring on GPUs: 500 TB ≈ 1T tokens at 10K tokens

title: The Deterministic Global Shuffle at Trillion-Token Scale
scenario: Your LLM pre-training uses 2,048 GPUs with data parallelism. Each GPU must see a unique, non-overlapping subset of the 2 trillion token corpus per epoch, and the global ordering must be deterministically shuffled (so that if training resumes from a checkpoint at step 50,000, every GPU sees exactly the same data it would have seen without the interruption). The corpus is stored as 2 million 1 GB shards on a parallel filesystem. A naive approach: generate a global permutation of all 2M shard indices, split into 2,048 contiguous chunks. But this requires a coordinator to hold and distribute a 2M-
common_mistake: "Each GPU independently shuffles its assigned shards using the same random seed." This only shuffles within each GPU's partition — it doesn't create a global shuffle. Documents assigned to GPU 0 in epoch 1 stay on GPU 0 in epoch 2, creating data ordering correlations across epochs that can harm convergence.
realistic_solution: Use a pseudorandom permutation function (PRP) that maps (epoch, global_index) → shard_index without materializing the full permutation. Implementation: a Feistel cipher with the epoch as the key. Each GPU computes its own shard assignments locally: for GPU rank r in epoch e, it reads shards at indices PRP(e, r × shards_per_gpu + 0), PRP(e, r × shards_per_gpu + 1), ..., PRP(e, r × shards_per_gpu + k-1). This is coordinator-free (every GPU computes independently), deterministic (same seed + epoch → same permutation), and produces a true global shuffle (the Feistel PRP is a bijection over the sha
napkin_math: Naive coordinator approach: 2M shard indices × 8 bytes = 16 MB permutation. Broadcasting 16 MB to 2,048 GPUs via AllBroadcast: 16 MB × log₂(2048) = 176 MB total network traffic per epoch. Not terrible, but adds a synchronization barrier. PRP approach: each GPU computes 2M/2048 = 977 Feistel cipher evaluations per epoch. At ~100ns per evaluation: 97.7 μs per GPU — negligible. Zero network traffic for shuffle coordination. For mid-training GPU count change (2,048 → 1,536 due to node failure): consumed shards = global_step × 2,048 × shards_per_step. Remaining shards: 2M - consumed. Redistribute r

title: The SSA Form Purpose
scenario: A colleague asks why ML compilers convert computation graphs into Static Single Assignment (SSA) form before running optimization passes. What property of SSA makes it essential for compiler optimization, and what would go wrong without it?
common_mistake: "SSA is just an internal detail — any graph representation works equally well for optimization." Without SSA, reaching-definition analysis becomes expensive and error-prone, making many optimizations impractical.
realistic_solution: SSA ensures every variable is defined exactly once, which makes def-use chains explicit and trivial to traverse. This is critical because most compiler optimizations — dead code elimination, constant propagation, common subexpression elimination — require knowing which definition reaches which use. Without SSA, the compiler must run iterative dataflow analysis to compute reaching definitions, which is O(n²) in the number of statements per pass. With SSA, the answer is immediate: there is exactly one definition per variable, and phi nodes at control-flow merge points make the choice explicit. F
napkin_math: For a graph with 1,000 operations, reaching-definition analysis without SSA requires O(1,000²) = 1M iterations per pass. With SSA, dependency lookup is O(1) per variable. For a compilation pipeline with 20 optimization passes, this saves ~20M iterations — the difference between milliseconds and seconds of compile time.

title: The Dialect Hierarchy Lowering
scenario: MLIR uses a hierarchy of 'dialects' — from high-level ML operations down to hardware-specific instructions. Why does an ML compiler need multiple IR levels instead of compiling directly from the computation graph to machine code?
common_mistake: "Multiple levels add unnecessary complexity — a single-pass compiler would be simpler and faster." This ignores that different optimizations are only valid and efficient at specific abstraction levels.
realistic_solution: Multi-level IRs enable optimizations at the right abstraction level. At the highest level (e.g., MLIR's `linalg` dialect), the compiler reasons about entire tensor operations — fusing a matmul with its subsequent bias add and ReLU. At the mid level (e.g., `affine` or `scf` dialects), the compiler reasons about loop structures — tiling, unrolling, vectorization. At the lowest level (e.g., `llvm` or `gpu` dialects), the compiler reasons about register allocation and instruction scheduling. A direct single-pass compilation would either lose the high-level semantic information needed for operator
napkin_math: A typical MLIR pipeline for a transformer block: `torch` dialect (semantic ops) → `linalg` dialect (tensor algebra, ~5 fusion passes) → `affine` dialect (loop nests, ~8 tiling/vectorization passes) → `gpu` dialect (thread mapping, ~3 passes) → `nvvm` or `llvm` (codegen). That is ~4 dialect levels and ~20 passes — each pass operating on the representation where its optimization is most natural.

title: The Tiling Factor Search Space
scenario: You are auto-tuning a matrix multiply kernel for an H100 GPU. The kernel has 4 tunable tiling parameters (M_tile, N_tile, K_tile, and number of pipeline stages), each with 8 possible values. How large is the search space, and why does this make exhaustive search impractical for a full model compilation?
common_mistake: "The search space is 4 × 8 = 32 configurations." This confuses additive and multiplicative combinatorics. Each parameter is independent, so the configurations multiply.
realistic_solution: The search space is $8^4 = 4{,}096$ configurations per kernel. But a transformer model typically has 50-100 distinct kernel shapes (different attention heads, FFN layers, varying sequence lengths). The total search space for the full model is $4{,}096 \times 80 \approx 327{,}680$ kernel configurations. Each configuration requires a benchmark run (~5ms for a single kernel trial), making exhaustive search for one kernel feasible ($4{,}096 \times 5\text{ms} \approx 20\text{s}$) but exhaustive search for the whole model expensive ($327{,}680 \times 5\text{ms} \approx 27\text{ minutes}$). This is w
napkin_math: $8^4 = 4{,}096$ configs/kernel × 80 unique kernels = 327,680 total. At 5ms/trial: exhaustive = $327{,}680 \times 0.005 = 1{,}638\text{s} \approx 27\text{ min}$. With guided search (100 trials/kernel): $100 \times 80 \times 0.005 = 40\text{s}$. Speedup = $41\times$, with typically <5% performance gap vs exhaustive optimum.

title: The Triton vs CUDA Tradeoff
scenario: Your team needs to write a custom fused attention kernel. The senior GPU engineer proposes writing it in CUDA; a newer team member suggests Triton. The CUDA version will take 3 weeks of engineering time and achieve 85% of peak H100 FLOPS. Estimate what fraction of peak FLOPS a Triton implementation would likely achieve, and explain why the abstraction gap creates this performance difference.
common_mistake: "Triton is just a wrapper around CUDA, so it should achieve the same performance." Triton operates at a higher abstraction level, which means certain hardware-specific optimizations are harder to express.
realistic_solution: A well-written Triton kernel typically achieves 70-80% of peak FLOPS — about 5-15% behind hand-tuned CUDA. The performance gap comes from the abstraction level: Triton programs operate on tiles of data (block-level programming) while CUDA operates on individual threads. This means Triton's compiler must infer the optimal shared memory usage, pipeline scheduling, and register allocation from the tile-level description. For standard patterns (GEMM, reduction, elementwise fusion), the Triton compiler's heuristics are excellent and close the gap to <5%. The gap widens for irregular patterns (spars
napkin_math: H100 FP16 peak: 989 TFLOPS. CUDA hand-tuned: $989 \times 0.85 = 841$ TFLOPS (3 weeks effort). Triton: $989 \times 0.75 = 742$ TFLOPS (2-3 days effort). Performance gap: $841 - 742 = 99$ TFLOPS (12% relative). Engineering efficiency: CUDA = $841/15\text{ eng-days} = 56$ TFLOPS/eng-day. Triton = $742/2.5\text{ eng-days} = 297$ TFLOPS/eng-day. Triton is $5.3\times$ more engineer-efficient.

title: The Lowering Pass Fusion Loss
scenario: After adding a custom activation function (SwiGLU) to your model, you profile the compiled kernel and find that the compiler failed to fuse it with the preceding linear layer. The standard ReLU version of the same architecture fuses perfectly. Why would SwiGLU break the fusion pass, and at which IR level does the failure occur?
common_mistake: "The compiler is buggy — file an issue and wait for a fix." While compiler bugs exist, most fusion failures are caused by pattern-matching limitations at specific IR levels, not bugs.
realistic_solution: The fusion failure occurs at the graph-level IR (e.g., MLIR's `linalg` dialect or XLA's HLO). Standard fusions like matmul+bias+ReLU are implemented as pattern-matching rewrite rules: the compiler recognizes the specific subgraph and replaces it with a fused kernel. SwiGLU has the form $\text{SwiGLU}(x) = (xW_1) \otimes \sigma(xW_2)$, which requires two parallel linear projections followed by an elementwise multiply — a non-trivial data dependency pattern. The fusion pass sees two matmuls feeding into a multiply, which doesn't match its matmul+activation fusion template. The fix is either (1)
napkin_math: Unfused SwiGLU with hidden_dim=8192: Two matmul writes to HBM ($2 \times 8192 \times B \times 2\text{B} = 32\text{KB} \times B$) + one elementwise read-multiply-write ($3 \times 8192 \times B \times 2\text{B} = 48\text{KB} \times B$). Fused version eliminates the intermediate HBM writes, saving $32\text{KB} \times B$ of bandwidth per token. At B=2048 tokens: $32\text{KB} \times 2048 = 64\text{MB}$ saved per layer, $\times 80$ layers = 5.12 GB of memory traffic saved per forward pass. At 3.35 TB/s HBM bandwidth, that is 1.5ms saved — significant for inference.

title: The Graph Break Recompilation Storm
scenario: After enabling `torch.compile()` on your training loop, you observe that the first epoch takes 45 minutes instead of the expected 5 minutes. Subsequent epochs run at the expected speed. The profiler shows thousands of compilation events in the first epoch. What is causing the recompilation storm, and how do you diagnose which operations are triggering graph breaks?
common_mistake: "The compiler is just slow — buy more CPU cores for compilation." Compilation speed is a symptom; the root cause is excessive graph fragmentation from unsupported operations.
realistic_solution: Graph breaks occur when TorchDynamo encounters Python operations it cannot trace into the computation graph — data-dependent control flow (if tensor.item() > threshold), in-place operations on tensors with aliasing, calls to non-compilable external libraries, or dynamic shapes that change every iteration. Each graph break creates a separate subgraph that must be independently compiled by the backend (TorchInductor). With thousands of graph breaks, you get thousands of tiny compiled kernels instead of a few large fused kernels. Diagnosis: set `torch._dynamo.config.verbose = True` and `torch._dy
napkin_math: 1,000 graph breaks × ~2s compilation each = 2,000s = 33 minutes of compilation overhead. After fixing: 8 large graphs × ~15s each = 120s = 2 minutes. The compiled kernels are also faster because larger fused graphs enable more cross-operator optimizations. Net first-epoch improvement: $45\text{min} \to 7\text{min}$.

title: The Cost Model vs Profiling Dilemma
scenario: Your ML compiler's auto-tuner uses an analytical cost model to predict kernel performance and select tiling configurations. After upgrading from A100 to H100 GPUs, you find that the cost model consistently selects suboptimal tile sizes — the auto-tuned kernels run 30% slower than the best configuration found by random search. Analyze why the cost model broke across GPU generations and propose a fix that scales to future hardware.
common_mistake: "Just retrain the cost model on H100 data." This fixes the immediate problem but creates a maintenance burden for every new GPU generation.
realistic_solution: The cost model broke because the H100 changed several microarchitectural parameters that the analytical model didn't account for: (1) The TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator) unit changes the cost of shared memory loads — the A100 cost model assumes explicit `cp.async` loads while H100's TMA can load entire tiles asynchronously. (2) The H100's L2 cache is 50MB vs A100's 40MB, changing the effective working set boundary. (3) The H100's Transformer Engine with FP8 changes the compute-to-memory ratio, shifting optimal tile sizes. The scalable fix is a hybrid approach: use a learned cost model (small n
napkin_math: Analytical model tuning on H100: 0 calibration cost, 30% suboptimal → wastes $0.30 per $1.00 of GPU compute. Random search: $4{,}096 \times 5\text{ms} = 20\text{s}$ per kernel, finds optimum, but $20\text{s} \times 80$ kernels = 27 min per model compilation.
