Learned cost model: 1,000 calibration samples × 5ms = 5s one-time cost, then <5% suboptimal per kernel, 100 guided trials × 80 kernels × 5ms = 40s per model compilation. The learned model saves 26.3 min of compilation time while staying within 5% of optimal.

title: The XLA vs TorchInductor Decision
scenario: Your infrastructure team must choose a compiler backend for a large-scale transformer training job. XLA (used via JAX) and TorchInductor (used via torch.compile) are the two candidates. Both produce fused kernels from high-level operations. Analyze the architectural differences between these two compilation strategies and explain when each has a structural advantage.
common_mistake: "XLA is for TPUs and TorchInductor is for GPUs — just pick based on hardware." Both support multiple hardware targets; the real difference is in compilation strategy, not hardware support.
realistic_solution: The fundamental architectural difference is whole-program vs per-operator compilation scope. XLA performs whole-program compilation: it captures the entire computation graph ahead of time, runs global optimization passes (cross-layer fusion, memory planning across the full model, buffer donation), and produces a single optimized executable. This gives XLA a structural advantage for static, regular workloads (standard transformer training with fixed shapes) because it can make globally optimal decisions — e.g., fusing the layer-norm at the end of one layer with the linear projection at the star
napkin_math: For a standard 70B transformer with fixed shapes: XLA achieves 55-60% MFU (global fusion, buffer planning). TorchInductor achieves 50-55% MFU (fragment-level fusion, less cross-layer optimization). Gap: ~10% relative. For a MoE model with dynamic routing: XLA requires padding all experts to max capacity (wasting 20-40% compute on padding). TorchInductor compiles the dynamic dispatch natively, executing only active expert paths. Net: TorchInductor wins by 15-25% on dynamic MoE workloads.

title: The Tensor Core Codegen Gap
scenario: You profile a compiled transformer training job on H100 GPUs and find that Tensor Core utilization is only 35% despite the workload being compute-bound. The compiler's generated SASS shows that many matrix multiplies are using FFMA (FP32 fused multiply-add) instructions instead of HMMA (half-precision matrix multiply-accumulate on Tensor Cores). Diagnose why the compiler's target-specific code generation is failing to use the Tensor Cores and identify at which compilation stage the fix must be applied.
common_mistake: "Add `--use-tensor-cores` to the compiler flags." There is no such flag. Tensor Core usage depends on specific preconditions being met during code generation.
realistic_solution: Tensor Cores have strict shape and alignment requirements: matrix dimensions must be multiples of 16 (for FP16 HMMA) or 8 (for FP8), input tensors must be in specific memory layouts (row-major A, column-major B for `mma.sync`), and data must be aligned to 128-byte boundaries in shared memory. The compiler's code generation is falling back to FFMA because one or more of these preconditions fails. Common causes: (1) The tiling pass chose tile sizes that are not multiples of 16 — this is a mid-level IR problem (affine/SCF dialect). (2) The memory layout pass did not insert the required transpose
napkin_math: HMMA throughput on H100: 989 TFLOPS (FP16). FFMA throughput: 67 TFLOPS (FP32). Ratio: $989/67 \approx 14.8\times$. At 35% Tensor Core utilization, effective throughput = $0.35 \times 989 + 0.65 \times 67 = 346 + 44 = 390$ TFLOPS. After fixing alignment to achieve 90% Tensor Core utilization: $0.90 \times 989 + 0.10 \times 67 = 890 + 7 = 897$ TFLOPS. Speedup: $897/390 = 2.3\times$ — purely from fixing the code generation pipeline.

title: The Auto-Tuning Transfer Problem
scenario: Your team auto-tuned a set of transformer kernels for batch_size=32, seq_len=2048 on H100. When serving traffic arrives with variable sequence lengths (128-8192) and batch sizes (1-64), the auto-tuned kernels perform 40% worse than the default configurations for many input shapes. Analyze why tuning for one shape fails to generalize, and design a strategy that covers the dynamic shape space without requiring exhaustive per-shape tuning.
common_mistake: "Just tune for the most common shape — it will work well enough for others." Optimal tile sizes depend critically on the ratio of compute to memory traffic, which shifts with every shape change.
realistic_solution: The auto-tuned configuration fails to generalize because the optimal tiling parameters are a function of the problem shape. For batch_size=32, seq_len=2048, the matmuls are large and compute-bound — the auto-tuner picks large tiles (128×128) to maximize Tensor Core utilization. For batch_size=1, seq_len=128, the same matmul is small and memory-bound — large tiles waste shared memory and leave most Tensor Cores idle, while small tiles (32×32) better match the problem size. The solution is a multi-configuration dispatch table: (1) Partition the shape space into ~8-12 buckets based on arithmetic
napkin_math: Shape space: 6 batch sizes × 8 seq lengths = 48 shape combinations. Exhaustive tuning: 48 × 4,096 configs × 5ms = 983s ≈ 16 min. Bucketed approach: 10 buckets × 4,096 configs × 5ms = 205s ≈ 3.4 min (with guided search: 10 × 100 × 5ms = 5s). Performance: exhaustive achieves optimal per-shape; bucketed achieves <3% gap for 97% of shapes, with outliers at bucket boundaries.

title: The Custom Op Compiler Co-Design
scenario: Your research team invents a novel 'Gated Linear Attention' (GLA) operator that replaces softmax attention. It achieves great accuracy but runs 2× slower than FlashAttention-2 on H100 despite having lower theoretical FLOP count. The operator involves a recurrent scan over sequence positions that cannot be expressed as a single GEMM call. Evaluate the compiler-hardware co-design challenge: why is the compiler failing to generate efficient code for this operator, and design a strategy to close the performance gap.
common_mistake: "The operator is just inherently slower — recurrence is fundamentally sequential." While recurrence introduces serial dependencies, parallel scan algorithms can parallelize it, and the real bottleneck is often the compiler's inability to map the scan to hardware primitives.
realistic_solution: The performance gap has three sources rooted in compiler-hardware mismatch: (1) **No hardware primitive**: FlashAttention maps to HMMA Tensor Core instructions because it is fundamentally a sequence of GEMMs. GLA's recurrent scan maps to no hardware primitive — the compiler generates scalar FFMA loops, underutilizing the Tensor Cores by ~15×. (2) **Suboptimal memory access pattern**: The scan's recurrent state (a d×d matrix per head, updated at each position) creates a loop-carried dependency. The compiler cannot tile across the sequence dimension because each position depends on the previous
napkin_math: FlashAttention-2 on H100 at seq_len=4096, d=128, 32 heads: ~0.8ms (Tensor Core-bound, 85% utilization). Naive GLA scan: 4096 sequential steps × (d² FLOPs per step) = $4096 \times 128^2 = 67M$ FLOPs per head, but at FFMA throughput (67 TFLOPS) = 1μs compute + ~30μs memory stalls from non-coalesced access = ~31μs × 32 heads = 1.0ms. Chunked scan (chunk=64): 64 inter-chunk serial steps × (small GEMM for state propagation) + 64-way parallel intra-chunk = ~0.4ms compute + ~0.6ms memory = 1.0ms total. With Triton fusion: eliminate intermediate HBM writes, ~0.8-1.1ms. Gap: $1.1/0.8 = 1.4\times$ — clo

title: The MLIR Retargetability Boundary
scenario: Your company trained a 70B transformer on NVIDIA H100s using PyTorch. A cloud cost analysis shows that AMD MI300X GPUs offer 30% lower $/TFLOP. The plan is to retarget the compiled model using MLIR's multi-target lowering — keep the high-level dialects identical and swap only the final backend from NVVM to AMDGPU. After migration, actual training throughput is only 60% of the H100's, despite MI300X having comparable peak TFLOPS. Evaluate where the retargetability promise of MLIR breaks down and what additional work is required.
common_mistake: "MLIR's whole point is retargetability — just swap the backend and it should work." MLIR provides retargetability at the IR level, but performance portability requires hardware-specific optimization at multiple levels, not just the final lowering.
realistic_solution: The retargetability promise breaks down at three levels: (1) **Tiling and memory hierarchy mismatch**: Tile sizes auto-tuned for H100 (80GB HBM3 at 3.35 TB/s, 50MB L2, 228KB shared memory per SM) are suboptimal for MI300X (192GB HBM3 at 5.3 TB/s, 256MB Infinity Cache, 64KB LDS per CU). MI300X's much larger cache favors different working-set sizes. The tiling passes in the mid-level dialects must be re-tuned. (2) **Missing hardware primitives**: NVIDIA's TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator) for async tile loads has no direct MI300X equivalent — the compiler must generate explicit DMA commands, requi
napkin_math: H100 training throughput (baseline): 100% (MFU ~55%). Naive MLIR retarget to MI300X: 60% of H100 throughput. Breakdown of the 40% gap: tiling mismatch = ~15% (wrong working set sizes), missing TMA codegen = ~10% (extra synchronization overhead), library coverage gap = ~15% (fallback to unoptimized kernels). After dedicated optimization work (~2 engineer-weeks): 88% of H100 throughput. Net cost savings: MI300X at 88% throughput × 70% cost = effective $0.80 per $1.00 H100-equivalent → 20% real savings.

title: The Compilation Latency Wall in Serving
scenario: Your serving fleet uses `torch.compile` to optimize inference kernels. When deploying a model update (same architecture, new weights), the first request after deployment takes 45 seconds because the compiler recompiles all kernels from scratch. This violates your 5-second cold-start SLA. The engineering team proposes three solutions: (A) cache compiled kernels and reuse across weight updates, (B) AOT-compile during CI/CD and ship pre-compiled artifacts, (C) serve un-compiled for the first 10 seconds while compilation happens in the background. Evaluate all three approaches and their tradeoffs.
common_mistake: "Just increase the cold-start SLA to 60 seconds." This is a product cop-out; the technical solutions have real tradeoffs worth analyzing.
realistic_solution: **Option A (Kernel caching)**: Works because compiled kernels depend on the computation graph structure and input shapes, NOT on weight values. If the architecture is unchanged, all cached kernels are valid. Tradeoff: requires a cache invalidation strategy (architecture changes must bust the cache), and the cache must be distributed across the serving fleet (shared filesystem or object store). Cold start: ~2s (cache load from S3) — meets SLA. Risk: stale cache after architecture change causes silent correctness bugs. **Option B (AOT compilation in CI/CD)**: The most robust approach. Compile th
napkin_math: Model with 80 unique kernel shapes. JIT compilation: 80 × 0.5s = 40s cold start (violates 5s SLA). Option A (cache load from S3): 80 × 25ms = 2s (meets SLA). Option B (AOT, load .so from disk): 80 × 12ms = 1s (meets SLA). Option C (eager fallback): 0s cold start, but eager throughput = 60% of compiled → at 1000 QPS, need 67% more GPUs for the first 40s = 0.75 extra GPU-minutes per deployment × 100 deployments/day = 75 GPU-minutes/day ≈ $2.50/day.

title: The New Accelerator Compiler Stack
scenario: Your company is designing a custom AI accelerator with a novel dataflow architecture: a 2D array of processing elements (PEs), each with 256KB of local SRAM, connected by a configurable on-chip network. Unlike GPUs (SIMT) or TPUs (systolic arrays), your PEs execute independent instruction streams and communicate via explicit message passing. Design the compiler stack — from PyTorch model to executable — specifying which MLIR dialects you would create or reuse, where the key lowering passes occur, and how auto-tuning integrates with the novel hardware.
common_mistake: "Fork the NVIDIA GPU backend and modify it for our hardware." GPU compilers assume SIMT execution (thousands of threads, shared memory, warp-level synchronization) — none of which applies to a message-passing dataflow architecture.
realistic_solution: The compiler stack requires 4 dialect levels with custom lowering passes: **Level 1 — Reuse `torch`/`linalg` dialects**: The top of the stack is hardware-independent. TorchDynamo captures the graph, and standard passes (operator fusion, constant folding, shape inference) apply unchanged. **Level 2 — Create a `dataflow` dialect**: This is the key innovation layer. Unlike GPU's `affine` loops, this dialect represents computation as a DAG of tiles mapped to PEs, with explicit communication edges. Operations: `dataflow.map_to_pe(tile, pe_id)`, `dataflow.send(tensor, src_pe, dst_pe)`, `dataflow.bar
napkin_math: For a 70B model matmul (M=4096, N=8192, K=4096) on a 16×16 PE array (256 PEs): Each PE computes a $(256 \times 512)$ output tile. Per-PE SRAM needed: A tile (256×4096×2B = 2MB — too large!) → must K-tile: K_tile=128, A tile = 256×128×2B = 64KB, B tile = 128×512×2B = 128KB, output = 256×512×2B = 256KB. Total = 448KB > 256KB budget. Must further tile: reduce output to 128×256 → 64KB, now total = 64+64+64 = 192KB ≤ 256KB. This requires 4× more communication rounds but fits in SRAM. Search space: 5 M_tile × 5 N_tile × 5 K_tile × 3 comm schedules = 375 configs × 100ms/eval = 37.5s auto-tuning time

title: The Full-Stack Compiler Optimization Audit
scenario: You are brought in to audit the ML compiler stack for a company training a 175B parameter model on 512 H100 GPUs. Current MFU is 38% — well below the expected 50-55% for this workload. The profiler shows that individual operator benchmarks (GEMM, LayerNorm, SoftMax) each achieve 70-85% of their theoretical peak, yet end-to-end MFU is only 38%. Design a systematic methodology to identify where the remaining 32-47% of performance is being lost between individual operator efficiency and end-to-end training throughput, attributing the gap to specific compiler stack layers.
common_mistake: "The gap must be in the communication overhead — run NCCL benchmarks." Communication overhead is one factor, but a 38% MFU with 70-85% per-operator efficiency points to multiple sources of waste distributed across the full compiler and runtime stack.
realistic_solution: The methodology decomposes the MFU gap into 5 measurable layers, working top-down: **Layer 1 — Communication overhead (~8-12%)**: Measure AllReduce time as a fraction of step time. At 512 GPUs with 3D parallelism, expect 10-15% of step time in collectives. Verify communication-computation overlap: the compiler should schedule backward-pass AllReduce to overlap with the next layer's backward compute. If overlap is poor, the communication tax is additive rather than hidden. **Layer 2 — Pipeline bubble (~5-8%)**: With pipeline parallelism, bubble fraction = $(P-1)/M$ where P=pipeline stages, M=mi
napkin_math: Per-operator efficiency: 78% (geometric mean of 70-85%). Communication: 90% efficiency (10% overhead). Pipeline: 89% (11% bubble). Fusion gaps: 94% (6% extra memory traffic). Launch overhead: 96% (4% idle). Padding: 97% (3% waste). Compound: $0.78 \times 0.90 \times 0.89 \times 0.94 \times 0.96 \times 0.97 = 0.78 \times 0.70 = 0.55 \times 0.70 = 0.385$ → MFU = 38.5%. Each multiplicative factor must be addressed independently. Fixing the top 3 (pipeline to 95%, communication overlap to 95%, fusion to 98%): $0.78 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.97 = 0.78 \times 0.82 = 0.

title: The cuBLAS GEMM Algorithm Selection
scenario: A junior engineer calls `torch.matmul` on two FP16 matrices of shape (8192, 4096) × (4096, 8192) on an H100 GPU. They notice the call routes through cuBLAS and achieves about 60% of peak Tensor Core TFLOPS. A colleague says 'cuBLAS automatically picks the best GEMM algorithm.' Is this true? What mechanism does cuBLAS use, and why might it not find the optimal algorithm on the first call?
common_mistake: Assuming cuBLAS has a single hardcoded algorithm per shape. In reality, there are dozens of GEMM implementations (tile sizes, swizzle patterns, split-K strategies) and the best one depends on exact matrix dimensions, data type, and GPU architecture.
realistic_solution: cuBLAS uses a heuristic lookup table to select an initial algorithm, then optionally runs autotuning (cublasLtMatmulAlgoGetHeuristic) to benchmark multiple candidates. The first call uses the heuristic, which may not be optimal. Calling `torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True` and enabling cuBLAS workspace tuning can improve selection. Libraries like CUTLASS expose the full algorithm space for manual tuning.
napkin_math: For an (8192, 4096) × (4096, 8192) GEMM in FP16: FLOPs = 2 × 8192 × 4096 × 8192 = 549 TFLOPS. H100 peak FP16 Tensor Core = 989 TFLOPS. Theoretical minimum = 549/989 = 0.56 ms. At 60% utilization = 0.93 ms. Proper algorithm selection can close the gap to ~80-85% utilization.

title: The cuDNN Convolution Dispatch
scenario: You profile a ResNet-50 training job and notice that different convolutional layers use different cuDNN algorithms — some use implicit GEMM, others use FFT-based convolution, and one small layer uses direct convolution. You expected cuDNN to use the same algorithm everywhere. Why does cuDNN select different algorithms for different layers, and what is the primary factor driving this selection?
common_mistake: Thinking cuDNN always uses the fastest algorithm regardless of context. In reality, algorithm selection depends on input/filter dimensions, available workspace memory, and whether deterministic mode is required.
realistic_solution: cuDNN selects convolution algorithms based on a combination of input tensor shape, filter size, stride, padding, available GPU workspace memory, and whether deterministic reproducibility is required. Implicit GEMM works well for standard 3×3 convolutions. FFT-based convolution wins for large spatial filters. Direct convolution is used for very small or 1×1 filters where the GEMM reshape overhead exceeds compute. Setting `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = True` forces cuDNN to auto-tune by timing each algorithm on the first batch, then caching winners — essential for fixed-shape workloads.
napkin_math: ResNet-50 has ~53 conv layers. With `cudnn.benchmark=True`, first iteration benchmarks ~5-8 algorithms per layer = ~300-400 timed trials. Adds ~30-60 seconds to first iteration. Subsequent iterations see 10-20% throughput improvement from optimal algorithm selection — pays back within the first epoch on ImageNet.

title: The TensorRT Layer Fusion Speedup
scenario: Your team exports a fine-tuned BERT model from PyTorch to TensorRT for production inference. TensorRT reports it fused 85 of the original 200 layers into 40 fused kernels. Inference latency drops from 12ms (PyTorch eager) to 4ms (TensorRT). A teammate asks: 'Where did the 3× speedup come from? The model does the same math.' Explain quantitatively why layer fusion produces such dramatic speedups, especially for memory-bandwidth-bound transformer layers.
common_mistake: Attributing the speedup entirely to 'fewer kernel launches.' Launch overhead is ~5-10μs per kernel — eliminating 160 launches saves only ~1ms. The real win is eliminating intermediate memory round-trips.
realistic_solution: Each unfused kernel writes its output to HBM, and the next kernel reads it back. For BERT layers, intermediate activations are ~2MB per layer at batch=1. With 200 unfused kernels, you do ~400 HBM round-trips. Fused kernels keep intermediates in registers or shared memory (L1), avoiding HBM entirely. Since HBM bandwidth is the bottleneck for small-batch transformer inference, eliminating these round-trips is the dominant speedup source. TensorRT also applies precision reduction (FP16/INT8), static shape specialization, and memory layout optimization.
napkin_math: Unfused: 200 kernels × 2MB intermediate × 2 (read+write) = 800 MB of HBM traffic. At 3.35 TB/s (H100 HBM): 0.24 ms just for intermediates. But kernel overhead + memory latency + cache thrashing adds ~60 ms at batch=1. Fused: 40 kernels, intermediates stay in SRAM. HBM traffic drops to ~50 MB (weights only). Net: ~3× end-to-end improvement.

title: The Framework Dispatch Overhead
scenario: You profile a small transformer model (125M parameters) doing inference with batch size 1 in PyTorch eager mode. The nsys trace shows that GPU kernels occupy only 35% of the wall-clock time — the rest is CPU-side overhead between kernel launches. Your colleague says 'the model is too small to saturate the GPU.' Is that the whole story? What specifically is happening in the 65% of time the GPU is idle, and what are your options to fix it?
common_mistake: Blaming 'Python overhead' generically. The real issue is the framework dispatch stack: Python → PyTorch dispatcher → ATen operator selection → cuBLAS/cuDNN call → CUDA driver → kernel launch. Each hop adds latency, and with small/fast kernels, this overhead dominates.
realistic_solution: The 65% idle time is the PyTorch dispatch overhead: (1) Python interpreter overhead per operator call (~10-20μs), (2) PyTorch's dispatcher traverses multiple dispatch keys (autograd, autocast, batched, vmap) even in inference mode (~5-15μs), (3) operator selection and argument marshaling (~5μs), (4) CUDA kernel launch latency (~5-10μs). For a 125M model with ~100 operators per forward pass, each taking ~20μs of GPU compute, the CPU overhead of ~40μs per operator dominates. Fixes: (1) `torch.compile()` to trace and fuse the graph, eliminating per-operator dispatch, (2) CUDA graphs to replay a c
napkin_math: 100 operators × 20μs GPU each = 2ms of GPU compute. 100 operators × 40μs CPU dispatch = 4ms overhead. Total = 6ms, GPU utilization = 2/6 = 33%. With torch.compile: CPU overhead drops to ~0.5ms (graph dispatch once). Total = 2.5ms, GPU utilization = 80%. With CUDA Graphs: CPU overhead ≈ 0. Total ≈ 2.1ms, GPU utilization ≈ 95%.

title: The Library vs Custom Kernel Decision
scenario: Your inference serving team runs a 70B MoE model. Profiling shows that the top-K expert routing + scatter/gather operations account for 22% of per-token latency. These operations use PyTorch's native indexing and scatter_ operators, which dispatch to generic cuBLAS/cuDNN routines. A kernel engineer proposes writing a custom Triton kernel that fuses the routing decision, expert dispatch, and result gathering into a single kernel. The library vendor (NVIDIA) says their next cuDNN release will include optimized MoE primitives in 6 months. How do you make this build-vs-wait decision?
common_mistake: Defaulting to 'always use vendor libraries' or 'always write custom kernels.' The correct answer depends on the operation's position on the library-coverage spectrum and the business cost of waiting.
realistic_solution: Evaluate across four axes: (1) Performance gap: 22% of latency in routing means a custom kernel could save ~5ms per token on a 23ms budget — significant for SLA. (2) Maintenance cost: Triton kernels are ~10× easier to maintain than raw CUDA, but still require updates per GPU generation. (3) Correctness risk: MoE routing has complex data-dependent control flow; bugs cause silent accuracy loss. (4) Time value: 6 months of serving at 22% overhead costs real money. Decision framework: if the operation is on the critical path AND vendor coverage is >3 months away AND the operation's data access pat
napkin_math: Current: 23ms per token, routing = 5ms. Custom kernel (fused): routing drops to ~1.5ms. New total: 19.5ms (15% end-to-end speedup). At 10,000 QPS serving fleet: saves ~35ms × 10K = 350 GPU-seconds/second, or roughly 15 fewer GPUs needed. At $2/hr per GPU: $260K/year savings. Triton kernel development cost: ~2 engineer-weeks. ROI: <1 week.

title: The Caching Allocator Fragmentation Trap
scenario: Your serving system runs a 13B model on an A100-80GB GPU. `nvidia-smi` reports 45GB used, and `torch.cuda.memory_allocated()` shows 30GB. Yet when you try to allocate a 20GB KV-cache buffer, PyTorch throws an OOM error despite 50GB appearing 'free' on the device. The ops team says 'the GPU has plenty of memory.' Why does the allocation fail, and what is the role of PyTorch's caching allocator in this failure?
common_mistake: Confusing nvidia-smi's 'used memory' with PyTorch's allocated memory. The 15GB gap (45GB - 30GB) is not 'free' — it's held by PyTorch's caching allocator as a pool of freed-but-not-returned CUDA blocks.
realistic_solution: PyTorch's caching allocator (CUDACachingAllocator) never returns memory to the CUDA driver by default. When tensors are freed, their CUDA blocks go into a free list, fragmented by size. The 15GB in the cache is split across many small blocks (e.g., thousands of 2MB and 8MB fragments from previous forward passes). The 20GB KV-cache needs a single contiguous allocation that no combination of cached fragments can satisfy. Diagnosis: `torch.cuda.memory_stats()` shows `allocated_bytes`, `reserved_bytes`, and `num_alloc_retries`. Fix: (1) `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` returns all cached blocks to the C
napkin_math: 45GB reported by nvidia-smi = 30GB actively allocated + 15GB in caching allocator free list. The 15GB free list might contain 3,000 blocks averaging 5MB each. Largest contiguous free block: ~50MB. Needed: 20GB contiguous. Gap: 400×. Even `empty_cache()` may not help if CUDA driver memory is itself fragmented from repeated alloc/free cycles.

title: The torch.compile Graph Break Cascade
scenario: You apply `torch.compile()` to your custom 7B transformer and expect a 2× inference speedup based on published benchmarks. Instead, you see only a 1.1× improvement. The compilation log shows 47 graph breaks. The model uses dynamic control flow (`if token_count > max_len: truncate()`), custom Python logging inside the forward pass, and calls a third-party tokenizer library within the model. What is a graph break, why did you get 47 of them, and how do you systematically eliminate them to recover the expected speedup?
common_mistake: Treating graph breaks as harmless warnings. Each graph break forces torch.compile to exit the compiled region, fall back to Python eager execution, then re-enter compilation — destroying the fusion and memory planning benefits across break boundaries.
realistic_solution: A graph break is where TorchDynamo (the Python bytecode tracer) encounters an operation it cannot represent in the FX graph, forcing a fallback to eager mode. Your 47 breaks come from three sources: (1) Data-dependent control flow (`if token_count > max_len`) — the tracer cannot statically resolve the branch. Fix: use `torch.cond()` or guard with `torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic()`. (2) Python side effects (logging inside forward) — I/O operations cannot be captured in a computational graph. Fix: move logging outside the compiled region or use `torch._dynamo.allow_in_graph()`. (3) Third-party libra
napkin_math: Expected: 1 compiled graph, full operator fusion, ~2× speedup. Actual: 48 subgraphs. Average subgraph: ~3 operators (150 total / 48). Per-subgraph fusion benefit: minimal (need 5+ operators to amortize compile overhead). Cross-subgraph dispatch overhead: 48 × ~30μs = 1.4ms added. If baseline inference is 15ms, the overhead alone eats 10% of runtime — turning a 2× speedup into 1.1×.

title: The Autograd Overhead in Serving
scenario: A production inference service running a 7B model in PyTorch shows that `torch.cuda.memory_allocated()` reports 28GB, but the model weights are only 14GB in FP16. The serving code uses a simple `model(input)` call. No optimizer or gradient accumulation is present. Where is the extra 14GB coming from, and how do you reclaim it?
common_mistake: Assuming PyTorch automatically skips gradient tracking during inference. Without explicit `torch.no_grad()` or `torch.inference_mode()`, PyTorch's autograd engine records the computational graph for every forward pass, storing intermediate activations for potential backward passes that will never happen.
realistic_solution: The extra 14GB is autograd's activation stash. By default, PyTorch's autograd system records intermediate tensors (activations at each layer boundary) during the forward pass so gradients can be computed during backward. For a 7B transformer at batch=1 with sequence length 2048, activations consume roughly 1× the model size. Fix: wrap inference in `torch.inference_mode()` (preferred over `torch.no_grad()` because it also disables version counting and view tracking, saving additional memory and CPU overhead). This eliminates the activation stash entirely. Additional optimization: set `model.eva
napkin_math: 7B model in FP16 = 14GB weights. Without inference_mode: autograd stores ~1 activation tensor per layer. 32 transformer layers × ~430MB activations per layer ≈ 13.8GB. Total: 14 + 13.8 ≈ 28GB. With inference_mode: 14GB weights only. Memory savings: 50%. Latency improvement: ~5-15% from eliminated autograd bookkeeping CPU overhead.

title: The Runtime Selection for Multi-Model Serving
scenario: Your ML platform team must serve five models in production: (1) a BERT classifier updated weekly, (2) a 70B LLM with continuous batching, (3) a real-time object detection model at 30 FPS, (4) a recommendation model with a 500M-row embedding table, and (5) a diffusion model for image generation. The team wants to standardize on a single inference runtime. You argue this is a mistake. Design a runtime selection strategy, explaining which runtime or serving framework best fits each model and why a single runtime cannot serve all five optimally.
common_mistake: Standardizing on TensorRT or ONNX Runtime for everything. Each runtime makes architectural assumptions (static shapes, dense compute, single-model-per-GPU) that break down for at least one of these workloads.
realistic_solution: Each model has a different bottleneck that favors a different runtime: (1) BERT classifier → TensorRT: static shapes, INT8 quantizable, layer fusion gives 3-5× over eager PyTorch. Rebuilt weekly via TensorRT build cache. (2) 70B LLM → vLLM/TensorRT-LLM: needs PagedAttention for KV-cache, continuous batching for throughput, tensor parallelism across GPUs. Generic runtimes lack these LLM-specific memory management features. (3) Object detection at 30 FPS → TensorRT with CUDA Graphs: hard real-time latency constraint means you cannot tolerate jitter from dynamic batching or Python dispatch. Pre-b
napkin_math: BERT on TensorRT vs PyTorch eager: 1.2ms vs 5ms (4× faster). 70B LLM on vLLM vs naive PyTorch: 40 tokens/s vs 8 tokens/s per GPU (5× throughput from PagedAttention + continuous batching). Object detection: TensorRT + CUDA Graphs gives P99 < 15ms; PyTorch eager P99 = 45ms with jitter spikes to 100ms. RecSys: embedding lookup is 80% of latency — no amount of compute optimization helps if embeddings don't fit in VRAM. Diffusion: 50 denoising steps × 20ms per step = 1s; CUDA Graphs eliminate 50 × 15μs = 0.75ms dispatch overhead — small but compounds with batching.

title: The CUDA Graphs vs Dynamic Shapes Tradeoff
scenario: Your team captures the entire LLM decode loop as a CUDA Graph to eliminate kernel launch overhead, achieving a 30% latency reduction for fixed-length generation. But when you deploy to production, you discover that real user requests have highly variable output lengths (10 to 4,000 tokens) and variable batch sizes (continuous batching adds/removes requests mid-generation). The CUDA Graph breaks because it was captured with a fixed batch size and fixed sequence length. The naive fix — capturing a separate CUDA Graph for every possible (batch_size, seq_len) combination — would require thousands
common_mistake: Abandoning CUDA Graphs entirely and reverting to eager mode. Or trying to pad everything to max dimensions, which wastes compute proportional to the padding ratio.
realistic_solution: Use a bucketed CUDA Graph strategy combined with padding. (1) Bucket batch sizes into powers of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32) and sequence lengths into fixed strides (e.g., multiples of 128). This yields ~6 × ~32 = ~192 graph variants instead of thousands. (2) Pad inputs to the next bucket boundary. Wasted compute from padding is bounded: worst case is 2× for batch and ~128 tokens for sequence — in practice ~15-25% overhead. (3) Pre-capture all graph variants at startup during warmup. Each graph consumes ~50-100MB (kernel parameters, not model weights), so 192 graphs ≈ 10-20GB — manageable on 80GB GP
napkin_math: Eager mode decode step: 1.5ms (0.5ms compute + 1.0ms dispatch overhead). CUDA Graph decode step: 0.6ms (0.5ms compute + 0.1ms graph replay). With padding overhead (average 20% wasted compute): 0.7ms. Net speedup over eager: 1.5/0.7 = 2.1× per decode step. For a 500-token generation: eager = 750ms, CUDA Graph bucketed = 350ms. Memory cost: 192 graphs × 80MB = ~15GB. On 80GB GPU with 14GB model (FP16 7B): leaves 51GB for KV-cache — acceptable.

title: N:M Sparsity and Tensor Core Alignment
scenario: NVIDIA's Ampere and Hopper GPUs support 2:4 structured sparsity natively in their Tensor Cores. A colleague proposes using 1:4 sparsity instead, arguing that removing 75% of weights (vs 50%) should yield even greater speedup. Why does 1:4 sparsity NOT get hardware acceleration on these GPUs, and what is the fundamental constraint that makes 2:4 the specific ratio supported?
common_mistake: Assuming that any sparsity ratio can be accelerated, and that higher sparsity always means more speedup. The hardware supports exactly one sparsity pattern.
realistic_solution: The Tensor Core's sparse math unit is hardwired to process exactly 2 non-zero values out of every group of 4 elements. This is a fixed datapath design: the hardware reads 4 values, uses a 2-bit index to select which 2 are non-zero, and feeds those 2 values into the multiply-accumulate pipeline. Changing the ratio to 1:4 would require a different selection circuit and different storage format. The 2:4 pattern was chosen because it provides a clean 2x theoretical speedup (half the multiplications) while maintaining enough representational capacity that accuracy loss is manageable for most archit
napkin_math: Dense 4 elements: 4 multiplies, 4 values stored. 2:4 sparse: 2 multiplies + 2-bit index, 2 values + metadata stored. Memory reduction: ~50%. Compute reduction: exactly 2x on the supported datapath. 1:4 would save 75% compute but requires custom hardware that doesn't exist in current Tensor Cores.

title: The Compression Pipeline Ordering Trap
scenario: You plan to apply both pruning and quantization to a 13B parameter model before deploying it on a single A100. A junior engineer asks: 'Does it matter whether we prune first and then quantize, or quantize first and then prune?' Explain why the order matters and which sequence is generally preferred.
common_mistake: Treating compression techniques as independent and order-agnostic. 'Just apply both and the savings multiply.' In reality, each technique changes the weight distribution that the next technique must operate on.
realistic_solution: Order matters because each compression step alters the statistical properties of the weights. The standard pipeline is: (1) prune first, (2) fine-tune to recover accuracy, (3) quantize. Pruning removes weights based on magnitude or sensitivity analysis of the full-precision model, where the signal is clearest. If you quantize first, the reduced precision introduces noise into the weight magnitudes, making pruning decisions less reliable — you may prune weights that only appear small due to quantization rounding. Additionally, quantization calibration should be performed on the final weight dis
napkin_math: 13B model in FP16 = 26 GB. After 50% structured pruning + fine-tune: 6.5B effective params = 13 GB. After INT4 quantization: 6.5B × 0.5 bytes = 3.25 GB. If you quantize first to INT4 (6.5 GB) then prune 50%, you get 3.25 GB in size, but accuracy is typically 2-4% worse because pruning decisions were made on noisy quantized weights.

title: The Distillation Data Multiplier
scenario: You are distilling a 70B teacher LLM into a 7B student. The student was pre-trained on 2T tokens. Your team proposes distilling on 10B tokens of teacher-generated soft labels, arguing that soft labels carry richer information than hard labels. After distillation, the student underperforms a baseline 7B model fine-tuned on the same 10B tokens with hard labels. What went wrong, and how much distillation data do you actually need?
common_mistake: Believing that soft labels are so information-rich that a small distillation dataset suffices. 'The teacher's probability distribution over the full vocabulary encodes dark knowledge, so we need less data.' This overestimates the information content of soft labels relative to the capacity gap between teacher and student.
realistic_solution: The 10:1 parameter ratio (70B to 7B) means the student must learn to approximate the teacher's behavior across a vast input space. 10B tokens (0.5% of pre-training data) is insufficient to transfer the teacher's knowledge — the student only sees a tiny slice of the distribution. The soft labels help, but they cannot compensate for sparse coverage of the input space. Empirically, effective distillation for LLMs requires data on the order of the student's pre-training budget — typically 0.5-1x the original token count. Here, that means 1-2T tokens of distillation data, not 10B. The cost driver s
napkin_math: Teacher forward pass per token: $2 \times 70\text{B} = 140$ GFLOPs. At 10B tokens: $140 \times 10^{10} = 1.4 \times 10^{21}$ FLOPs = 1.4 ZFLOPs. At 1T tokens: 140 ZFLOPs. H100 at 40% MFU = 396 TFLOPS effective. 1T-token distillation: $140 \times 10^{21} / (396 \times 10^{12}) = 353,535$ GPU-seconds $\approx$ 98 GPU-hours $\approx$ $196 at $2/hr. The data volume, not GPU cost, is the real constraint — generating and storing 1T tokens of soft labels (full vocabulary distributions) is the bottleneck.

title: The Low-Rank Decomposition Latency Paradox
scenario: You decompose a $4096 \times 4096$ weight matrix in an edge-deployed transformer into two low-rank matrices of dimensions $4096 \times 256$ and $256 \times 4096$ (rank 256). This reduces parameters from 16.8M to 2.1M — an 8x reduction. However, inference latency on a Jetson Orin (with Tensor Cores) only drops by 15%, not the expected 8x. Explain why the FLOP reduction does not translate to proportional latency reduction on this hardware.
common_mistake: Equating parameter reduction with proportional speedup. 'We have 8x fewer parameters, so we should see ~8x speedup.' This confuses model size with compute time and ignores hardware execution realities.
realistic_solution: Three factors explain the gap. First, the FLOP reduction is not 8x — it's only 4x. The original matrix multiply costs $2 \times 4096^2 = 33.6$ GFLOPs. The two low-rank multiplies cost $2 \times (4096 \times 256 + 256 \times 4096) = 4.2$ GFLOPs — a 4x reduction (parameters and FLOPs scale differently for rank decomposition). Second, the two sequential matrix multiplies have lower arithmetic intensity than one large multiply. The original $4096 \times 4096$ matmul has high arithmetic intensity and saturates Tensor Cores; each low-rank multiply ($4096 \times 256$) is a tall-skinny or skinny-tall
napkin_math: Original: $4096 \times 4096$ matmul = 33.6M FLOPs, one kernel launch, activations read once. Decomposed: $4096 \times 256$ + $256 \times 4096$ = 2.1M + 2.1M = 4.2M FLOPs, two kernel launches. FLOP ratio: 8x (parameter-wise) but only 4x (compute-wise). Arithmetic intensity of $4096 \times 256$ matmul: much lower than $4096 \times 4096$. On Orin with 275 TOPS INT8 but only ~40% utilization on skinny matmuls → effective throughput drops from ~110 TOPS to ~27 TOPS. Net real speedup: roughly $33.6/4.2 \times (27/110) \approx 2\times$, explaining the ~15-20% latency improvement (the matmul is only p

title: The Activation Sparsity Mirage in MoE
scenario: Your team observes that a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model with 8 experts (top-2 routing) has extremely high activation sparsity — at any given token, 75% of expert parameters are inactive. A systems engineer proposes exploiting this by only loading the 2 active experts' weights into GPU memory per token, keeping the other 6 experts in CPU DRAM. They claim this will reduce GPU memory usage by 75%. In production serving with continuous batching at 200 QPS, this approach causes P99 latency to spike to 5x the baseline. Diagnose the failure.
common_mistake: Conflating per-token sparsity with per-batch sparsity. 'If each token only uses 2 of 8 experts, we only need to load 2 experts at a time.' This ignores what happens when you batch hundreds of tokens together.
realistic_solution: The sparsity is per-token, not per-batch. With continuous batching at 200 QPS and batch sizes of 32-64 tokens, every batch contains tokens routed to different experts. With top-2 routing across 8 experts, the probability that all 8 experts are needed in a batch of just 20 tokens is already >99% (birthday problem: each token independently picks 2 of 8 experts). So the 'sparse' MoE is effectively dense at the batch level. The CPU-offloading scheme must load all 8 experts from DRAM for nearly every batch, incurring PCIe transfer latency (~12 GB/s on PCIe Gen4) per batch. For a model where each ex
napkin_math: 8 experts, top-2 routing. Per-token: 2/8 = 25% active (75% sparse). Per-batch (n=32 tokens, each picks 2 of 8): probability expert $i$ is NOT picked by any token = $(6/8)^{32} \approx 0.0001$. Probability ALL 8 experts needed: $\approx (1 - 0.0001)^8 \approx 99.9\%$. At batch size 32, activation sparsity is effectively 0% at the memory level. PCIe bottleneck: 120 GB at 12 GB/s = 10s per swap, vs. target latency of ~50ms per batch.

title: The NAS Latency Predictor Blind Spot
scenario: You are using hardware-aware NAS to find an optimal architecture for a mobile vision model targeting a Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 NPU. The NAS uses a latency lookup table (LUT) built by profiling individual operators (convolutions, depthwise separable convolutions, attention blocks) in isolation. After 500 GPU-hours of search, the top architecture achieves the predicted 8ms latency on the NPU — but only when run in isolation. When deployed inside the full camera pipeline (which also runs ISP processing, object tracking, and UI rendering on the same SoC), actual latency balloons to 22ms. The N
common_mistake: Blaming thermal throttling alone. 'The SoC gets hot under full load.' Thermal effects contribute but are not the primary cause, and they would affect all architectures equally — they wouldn't make this specific NAS-found architecture worse than alternatives.
realistic_solution: The NAS latency predictor has a fundamental blind spot: it profiles operators in isolation on the NPU, but the production deployment shares the SoC's resources with other workloads. Three specific failures occur: (1) Memory bandwidth contention — the NAS-selected architecture uses depthwise separable convolutions with large spatial dimensions that are memory-bandwidth-bound. In isolation, they get full LPDDR5 bandwidth (~50 GB/s). When the ISP and GPU are also active, available bandwidth drops to ~15 GB/s, and memory-bound operators slow down 3x while compute-bound operators are unaffected. A
napkin_math: Isolated NPU: 50 GB/s bandwidth, 26 TOPS INT8. Memory-bound depthwise conv at batch=1: $\frac{\text{data volume}}{50\text{ GB/s}}$. Under contention (15 GB/s): 3.3x slower. NAS-selected arch: 60% memory-bound ops. Isolated: $(0.6 \times 5\text{ms}) + (0.4 \times 3\text{ms}) = 4.2\text{ms}$ compute + overhead. Under contention: $(0.6 \times 16.5\text{ms}) + (0.4 \times 3\text{ms}) = 11.1\text{ms}$ compute. Alt arch with dense convs (90% compute-bound): under contention $(0.1 \times 5\text{ms}) + (0.9 \times 6\text{ms}) = 5.9\text{ms}$ — nearly unaffected.

title: The Structured Pruning Recovery Budget
scenario: You structurally prune 30% of attention heads and 25% of FFN intermediate dimensions from a 13B LLM, reducing it to ~9B effective parameters. Perplexity increases from 6.2 to 8.1. You fine-tune on 10B tokens and recover to 6.8 — but your target is 6.4 or better. Your manager asks whether more fine-tuning data will close the gap, or whether you pruned too aggressively. How do you determine which is the case, and what is the systematic way to set the pruning ratio for a given accuracy target?
common_mistake: Throwing more data at the problem without diagnostic analysis. 'We just need to fine-tune longer.' Or conversely: 'We pruned too much — try 15% instead.' Both are guessing without a principled framework.
realistic_solution: Use a scaling-law-guided approach. Plot the fine-tuning recovery curve: perplexity vs. fine-tuning tokens at the current pruning ratio (30% heads, 25% FFN). If the curve is still decreasing meaningfully at 10B tokens, more data will help — the model has capacity to recover but hasn't converged. If the curve has plateaued (perplexity flat from 8B to 10B tokens), you've hit the capacity floor of the pruned architecture and no amount of data will reach 6.4. To set the pruning ratio systematically: (1) Prune at multiple ratios (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%) and measure immediate perplexity degradation. (2)
napkin_math: Fine-tuning cost per data point: $6 \times 9\text{B} \times D$ FLOPs for the pruned model. At 10B tokens: $6 \times 9 \times 10^9 \times 10 \times 10^9 = 5.4 \times 10^{20}$ FLOPs. On H100 at 40% MFU (396 TFLOPS): $5.4 \times 10^{20} / (3.96 \times 10^{14}) \approx 1.36 \times 10^6$s $\approx$ 378 GPU-hours $\approx$ $756. Running the 4-point Pareto sweep (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%) at 2B tokens each: 4 × 75 GPU-hours = 300 GPU-hours = $600. The diagnostic sweep ($600) costs less than one more blind fine-tuning run ($756) and gives you the answer definitively.

title: The Real-Time Compression Stack Design
scenario: You must deploy a 7B parameter language model on a Jetson AGX Orin (64 GB unified memory, 275 TOPS INT8) for a robotics application with a hard real-time constraint: 50ms per inference call (single token generation during decode). The model in FP16 is 14 GB and decode latency is 180ms. Design a complete compression pipeline — specifying techniques, their order, target ratios, and expected accuracy impact — to meet the latency constraint while maximizing output quality.
common_mistake: Applying a single technique (e.g., INT4 quantization alone) and hoping it's enough, or applying multiple techniques in arbitrary order without reasoning about composition effects.
realistic_solution:
Start from the bottleneck analysis. At decode (batch=1, single token), the operation is memory-bandwidth-bound: the GPU must stream all weights from memory once per token. On the Orin, unified memory bandwidth is ~205 GB/s. Decode latency $\approx$ model_size / bandwidth. At FP16 (14 GB): 14/205 = 68ms — already over budget even at theoretical peak (real throughput ~70% → 98ms, close to the observed 180ms with overhead). Target: 50ms → need model_size < 205 × 0.05 × 0.7 = ~7.2 GB after compression.

Pipeline design (ordered):
1. **Structured pruning** (20% of attention heads, 15% of FFN): redu
napkin_math: Baseline: 7B params × 2 bytes (FP16) = 14 GB. Bandwidth: 205 GB/s × 70% = 143 GB/s effective. Decode latency: 14 / 143 = 98ms (matches ~180ms observed with kernel overhead). After pruning (5.6B) + INT4 (0.5 B/param): 2.8 GB weights. Decode: 2.8 / 143 = 19.6ms. With activation memory + overhead: ~30ms. Margin to 50ms SLA: 20ms (40% headroom).

title: The Distillation vs. Pruning Fleet Economics
scenario:
You serve a 70B LLM at 10,000 QPS across 400 H100 GPUs (tensor-parallel across 4 GPUs per replica = 100 replicas). Your VP asks you to cut the serving fleet cost by 50% without dropping quality below 95% of the current model's benchmark scores. You have two proposals:

**Proposal A:** Structurally prune the 70B model to 35B, fine-tune, and serve on 2 GPUs per replica.
**Proposal B:** Distill the 70B into a purpose-trained 7B model and serve on 1 GPU per replica.

Which proposal do you recommend, and what are the hidden costs and risks of each?
common_mistake: Comparing only the serving GPU count without considering one-time compression costs, accuracy risk, latency profile changes, or operational complexity.
realistic_solution:
**Proposal A (Prune 70B → 35B):** Serving: 2 GPUs/replica × 100 replicas = 200 H100s (50% reduction — meets target). One-time cost: structured pruning + fine-tuning on ~100B tokens ≈ 10,000 GPU-hours ≈ $20,000. Quality: structured pruning of 50% typically retains 96-98% of benchmark quality with good fine-tuning. Risk: moderate. Latency: prefill is ~2x faster (fewer params), decode is ~2x faster (less memory to stream). Operational complexity: same serving infrastructure, just fewer GPUs per replica.

**Proposal B (Distill 70B → 7B):** Serving: 1 GPU/replica. At 10,000 QPS, a 7B model on a sin
napkin_math:
Current fleet: 400 H100s × $2/hr = $800/hr = $7M/year.
Proposal A: 200 H100s = $3.5M/year. One-time: $20K. Payback: 1 day.
Proposal B: 100 H100s = $1.75M/year. One-time: $400K. Payback: 50 days.
But if Proposal B misses the 95% quality bar → $400K wasted + re-do with Proposal A → 2 months delay. Expected value calculation: if P(quality ≥ 95%) = 60% for distillation, EV(B) = 0.6 × $1.75M + 0.4 × ($3.5M + $400K) = $2.61M/year effective. EV(A) = $3.5M/year. Proposal A is better unless P(quality) > 82%.

title: The Full-Stack Compression Audit
scenario: You inherit a production serving system for a 30B parameter LLM. The previous team applied the following compression techniques in this order: (1) LoRA fine-tuning (rank 64) on domain data, (2) unstructured magnitude pruning to 40% sparsity, (3) GPTQ 4-bit quantization, (4) knowledge distillation from the original 30B into the compressed model. The system works but quality is significantly below expectations — 12% worse on domain benchmarks than a comparable 7B model trained from scratch. You are asked to audit the compression pipeline and redesign it from first principles. Identify every mist
common_mistake: Fixing one issue (e.g., 'just change unstructured to structured pruning') without recognizing that the entire pipeline ordering and technique selection is fundamentally broken in multiple ways.
realistic_solution:
There are at least 5 critical errors in the existing pipeline:

**Error 1: LoRA before pruning.** LoRA adapters modify the weight matrices by adding low-rank updates ($W + BA$). Pruning after LoRA means you're pruning the merged weights, which may remove the exact dimensions that LoRA learned to update. The LoRA adaptation is partially destroyed. Fix: prune first, then apply LoRA to the pruned model.

**Error 2: Unstructured pruning.** 40% unstructured sparsity provides zero acceleration on H100 Tensor Cores (only 2:4 structured sparsity is hardware-accelerated). The weights are still stored a
napkin_math:
Current pipeline quality budget: Each step compounds error. LoRA fine-tune: +2% domain accuracy. Unstructured pruning (40%): -8% accuracy, 0% speedup. GPTQ on pruned distribution: -5% (vs typical -2% on clean weights). Dead-end distillation: ~0% recovery. Net: -11% domain accuracy = matches the observed 12% degradation.

Redesigned pipeline: Structured pruning (30%): -4% accuracy. Distillation fine-tune: +3% recovery. LoRA domain adapt: +2%. GPTQ on clean weights: -2%. Net: -1% from baseline. Plus actual speedup: 30% fewer params as dense + 4-bit = model size drops from 60 GB (FP16) to ~10.5 G

title: The Equalized Odds Constraint
scenario: Your credit scoring model must satisfy 'equalized odds' across gender groups. A colleague says this just means the model should have the same accuracy for men and women. Why is that description incomplete, and what does equalized odds actually require?
common_mistake: Conflating equalized odds with equal accuracy. A model can have 90% accuracy for both groups but still violate equalized odds if the error types differ.
realistic_solution: Equalized odds requires that the True Positive Rate (TPR) and the False Positive Rate (FPR) are equal across protected groups, conditioned on the true label. Equal accuracy can hide compensating errors: Group A might have high TPR but high FPR, while Group B has low TPR and low FPR — same accuracy, but Group A faces more false accusations while Group B is systematically denied. Equalized odds ensures that both the benefit of correct approval (TPR) and the harm of incorrect denial (FPR) are distributed equally. From a systems perspective, this means your evaluation pipeline must compute confusi
napkin_math: Model with 90% accuracy for both groups. Group A: TPR=0.95, FPR=0.15. Group B: TPR=0.80, FPR=0.05. Accuracy is balanced but equalized odds violation = |0.95−0.80| = 0.15 for TPR and |0.15−0.05| = 0.10 for FPR. Both exceed typical thresholds (0.05).

title: The Fairness Impossibility Tradeoff
scenario: Your team's loan approval model must satisfy both demographic parity (equal approval rates across groups) and equalized odds (equal TPR and FPR across groups). A fairness researcher on the team says satisfying both simultaneously is mathematically impossible except in trivial cases. Why?
common_mistake: Assuming you can tune thresholds to satisfy all fairness criteria at once. The impossibility is mathematical, not an engineering limitation — no amount of compute or data fixes it.
realistic_solution: The Chouldechova-Kleinberg impossibility theorem (2016-2017) proves that when base rates differ between groups (e.g., different default rates across demographics), you cannot simultaneously satisfy calibration, equalized odds, and demographic parity. Intuitively: if Group A has a 5% default rate and Group B has 15%, equalizing approval rates (demographic parity) forces different score thresholds per group, which breaks equalized odds. Conversely, enforcing equal error rates across groups with different base rates necessarily produces unequal approval rates. This means every fairness-aware syst
napkin_math: Group A base rate: 5% default, Group B: 15% default. If demographic parity requires 80% approval for both → Group A threshold set lower (easy to meet), Group B threshold set higher (harder to meet). Group A FPR will be much lower than Group B FPR, violating equalized odds.

title: The Disaggregated Evaluation Pipeline
scenario: Your image classification model achieves 94% accuracy on the test set. Management is satisfied and wants to ship. You propose running disaggregated evaluation first. The test set has 10 demographic subgroups and 50 intersectional subgroups (age × gender × skin tone). Calculate the minimum sample size per subgroup needed to detect a 5-percentage-point accuracy gap with 80% power, and estimate the total evaluation dataset size required.
common_mistake: Assuming the existing test set is large enough. With 10K test images and 50 intersectional subgroups, some subgroups may have fewer than 50 samples — far too few for statistical significance.
realistic_solution: Using a two-proportion z-test, detecting a 5pp difference (94% vs 89%) with 80% power and α=0.05 requires approximately 780 samples per subgroup. For 50 intersectional subgroups: 50 × 780 = 39,000 minimum test samples, assuming perfect balance. Real-world subgroup distributions are highly skewed (intersectional minorities may be <1% of data), so you need to oversample rare subgroups. Practical minimum: ~100K labeled evaluation images with stratified sampling. The systems implication: disaggregated evaluation is a data engineering problem. You need a metadata-rich evaluation dataset with demogr
napkin_math: Sample size for two-proportion test: $n \approx \frac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2 \times (p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2))}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$. With $p_1=0.94$, $p_2=0.89$, $z_{0.025}=1.96$, $z_{0.2}=0.84$: $n \approx \frac{(1.96+0.84)^2 \times (0.0564 + 0.0979)}{0.0025} \approx 780$ per group. 50 subgroups × 780 = 39,000 samples minimum.

title: The Fairness Monitoring Compute Budget
scenario: Your content recommendation system serves 100M daily active users across 8 demographic groups. Compliance requires computing equalized odds metrics every hour. Each fairness evaluation requires joining prediction logs with outcome labels (clicks within 30 minutes) and computing per-group confusion matrices. Estimate the hourly data volume, the storage for a rolling 30-day audit window, and the compute cost of this fairness monitoring pipeline.
common_mistake: Treating fairness monitoring as a lightweight dashboarding task. At 100M DAU, the log join alone is a significant data engineering workload, and the 30-day audit retention creates substantial storage requirements.
realistic_solution: At 100M DAU with ~20 recommendations per user per day, that is 2B predictions/day or ~83M predictions/hour. Each prediction log record (user_id, item_id, prediction_score, timestamp, demographic_group, outcome_label) is ~200 bytes. Hourly data: 83M × 200B ≈ 16.6 GB. The outcome join requires a temporal window join — matching predictions with click events that arrive up to 30 minutes later — which means buffering ~16.6 GB of predictions and joining against a stream of click events. This is a streaming join (Flink/Spark Structured Streaming), not a simple SQL query. Rolling 30-day audit: 16.6 GB
napkin_math: 100M DAU × 20 recs/user = 2B predictions/day. Hourly: 83M records × 200 bytes = 16.6 GB/hr. Monthly audit window: 16.6 × 24 × 30 = 11,952 GB ≈ 12 TB. At $0.023/GB/month (S3): $276/month for storage alone. Streaming join cluster: ~4 instances × $0.192/hr (m5.xlarge) × 720 hrs = $553/month. Total fairness monitoring overhead: ~$830/month — small relative to the serving fleet, but non-zero and often unbudgeted.

title: The Fairness-Accuracy Pareto Frontier
scenario: Your hiring recommendation model has 85% accuracy with no fairness constraints. When you add a demographic parity constraint (equal recommendation rates across gender), accuracy drops to 78%. The VP of Engineering says a 7-point accuracy drop is unacceptable and asks you to 'find a better algorithm that doesn't have this tradeoff.' Analyze why the VP's request is fundamentally misguided, and propose a systems-level approach to navigate the fairness-accuracy Pareto frontier.
common_mistake: Agreeing to 'find a better algorithm' as if the tradeoff is a software bug. Or conversely, insisting on 78% accuracy as the only option. Both miss the Pareto frontier framing.
realistic_solution: The fairness-accuracy tradeoff is not an algorithm bug — it is a Pareto frontier. When base rates differ across groups, enforcing equal outcomes necessarily costs accuracy (this follows from the impossibility theorem). However, the frontier is not a single point — it is a curve. The systems approach: (1) Map the full Pareto frontier by sweeping the fairness constraint strength (λ in the Lagrangian formulation), producing ~20 candidate models from (85%, no fairness) to (78%, full demographic parity). (2) Build a model selection dashboard that lets stakeholders visualize the accuracy-fairness cu
napkin_math: Pareto sweep: 20 constraint values × 4-hour training runs = 80 GPU-hours for frontier mapping. With threshold post-processing, typical recovery is 2-3pp: (78% + 2.5pp) = 80.5% accuracy at full demographic parity. The VP's 'acceptable' operating point might be (82%, partial fairness with disparate impact ratio > 0.8), which is findable on the frontier without any algorithmic change.

title: The Intersectional Subgroup Explosion
scenario: A regulatory audit requires your loan model to demonstrate fairness across intersectional subgroups — not just gender or race independently, but combinations like 'Black women over 55' or 'Hispanic men under 25.' Your model has 4 protected attributes (gender: 2, race: 6, age-bracket: 5, disability: 2). The auditor wants equalized odds computed for every intersectional combination. Analyze the computational and statistical challenges this creates, and diagnose why naive subgroup enumeration fails.
common_mistake: Computing fairness metrics for all 2 × 6 × 5 × 2 = 120 subgroups and reporting the results. This produces a table that looks thorough but is statistically meaningless for most cells.
realistic_solution: 120 subgroups create two compounding problems. (1) Statistical: with a 100K test set, average subgroup size is 833 samples, but distribution is highly skewed. 'Disabled Hispanic women 55+' might have 12 samples — far below the ~780 needed for a 5pp gap test. You cannot make fairness claims about subgroups with insufficient samples. (2) Multiple comparisons: testing equalized odds across 120 subgroups at α=0.05 yields ~6 false positives by chance alone. Without Bonferroni or FDR correction, you will 'discover' disparities that don't exist. The systems solution: (a) Use Gerrymandering-aware fair
napkin_math: 4 attributes → 2×6×5×2 = 120 subgroups. 100K test set → 833 avg samples/subgroup. But top-10% subgroups hold ~60% of data; bottom-30% have <100 samples each (~36 subgroups statistically underpowered). Bonferroni correction: α_adjusted = 0.05/120 = 0.00042 → requires ~2,400 samples per subgroup for same power. Total needed: 120 × 2,400 = 288K samples — nearly 3× the test set.

title: The EU AI Act Compliance Pipeline
scenario: Your company deploys a high-risk AI system (credit scoring) in the EU. The EU AI Act requires: (1) a conformity assessment before deployment, (2) continuous post-market monitoring, (3) full traceability of training data and model decisions, and (4) the ability to provide explanations to affected individuals upon request. Your current ML infrastructure has none of these capabilities. Analyze what systems infrastructure you need to build, and identify which requirement is the most expensive to retrofit.
common_mistake: Treating compliance as a documentation exercise — writing policies and filing paperwork. The EU AI Act requires operational infrastructure, not just documents. Or focusing only on explainability while ignoring the data lineage requirement.
realistic_solution: Each requirement maps to concrete systems infrastructure: (1) Conformity assessment → automated bias testing pipeline (disaggregated evaluation across protected groups), performance benchmarking suite, dataset documentation (datasheets), model cards generated automatically from training metadata. (2) Post-market monitoring → production fairness monitoring (hourly metric computation, drift detection on per-group accuracy, automated alerting when metrics breach thresholds), incident reporting pipeline. (3) Traceability → data lineage system (tracking every training sample through preprocessing,
napkin_math: Traceability cost estimate for a credit model serving 10M decisions/day: Each decision audit record (input features + model version + prediction + explanation hash) ≈ 2 KB. Daily: 10M × 2 KB = 20 GB. 7-year retention (regulatory): 20 GB × 365 × 7 = 51.1 TB. At $0.023/GB/month (S3): $14K/year for storage alone. Training data lineage for 1B training records with full provenance: ~500 GB metadata. Total infrastructure: ~$50-100K/year — dwarfed by the cost of non-compliance (up to 6% of global turnover).

title: The Bias Drift Silent Failure
scenario: Your content moderation model passed all fairness audits at deployment: equalized odds violations were below 2% across all demographic groups. Six months later, a journalist investigation reveals the model is now flagging content from one minority group at 3× the rate of the majority group. Your aggregate accuracy metrics never triggered an alert — they actually improved slightly over the period. Evaluate how this silent fairness degradation occurred despite passing the initial audit, and design a monitoring system that would have caught it.
common_mistake: Blaming the initial audit as inadequate or assuming someone changed the model. The model weights haven't changed — the world changed around it.
realistic_solution: This is a fairness-specific form of silent degradation caused by demographic distribution shift. Several mechanisms can cause it: (1) Content shift: the minority group's posting patterns changed (new slang, cultural events, different topics) while training data doesn't reflect this — the model's learned features now correlate with group membership rather than actual policy violations. (2) Feedback loop amplification: the model's initial (small, within-threshold) bias caused slightly more content from the minority group to be flagged → human reviewers, primed by the model's suggestions, confirm
napkin_math: Drift rate: 2% equalized odds gap → 8% over 6 months = 1pp/month. With weekly fairness evaluation and a 3-sigma alert threshold on the month-over-month delta (baseline noise ~0.3pp/week), the system would detect the trend at month 2-3 (4-5% gap), 3-4 months before the journalist. Cost of weekly disaggregated evaluation on a 50K held-out set across 8 groups: ~$50/week in compute (confusion matrix computation is cheap). Cost of not detecting: reputation damage, regulatory fines (up to 6% of turnover under EU AI Act), class-action risk.

title: The DRO Training Cost Multiplier
scenario: Your team proposes replacing standard ERM (Empirical Risk Minimization) training with Group DRO (Distributionally Robust Optimization) to improve worst-group accuracy on a large language model fine-tuning job. The 7B model currently trains in 48 hours on 8 A100s using ERM. Evaluate the compute and systems costs of switching to Group DRO. The PM wants to know: 'How much more expensive is fairness-aware training, and is there a cheaper alternative that achieves 80% of the benefit?'
common_mistake: Assuming DRO is a drop-in loss function replacement with negligible overhead. Or assuming the cost is simply 'k× more' where k is the number of groups.
realistic_solution: Group DRO has three hidden costs beyond the loss function change: (1) Per-group loss tracking: at each step, you compute loss separately for each group, then upweight the worst-performing group. With g groups and a large batch, this requires group-aware batch construction — every batch must contain sufficient samples from every group, which conflicts with random shuffling and can reduce effective batch size. For rare groups (<5% of data), you may need to oversample 4-10×, effectively increasing epoch length. (2) Convergence slowdown: DRO's adversarial reweighting creates a min-max optimization
napkin_math: ERM baseline: 48 hrs × 8 A100s = 384 GPU-hours. At $2/GPU-hr: $768. Group DRO: 384 × 2 (convergence) × 3 (hyperparam search) = 2,304 GPU-hours = $4,608 — a 6× increase. Threshold adjustment: 0 additional training hours + 1 hr evaluation sweep = $16. Importance-weighted ERM: 384 × 1.3 = 499 GPU-hours = $998. For 80% of DRO's benefit at 1.3× cost vs 6×, importance weighting + threshold adjustment is the clear winner.

title: The End-to-End Fairness Infrastructure
scenario: You are the Staff engineer tasked with designing a company-wide Responsible AI infrastructure that supports 50 ML models across 12 teams. Requirements: (1) every model must pass disaggregated fairness evaluation before deployment (gate), (2) production models must have continuous fairness monitoring with automated rollback if metrics breach thresholds, (3) full artifact provenance — for any production prediction, an auditor must be able to trace back to the exact training data, preprocessing steps, model version, and fairness evaluation results, (4) the system must support the EU AI Act's 'rig
common_mistake: Designing a centralized fairness service that becomes a bottleneck for all 50 models, or treating this as a 'fairness library' that each team imports. Both fail at scale — the first creates organizational bottleneck, the second creates inconsistency.
realistic_solution: Architecture: a platform approach with four layers. (1) Fairness SDK (client-side): a lightweight library each team integrates into their training and serving pipelines. It handles demographic annotation propagation, per-group metric computation, and structured logging. This runs in each team's infrastructure — no central bottleneck. (2) Fairness Gate Service (CI/CD integration): a gRPC service that receives evaluation reports from the SDK and enforces deployment policies. Before any model can be promoted to production (via the model registry), it must submit a fairness evaluation report. The
napkin_math: 50 models × avg 1M predictions/day = 50M audit records/day × 2KB each = 100 GB/day. 7-year retention: 100 GB × 365 × 7 = 255 TB. Lineage metadata per training run: ~10 GB (data manifest + preprocessing DAG + metrics). 50 models × 12 retrains/year = 600 runs × 10 GB = 6 TB/year. Fairness gate service: <100 RPS (50 models × few deployments/day) — a single pod handles this. Monitoring pipeline: 50M predictions/day ÷ 86400 = ~580 events/sec — a modest Flink cluster (4 TaskManagers). SHAP pre-computation: 50 models × 100 patterns × ~2 sec/explanation = ~2.8 hrs/day on a single GPU. Total infrastruc

title: Real-Time Aggregates Latency Tradeoff
scenario: Your fraud detection system has a strict 50ms p99 latency SLA (Inference: 25ms, API/Routing: 10ms, leaving 15ms for Feature Retrieval). You must add a critical feature: 'user transaction amount z-score over the last 30 days'. An active user averages 1,000 transactions/month (approx 50KB of raw event data). You are evaluating two feature computation architectures: (A) On-the-fly: Store raw events in a Redis list, fetch all 50KB at inference, and compute the z-score in the serving API. (B) Streaming: Use Flink to maintain sliding window aggregates (count, sum, sum_of_squares) and continuously ov
common_mistake: Assuming on-the-fly compute only adds CPU latency and ignoring the p99 network I/O cost of fetching 50KB of raw events per request.
realistic_solution: Approach A (On-the-fly) decomposes latency into: Redis lookup (1-2ms) + Network transfer of 50KB (approx 4-8ms at p99 depending on network topology and Redis serialization) + CPU JSON parsing and math (5-10ms). Total feature latency approaches 10-20ms, frequently violating the 15ms budget at p99. However, it requires no streaming infrastructure and avoids write-amplification. Approach B (Streaming) decomposes latency into: Redis lookup of a single pre-computed float (1ms) + Network transfer of 8 bytes (<1ms) + CPU parsing (<1ms). Total latency is ~2ms, safely within budget. The systemic tradeo
napkin_math: On-the-fly latency: Fetching 50KB over 10Gbps takes ~0.04ms theoretical, but Redis RESP protocol serialization + TCP overhead at p99 adds ~5ms. CPU parsing 1000 JSON events = ~8ms. Total = ~13ms (extremely tight for 15ms budget). Streaming write load: 10M active users * 33 tx/day = 330M tx/day = ~3,800 writes/sec to Redis (easily handled). Flink state size for 30 days raw events: 10M users * 50KB = 500GB state (expensive memory requirement).

title: Fraud Detection Feature Latency Bottleneck
scenario: A real-time fraud detection pipeline processes 5,000 QPS with a strict end-to-end SLA of 100ms P99. Currently, the P99 latency is 180ms. The breakdown is: 40ms to fetch 200 static user features from a Redis feature store, 90ms to compute 50 real-time sliding-window features (e.g., 'transaction volume in last 10 mins') from raw Kafka events, 20ms to serialize/assemble the feature vector via JSON, and 30ms for XGBoost model inference. These steps execute sequentially.
common_mistake: Attempting to optimize the XGBoost model using quantization or TensorRT, which only addresses 30ms of the 180ms total.
realistic_solution: To meet the 100ms SLA, we must decompose and attack the feature pipeline bottlenecks. First, execute the Redis feature fetch and the real-time feature computation in parallel, which immediately drops the feature gathering time from 130ms to max(40ms, 90ms) = 90ms. Second, optimize the real-time computation: instead of calculating sliding windows on-the-fly from raw Kafka events, use a stream processor (like Flink) to pre-aggregate tumbling windows and materialize them into a fast KV store. This turns a 90ms complex streaming join/aggregation into a simple 10ms lookup. Third, replace the JSON s
napkin_math: Current P99: 40ms (Redis) + 90ms (Kafka RT) + 20ms (JSON) + 30ms (Inference) = 180ms. Step 1 (Parallelize): max(40, 90) + 20 + 30 = 140ms. Step 2 (Pre-aggregate RT): max(40, 10) + 20 + 30 = 90ms. Step 3 (Protobuf): max(40, 10) + 5 + 30 = 75ms. Final P99 is 75ms, safely below the 100ms SLA.

title: High-Fanout Item Feature Retrieval
scenario: Your recommendation serving system scores 1,000 candidate items per user request at 2,000 QPS. The end-to-end P99 latency is 320ms against a 150ms SLA. Profiling reveals: Candidate Generation takes 20ms, User Feature Fetch takes 10ms, Item Feature Fetch takes 250ms, Cross-feature computation takes 10ms, and Model Scoring takes 30ms. The 250ms item fetch is caused by 10 concurrent threads making 100 sequential gRPC calls each to a Redis-backed feature store. Network RTT is 0.5ms.
common_mistake: Reducing the candidate pool from 1,000 to 200 to cut down fetch times, which severely degrades recommendation quality.
realistic_solution: The 250ms bottleneck is dominated by network RTT overhead due to an N+1 query anti-pattern in feature retrieval. We decompose the latency: 100 sequential calls per thread * (0.5ms RTT + 2.0ms DB processing) = 250ms. Optimization 1: Implement batched retrievals (MGET). Group the 1,000 item IDs into 5 parallel batches of 200 items. Optimization 2: Introduce an in-memory L1 cache (e.g., Caffeine) on the serving nodes for item features, as item features typically mutate slowly compared to user features. With an 80% cache hit rate, we only need to fetch 200 items over the network per request, furth
napkin_math: Current Item Fetch: 100 sequential calls * 2.5ms = 250ms. Optimized Fetch with MGET: 1,000 items split into 5 parallel gRPC calls. Network RTT (0.5ms) + Redis MGET processing for 200 keys (~4.5ms) = 5ms total latency. With 80% L1 cache hit rate, we only fetch 200 keys total, reducing Redis load and dropping fetch latency to ~2ms. New End-to-End P99: 20ms (Gen) + 10ms (User) + 5ms (Item Fetch) + 10ms (Cross) + 30ms (Model) = 75ms. This represents a 76% reduction in latency.

title: DLRM Iteration Loop Optimization
scenario: Your team develops a 50GB DLRM (Recommendation) model. The current ML workflow takes 48 hours per experiment: 12h data preprocessing (CPU), 24h training on 1xA100 (80GB), and 12h offline evaluation on 500M historical records. With 10 engineers each running 3 experiments per week, iteration speed is cripplingly slow and cloud compute budgets are constantly exceeded. You must architect a new experiment tracking and execution pipeline to reduce end-to-end iteration time to under 10 hours without exceeding the current 720 A100-hours/week compute budget.
common_mistake: Scaling to 8xA100s for training without addressing the 24 hours spent in CPU-bound data preprocessing and exhaustive offline evaluation.
realistic_solution: To hit the <10h target while bounding costs, architect a 3-stage optimized workflow. 1) Data Preprocessing: Move from on-the-fly CPU processing to a materialized feature store with GPU-accelerated ETL (e.g., NVTabular). This caches reusable data and cuts the 12h step to 1h. 2) Training: Scale from 1xA100 to 4xA100 using hybrid parallelism (data parallel for MLPs, model parallel for massive embedding tables). This reduces 24h to ~7h (assuming 85% scaling efficiency). 3) Evaluation: Implement a tiered evaluation funnel tied to your experiment tracking system. Instead of evaluating all 500M recor
napkin_math: Current: 10 eng * 3 exp * 24h = 720 A100-hours/week. New workflow wall-clock: 1h data + 7h train + 1.5h eval = 9.5h. Compute per exp: 4xA100 * 7h = 28 A100-hours. Weekly compute: 30 runs * 28 = 840 A100-hours. To stay within the 720h budget, the experiment tracking system enforces early-stopping on the bottom 30% of runs based on epoch 1 validation metrics: 30 runs * 28h * 0.7 (effective completion rate) = 588 A100-hours/week. Budget maintained, wall-clock reduced 5x.

title: RAG Pipeline Evaluation Architecture
scenario: You manage the ML workflow for a RAG system consisting of document chunking, an embedding model, a Vector DB, and a GPT-4 LLM. A full experiment evaluates a 10,000-query golden dataset to measure retrieval recall and generation accuracy. Currently, each experiment takes 8 hours and costs $300 in LLM API calls. Engineers are bottlenecked when tweaking just the chunking strategy or prompts. Architect a modular experiment tracking and evaluation workflow to reduce average iteration time to <1 hour and cost to <$50 per run.
common_mistake: Running the full 10k query pipeline concurrently to solve the latency bottleneck, which completely ignores the $300 per-run API cost constraint.
realistic_solution: Architect a component-aware Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for experiment tracking. 1) Decouple Retrieval Evaluation from Generation Evaluation. If an engineer changes chunking/embeddings, evaluate *only* the retriever (Recall@K) against golden contexts without invoking the LLM. 2) Implement a caching layer using experiment artifact hashing. Unchanged steps (e.g., embeddings when only the prompt changes) are skipped. 3) For Generation Evaluation (prompt tweaks), introduce subset testing. Evaluate on a stratified subset of 1,000 difficult queries first (Tier 1). The full 10k dataset (Tier 2) is o
napkin_math: Baseline: 10,000 queries * $0.03/query = $300 and 8 hours (approx 3 QPS effective). Optimized Workflow (Retriever tweak): 0 LLM calls = $0, runs locally in 5 mins. Optimized Workflow (Prompt tweak): evaluate on 1k subset = 1,000 * $0.03 = $30. At 20 QPS (async), 1,000 queries takes ~50 seconds. Assuming a 50/50 split of retriever vs prompt experiments, average cost plummets from $300 to $15 per run. Iteration time drops from 8 hours to under 10 minutes.

title: TPU MXU Padding and Memory Stalls
scenario: You migrated a two-tower retrieval model to a TPU v4 Pod. The model uses a custom dense architecture with an input dimension of 1000, hidden dimension of 500, and a serving batch size of 150. Throughput is capped at 12,000 QPS. Hardware profiling reveals severe memory stall times and an abysmal Matrix Multiply Unit (MXU) utilization of 14%, despite theoretical arithmetic intensity suggesting it should be compute-bound.
common_mistake: Assuming the model is memory-bandwidth bound and attempting to apply aggressive weight quantization or operator fusion without addressing the matrix shapes.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a severe hardware-compiler-algorithm mismatch. TPU v4 uses a 128x128 Systolic Array (MXU). The XLA compiler maps matrix multiplications to this hardware by padding dimensions to be multiples of 128 (or 8/128 depending on the specific axis and tiling). A batch size of 150 is padded to 256. An input dimension of 1000 is padded to 1024. A hidden dimension of 500 is padded to 512. The hardware is executing a 256x1024x512 matrix multiplication, loading padded zeros from High Bandwidth Memory (HBM). This destroys arithmetic intensity and wastes MXU cycles. The co-design fix involve
napkin_math: Actual compute needed: 150 * 1000 * 500 = 75M MACs. Padded compute executed: 256 * 1024 * 512 = 134.2M MACs. Wasted compute = 44%. Furthermore, memory reads for padded tensors increase bandwidth pressure.
